Ayahs

ٱقْتَرَبَ لِلنَّاسِ حِسَابُهُمْ وَهُمْ فِى غَفْلَةٍ مُّعْرِضُونَ (١)
Mankind’s reckoning has drawn near to them, yet they are disregardful in [their] obliviousness. (1)
مَا يَأْتِيهِم مِّن ذِكْرٍ مِّن رَّبِّهِم مُّحْدَثٍ إِلَّا ٱسْتَمَعُوهُ وَهُمْ يَلْعَبُونَ (٢)
There does not come to them any new reminder from their Lord but they listen to it as they play around, (2)
لَاهِيَةً قُلُوبُهُمْ ۗ وَأَسَرُّوا۟ ٱلنَّجْوَى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ هَلْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ ۖ أَفَتَأْتُونَ ٱلسِّحْرَ وَأَنتُمْ تُبْصِرُونَ (٣)
their hearts set on diversions. The wrongdoers secretly whisper together, [saying], ‘Is this [man] not a human being like yourselves? Will you give in to magic with open eyes?’ (3)
قَالَ رَبِّى يَعْلَمُ ٱلْقَوْلَ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٤)
He said, ‘My Lord knows every word [spoken] in the sky and on the earth, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing.’ (4)
بَلْ قَالُوٓا۟ أَضْغَـٰثُ أَحْلَـٰمٍۭ بَلِ ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ بَلْ هُوَ شَاعِرٌ فَلْيَأْتِنَا بِـَٔايَةٍ كَمَآ أُرْسِلَ ٱلْأَوَّلُونَ (٥)
But they said, ‘[They are] muddled dreams!’ ‘Indeed, he has fabricated it!’ ‘Indeed, he is a poet!’ ‘Let him bring us a sign, like those sent to the former generations.’ (5)

Virtue of Surah al-Anbiya

It has been narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah:

من قرء سورة الانبياء حاسبه اللَّه حسابا يسيرا، و صافحه و سلم عليه كل نبى ذكر اسمه فى القرآن:

"Whoever recites Surah al-Anbiya, Allah will grant him an easy reckoning (and will not be strict in auditing his deeds on the Day of Resurrection), and every prophet whose name is mentioned in the Quran will shake hands with him and send salutations upon him." [^1] And we read from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

من قرء سورة الانبياء حبا لها كان كمن رافق النبيين اجمعين فى جنات النعيم، و كان مهيبا فى اعين الناس حياة الدنيا:

"Whoever recites Surah al-Anbiya out of love and affection for it, shall be like one who accompanies all the prophets in the gardens of bliss in Paradise, and he shall be held in great awe in the eyes of people during his worldly life." [^2] The phrase "out of love and affection for it" (حبا لها) is, in fact, a key to understanding the meaning of the narrations that have reached us regarding the virtues of the Surahs of the Quran. That is, the goal is not merely the recitation and chanting of words, but rather a love for the content; and certainly, love for the content is meaningless without action. If someone claims to love a certain Surah while his actions contradict its concepts, he is lying.

We have said many times that the Quran is a book of belief and action, and recitation is a prelude to reflection, and reflection is a prelude to faith and action!

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 3, p. 412.

[^2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 3, p. 412.

Content of the Surah

  1. This Surah, as its name suggests, is the Surah of the Prophets, for the names of sixteen prophets—some with mentions of episodes from their lives and others merely by reference—appear in this Surah (Musa, Harun, Ibrahim, Lut, Ishaq, Ya’qub, Nuh, Dawud, Sulayman, Ayyub, Isma’il, Idris, Dhul-Kifl, Dhun-Nun (Yunus), Zakariyya, and Yahya عليهم السلام).

Therefore, the emphasis of the important discussions in this Surah is on the missions and programs of the prophets.

In addition to these, there are other prophets whose names are not explicitly mentioned in this Surah, but who are discussed (such as the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and Prophet Jesus).

  1. Furthermore, the characteristics of "Meccan" Surahs, which speak of religious beliefs, especially the Origin (Mabda') and the Resurrection (Ma'ad), are fully reflected in this Surah.

  2. This Surah discusses the unity of the Creator and that there is no deity or creator besides Him, as well as the creation of the world based on purpose and plan, the unity of the laws governing this universe, the unity of the source of life and existence, and the unity of all beings in the program of annihilation and death.

  3. Another part of this Surah speaks of the victory of truth over falsehood, monotheism over polytheism, and the armies of justice over the legions of Iblis.

  4. Interestingly, this Surah begins with severe warnings to the heedless people who are unaware of the Reckoning, and its conclusion is complemented by further warnings in this regard.

The prophets whose names appear in this Surah sometimes have their lives and programs detailed in other Surahs, but in this Surah, the focus is primarily on those moments when they were caught in severe straits—how they reached out to the hem of Divine grace and how Allah opened these dead-ends for them, granting them salvation from storms and whirlpools.

"Ibrahim" when he was trapped in the fire of Nimrod.

"Yunus" when he sank into the belly of the fish.

"Zakariyya" when he saw the sun of his life nearing its setting yet had no successor to complete his programs.

And likewise, other prophets during their entrapment in fierce storms.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

اقْتَرَبَ لِلنَّاسِ حِسابُهُمْ وَ هُمْ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مُعْرِضُونَ (1) ما يَأْتِيهِمْ مِنْ ذِكْرٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ مُحْدَثٍ إِلاَّ اسْتَمَعُوهُ وَ هُمْ يَلْعَبُونَ (2) لاهِيَةً قُلُوبُهُمْ وَ أَسَرُّوا النَّجْوَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا هَلْ هذا إِلاَّ بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ أَ فَتَأْتُونَ السِّحْرَ وَ أَنْتُمْ تُبْصِرُونَ (3) قالَ رَبِّي يَعْلَمُ الْقَوْلَ فِي السَّماءِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ (4)

بَلْ قالُوا أَضْغاثُ أَحْلامٍ بَلِ افْتَراهُ بَلْ هُوَ شاعِرٌ فَلْيَأْتِنا بِآيَةٍ كَما أُرْسِلَ الْأَوَّلُونَ (5)

Commentary

Various Excuses

This Surah—as we have noted—begins with a powerful warning to all people, a jolting and awakening warning. It says: "The people's reckoning has drawn near to them, while they are in heedlessness, turning away" (اقْتَرَبَ لِلنَّاسِ حِسابُهُمْ وَ هُمْ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مُعْرِضُونَ‌).

Their actions show that this heedlessness and unawareness has permeated their entire being; otherwise, how is it possible for a person to believe in the proximity of the Reckoning—and that too by an exceedingly meticulous Accountant—and yet take all matters so lightly and be tainted by every kind of sin?

The word "iqtaraba" (اقْتَرَبَ) carries more emphasis than "qurba" (قرب), indicating that this reckoning has become very close.

The expression "al-nas" (the people), although it apparently includes all people and suggests that everyone is in heedlessness, undoubtedly has exceptions whenever the masses are spoken of. Here, the group of awakened hearts who are always thinking of the Reckoning and preparing for it must be excluded from this judgment.

It is interesting that it says the reckoning has approached the people, not the people have approached the reckoning—as if the Reckoning is running swiftly to meet the people! Furthermore, the difference between "heedlessness" (ghaflah) and "turning away" (i'rad) might be from the perspective that:

They are heedless of the proximity of the Reckoning, and this heedlessness causes them to turn away from the signs of Truth. In reality, "heedlessness of the Reckoning" is the cause, and "turning away from the signs of Truth" is its effect. Alternatively, it may mean turning away from the Reckoning itself and the readiness to answer in that great court; that is, because they are heedless, they do not prepare themselves and they turn away.

Here, the question arises: what is the meaning of the Reckoning and the Resurrection drawing near?

Some have said: It means that the remainder of the world compared to what has passed is little, and for this reason, the Resurrection will be near (relatively near). Especially since it is narrated from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that he said:

بعثت انا و الساعة كهاتين!

"I was sent, and the Hour is like these two!" (He said this while pointing to his index and middle fingers held together). [^1] Others have said: This expression is due to the certainty of the Resurrection, just as we read in the famous Arabic proverb: "Everything that is coming is near" (كل ما هو آت قريب).

At the same time, these two interpretations are not contradictory, and the verse may refer to both points.

Some commentators, such as al-Qurtubi, have also suggested the possibility that "Reckoning" here refers to the "Minor Resurrection," which is death, because at the time of death, a portion of the accounting and recompense for deeds reaches man. [^2] However, the verse apparently refers to the Major Resurrection.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses in question.

[^2]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, vol. 6, p. 4307.

The next verse describes one of the signs of their turning away as follows: "No fresh reminder comes to them from their Lord but they listen to it while they play and sport!" (ما يَأْتِيهِمْ مِنْ ذِكْرٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ مُحْدَثٍ إِلَّا اسْتَمَعُوهُ وَ هُمْ يَلْعَبُونَ‌).

It never happens that they encounter a Surah or a verse—in short, an awakening word from the Lord—with seriousness, reflecting upon it for an hour or at least considering the possibility that this word affects their life and destiny. They think neither of the Divine Reckoning nor of the Lord's warnings.

Fundamentally, one of the misfortunes of ignorant, arrogant, and selfish individuals is that they always take the advice and counsel of well-wishers as a joke and a game. This is why they never wake up from the sleep of heedlessness, whereas if they were to encounter it seriously even once, their life path might change at that very moment.

The word "dhikr" (reminder) in the above verse refers to any awakening word, and the expression "muhdath" (fresh/new) indicates that the Heavenly Books are revealed one after another, and the Surahs and verses of the Quran each contain fresh and new content, entering through various ways to penetrate the hearts of the heedless. But what benefit is there for those who take all of this as a joke?

In principle, it seems they are terrified of new things; they are content with the same old superstitions inherited from their ancestors, as if they have made an eternal pact to oppose every new truth, whereas the basis of the law of evolution is that it confronts man with fresh and new issues every day.

Again, for further emphasis, it says: "While their hearts are preoccupied with trifles and heedlessness" (لاهِيَةً قُلُوبُهُمْ‌).

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 13; p. 355

Externally, they consider all serious matters as play and sport (as indicated by the present continuous and absolute form of the verb "yal'abun"), and internally, they are caught in "lahw" (diversion) and the preoccupation of the mind with worthless and distracting matters.

Naturally, such people will never find the path of salvation.

Then, referring to a corner of their satanic plots, He says: "These wrongdoers conceal their private conversations which they hold for plotting, saying: 'Is this [man] anything but a human being like yourselves?'" (وَ أَسَرُّوا النَّجْوَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا هَلْ هذا إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ‌) [^1] Now that he is nothing more than an ordinary human, surely these extraordinary works of his and the influence of his words can be nothing but sorcery. "Will you then succumb to sorcery while you see [it]?" (أَ فَتَأْتُونَ السِّحْرَ وَ أَنْتُمْ تُبْصِرُونَ‌).

We said this Surah was revealed in "Mecca," and in those days the enemies of Islam were quite powerful; so what was the necessity for them to hide their words, even their private whispers? (Note that the Quran says they were concealing their whispered conversations).

This might be because they consulted each other on matters involving plots and schemes so that they could stand against the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله before the masses with a unified plan.

[^1]: In Arabic literature, it is customary that if the subject is an explicit noun, the verb is brought in the singular; however, this is not a universal and permanent rule. Sometimes, for specific reasons, the verb is brought in the plural while the subject is an explicit noun; the phrase "وَ أَسَرُّوا النَّجْوَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا" is of this type.

Furthermore, they were certainly ahead in terms of power and force, but in terms of logic and the power of the word's influence, the superiority lay with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Muslims. This very superiority caused them to sit in secret consultations to choose labels and fabricated responses against the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

In any case, in their speech, they relied on two things: one, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله being a human, and the other, the label of sorcery. In the following verses, other labels will also come, which the Quran proceeds to answer.

But the Quran first gives them a general response through the tongue of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "My Lord knows every word [spoken] in the heaven and the earth" (قالَ رَبِّي يَعْلَمُ الْقَوْلَ فِي السَّماءِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌). Do not imagine that your secret words and hidden plots are concealed from Him, "for He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing" (وَ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ‌).

He knows everything and is aware of every deed; He not only hears the words but is aware of the thoughts that pass through the brains and the decisions hidden in the chests.

After mentioning two parts of the opponents' excuses, it addresses four other parts, saying: "They said: 'What the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله has brought as revelation is nothing but muddled dreams' which he imagines to be truth and reality!" (بَلْ قالُوا أَضْغاثُ أَحْلامٍ‌) [^1]

[^1]: "Adghath" is the plural of "dighth," meaning a bundle of firewood or dried grass and the like, and "ahlam" is the plural of "hulm," meaning a dream or vision. Since one puts together a handful of scattered items to collect a bundle of firewood, this expression is applied to muddled and scattered dreams.

And sometimes they change their words and say: "He is a liar who has fabricated these words against Allah!" (بَلِ افْتَراهُ‌).

And sometimes they say: "No, he is a poet," and these verses are a collection of his poetic imaginations (بَلْ هُوَ شاعِرٌ).

And in the final stage, they say: Setting all this aside, "If he is telling the truth that he is a messenger of Allah, he must bring us a miracle just as the former prophets were sent with miracles" (فَلْيَأْتِنا بِآيَةٍ كَما أُرْسِلَ الْأَوَّلُونَ‌).

The examination of these contradictory attributions to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is itself the best proof that they were not seekers of truth; rather, their goal was excuse-making and, so to speak, driving the opponent out of the field at any price and in any way.

Sometimes they called him a sorcerer, sometimes a poet, sometimes a fabricator, and sometimes (God forbid) a visionary who considered his disturbed dreams as revelation! Sometimes they say, "Why are you a human?" and sometimes, despite seeing all those miracles, they still demand another miracle.

If we had no proof of the falsehood of their words other than this scattered talk, it would be sufficient alone; but in the following verses, we will see that the Quran gives them a decisive answer through other ways as well.

Key Point: Is the Quran Created (Hadith)?

A group of commentators, under these verses and in relation to the word "muhdath" (fresh/new) in the second verse discussed, have raised extensive discussions regarding whether the "Word of Allah" is created (hadith) or eternal (qadim). This is the very issue that was a subject of intense debate for many years during the time of the Abbasid caliphs and occupied the minds of a group of scholars for a long time.

However, today we know well that this discussion was mostly a political diversion to keep Islamic scholars occupied and distract them from fundamental and essential issues concerning the state of governance, the people's way of life, and the core truths of Islam.

Today it is perfectly clear to us that if by "Word of Allah" we mean its content and meaning, it is certainly eternal; that is, it has always been in the knowledge of Allah, and the vast knowledge of the Lord has always encompassed it.

And if by it we mean these phonemes, these words, and this revelation that was sent down to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, then that is undoubtedly "hadith" (created/originated).

What rational person says that phonemes and words are eternal? Or that the descent of revelation upon the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was not from the beginning of his mission? Therefore, you see that whichever side of the discussion we take, the matter is as clear as daylight.

In other words, the Quran has words and meanings; its words are certainly created, and its meanings are certainly eternal. Thus, there is no room for dispute.

Furthermore, what scientific, social, political, or moral problem of the Islamic society does this discussion solve? And why were some past scholars deceived by the tricks of cunning, plotting rulers?! Thus, we see that some of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, while clearly explaining this issue, practically warned them to avoid such discussions. [^1]

[^1]: Al-Ihtijaj by al-Tabarsi, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 3, p. 412.

Ayahs

مَآ ءَامَنَتْ قَبْلَهُم مِّن قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهَآ ۖ أَفَهُمْ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٦)
No town that We destroyed before them believed. Will these then have faith [if they are sent signs]? (6)
وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا قَبْلَكَ إِلَّا رِجَالًا نُّوحِىٓ إِلَيْهِمْ ۖ فَسْـَٔلُوٓا۟ أَهْلَ ٱلذِّكْرِ إِن كُنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٧)
We did not send [any apostles] before you except as men to whom We revealed. Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know. (7)
وَمَا جَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ جَسَدًا لَّا يَأْكُلُونَ ٱلطَّعَامَ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ خَـٰلِدِينَ (٨)
We did not make them bodies that did not eat food, nor were they immortal. (8)
ثُمَّ صَدَقْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْوَعْدَ فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُمْ وَمَن نَّشَآءُ وَأَهْلَكْنَا ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ (٩)
Then We fulfilled Our promise to them, and We delivered them and whomever We wished, and We destroyed the transgressors. (9)
لَقَدْ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكُمْ كِتَـٰبًا فِيهِ ذِكْرُكُمْ ۖ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (١٠)
Certainly We have sent down to you a Book in which there is an admonition for you. Do you not exercise your reason? (10)

Commentary

All Prophets Were of the Human Species

We mentioned that in the preceding verses, six examples of the contradictory objections raised by the enemies of Islam against the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) were recounted. The verses under discussion address these objections, sometimes in a general and collective manner and sometimes by responding to specific points.

The first verse under discussion refers to their "extemporaneous miracles" (by which is meant the proposing of arbitrary miracles as a form of excuse-making) and states: "Not a single city among those We destroyed before them believed [after such miracles were shown]; will they then believe?" (ما آمَنَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْناها أَ فَهُمْ يُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

Simultaneously, it warns them that if your request regarding extemporaneous miracles is granted and you still do not believe, your destruction is certain! There is also the possibility in the interpretation of this verse that the Quran is referring to all their contradictory objections, stating: This manner of dealing with the call of the true Prophets is not new; stubborn individuals have always resorted to such excuses, and the end of their affair was nothing but disbelief followed by destruction and painful Divine punishment.

The next verse specifically addresses their first objection regarding the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) being a human being, stating: You are not the only one who is a Prophet and yet a human; "And We sent not before you except men to whom We revealed" (وَ ما أَرْسَلْنا قَبْلَكَ إِلَّا رِجالًا نُوحِي إِلَيْهِمْ‌).

This is a historical reality of which everyone is aware, "so ask the people of the message (Ahl al-Dhikr) if you do not know" (فَسْئَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ‌).

Who are the "Ahl al-Dhikr"?

Undoubtedly, from a literal perspective, "Ahl al-Dhikr" encompasses all those who are knowledgeable and informed. The above verse expresses a general rational principle regarding "the referral of the ignorant to the learned," even though the specific instance and context of the verse were the scholars of the People of the Book; however, this does not prevent the universality of the rule.

For this reason, Muslim scholars and jurists have used this verse as evidence for the issue of the "permissibility of following (taqlid) Islamic jurists (mujtahids)."

And if we see in narrations reached to us through the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) that "Ahl al-Dhikr" is interpreted as Ali (عليه السلام) or the other Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), it is not meant to be exclusive, but rather to identify the clearest manifestations of this general law.

For further explanation on this matter, please refer to the commentary on verse 43 of Surah An-Nahl (Volume 11, pages 240 to 246).

The next verse provides more explanation regarding the human nature of the Prophets and says:

"And We did not make them [the Prophets] bodies that did not eat food, nor were they immortal" (وَ ما جَعَلْناهُمْ جَسَداً لا يَأْكُلُونَ الطَّعامَ وَ ما كانُوا خالِدِينَ‌).

The phrase "they did not eat food" refers to something mentioned elsewhere in the Quran in connection with this same objection: "And they say, 'What is this messenger that eats food and walks in the markets?'" (Furqan: 7).

The phrase "nor were they immortal" is a completion of this same meaning, because the polytheists used to say: It would have been better if an angel had been sent instead of a human—an angel who would have eternal life and whom the hand of death could not reach! The Quran replies to them: None of the previous Prophets had eternal life, so why should the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) have it?

In any case, undoubtedly—as we have said many times—the leader of humans must be of their own kind, with the same instincts, emotions, feelings, needs, and interests, so that he may feel their pains and choose the best method of treatment by drawing inspiration from his teachings, so that he may be a model and an example for all humans and leave no excuse for anyone.

Then, as a threat and a warning to the persistent and stubborn deniers, it says: We had promised Our Prophets that We would deliver them from the clutches of the enemies and frustrate their plans; yes, "Then We fulfilled the promise to them and saved them and whomever We willed, and We destroyed the transgressors" (ثُمَّ صَدَقْناهُمُ الْوَعْدَ فَأَنْجَيْناهُمْ وَ مَنْ نَشاءُ وَ أَهْلَكْنَا الْمُسْرِفِينَ‌).

Indeed, just as it was Our Sunnah (tradition) to choose human leaders from among human beings, it was also Our Sunnah to support them against the conspiracies of the opponents, and if repeated advice did not affect them, to cleanse the face of the earth of their defiled existence.

It is clear that the meaning of "whomever We willed" (وَ مَنْ نَشاءُ) is a willing that revolves around the criteria of faith and righteous deeds. It is also clear that the "transgressors" (musrifin) here refers to those who committed excess against themselves and the society in which they lived, by denying Divine signs and rejecting the Prophets.

Therefore, we read elsewhere in the Quran: "Thus, it is a right upon Us that We save the believers" (Yunus: 103).

In the final verse under discussion, in a short and meaningful sentence, it responds again to most of the polytheists' objections, saying: "We have certainly sent down to you a Book in which is your mention (reminder). Will you not then reason?" (لَقَدْ أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكُمْ كِتاباً فِيهِ ذِكْرُكُمْ أَ فَلا تَعْقِلُونَ‌).

Anyone who examines the verses of this Book—which is a source of reminder, the awakening of the heart, the movement of thought, and the purity of societies—will know well that it is a clear and eternal miracle. Despite this manifest miracle, whose miraculous aspects are evident from various perspectives (extraordinary attraction, content, laws and regulations, beliefs and knowledge, etc.), do you still wait for the appearance of another miracle? What miracle could better prove the truthfulness of the call of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) than this?! Furthermore, the verses of this Book cry out that it is not magic, but reality and profound, attractive teachings; do you still say it is magic? Can the label "muddled dreams" (أَضْغاثُ أَحْلامٍ) be applied to these verses? Where are confused and meaningless dreams, and where are these harmonious and coherent words?

Can it be considered a lie and a fabrication, while the signs of truthfulness are evident throughout it?

And was its bringer a "poet," while poetry revolves around imagination and the verses of this Book are all based on realities?! In short, careful study and examination of this Book prove that these contradictory attributions are mismatched patches and malicious, irrational words.

Regarding the meaning of the word "dhikrukum" (your mention/reminder) in the above verse, commentators have various explanations:

Some have said: It means that the verses of the Quran are a source of reminder and the awakening of your thoughts, as it says elsewhere: "So remind by the Quran whoever fears My threat" (Qaf: 45).

A group has said: It means that this Quran will raise your name and fame in the world; that is, it is a source of glory and honor for you—you believers and Muslims, or you the Arab people, since the Quran was revealed in your language, and if it were taken from you, you would have no name or trace in the world.

Others have said: It means that in this Quran, whatever you need in matters of religion and the world, or in the field of noble ethics, has been mentioned.

Although these interpretations are not inconsistent with one another and all may be encompassed in the expression "dhikrukum," the first interpretation seems clearer.

And if it is asked: How is this Quran a source of awakening while many polytheists heard it and were not awakened? We reply: The awakening nature of the Quran is not compulsory or forced; rather, it is conditional upon the person himself wanting it and opening the windows of his heart to it.

Ayahs

وَكَمْ قَصَمْنَا مِن قَرْيَةٍ كَانَتْ ظَالِمَةً وَأَنشَأْنَا بَعْدَهَا قَوْمًا ءَاخَرِينَ (١١)
How many a town We have smashed that had been wrongdoing, and We brought forth another people after it. (11)
فَلَمَّآ أَحَسُّوا۟ بَأْسَنَآ إِذَا هُم مِّنْهَا يَرْكُضُونَ (١٢)
So when they sighted Our punishment, behold, they ran away from it. (12)
لَا تَرْكُضُوا۟ وَٱرْجِعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ مَآ أُتْرِفْتُمْ فِيهِ وَمَسَـٰكِنِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُسْـَٔلُونَ (١٣)
‘Do not run away! Return to the opulence you were given to enjoy and to your dwellings so that you may be questioned!’ (13)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰوَيْلَنَآ إِنَّا كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ (١٤)
They said, ‘Woe to us! We have indeed been wrongdoers!’ (14)
فَمَا زَالَت تِّلْكَ دَعْوَىٰهُمْ حَتَّىٰ جَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ حَصِيدًا خَـٰمِدِينَ (١٥)
That remained their cry until We turned them into a mown field, stilled [like burnt ashes]. (15)

Commentary

How the Oppressors Were Caught in the Grip of Torment

Following the discussions regarding the polytheists and the stubborn disbelievers, the verses under discussion determine their fate by comparing it with the fate of previous nations.

First, it says: "How many a town that was acting wrongfully have We shattered" (وَ كَمْ قَصَمْنا مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ كانَتْ ظالِمَةً).

"And after them, We brought forth another people and population into existence and into the field of trial" (وَ أَنْشَأْنا بَعْدَها قَوْماً آخَرِينَ).

Considering that "qasm" (قصم) means breaking with intensity and is sometimes even used to mean crushing, and given that the emphasis is placed on the "wrongdoing" and "oppression" of these nations, it indicates that Allah exacts the severest vengeance and punishment upon oppressive peoples.

Furthermore, it points out that if you examine the history of those who came before, you will realize that the threats of the Prophet of Islam are not a joke or without calculation; they are a bitter reality that you must think about deeply.

Then, it describes their state when the scope of the torment spread across their settlements, and it highlights their state of helplessness in the face of Divine punishment, saying: "When they sensed Our punishment was about to overtake them,

they took to their heels and fled" (فَلَمَّا أَحَسُّوا بَأْسَنا إِذا هُمْ مِنْها يَرْكُضُونَ) [1]. Just like a defeated army that sees the naked swords of the enemy behind them, they scatter in every direction.

However, as a rebuke and reproach, they are told: "Do not flee, and return to the life of luxury and ease you enjoyed! And to your palaces, castles, and dwellings adorned with gold and ornaments, so that perhaps seekers may come and make requests of you" (لا تَرْكُضُوا وَ ارْجِعُوا إِلى‌ ما أُتْرِفْتُمْ فِيهِ وَ مَساكِنِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُسْئَلُونَ). This expression may be an allusion to the fact that in the life of luxury they led, seekers and petitioners were constantly coming and going at their doors, arriving with hope and returning deprived. It tells them: Return and repeat those same loathsome scenes; this is, in truth, a form of mockery and reproach.

Some commentators have also suggested that the phrase "لَعَلَّكُمْ تُسْئَلُونَ" (so that you may be questioned/asked) refers to their pompous and arrogant establishment where they would sit personally in a corner and constantly issue orders, while servants would come to them in succession to ask for their commands and instructions.

As for who the speaker of these words is, it is not explicitly stated in the verse.

It could be a call by Allah's angels or His prophets and messengers.

[1]: "Rakd" (ركض) means both running with speed and galloping a mount; sometimes it also means striking the foot upon the ground, as in: "ارْكُضْ بِرِجْلِكَ هذا مُغْتَسَلٌ بارِدٌ وَ شَرابٌ": "O Job! Strike the ground with your foot; here is a spring for washing, cool and refreshing, and a drink" (Surah Sad, 42).

Or it could be from within their hidden conscience and their soul.

In reality, this was a Divine call addressed to them: "Do not flee and return," which reached them through one of these three ways.

It is interesting that here, among all material blessings, "dwelling" (maskan) is specifically pointed out. This is perhaps because the primary means of human comfort is the existence of a suitable dwelling, or because a person often spends the largest portion of their life's income on their home and has the greatest attachment to it.

In any case, at this moment they wake up; what they previously thought was a joke, they now see before them in the most serious form, and their cry rises: "They said: Woe to us! Indeed, we were wrongdoers!" (قالُوا يا وَيْلَنا إِنَّا كُنَّا ظالِمِينَ).

However, this forced awakening, which occurs for everyone in the face of the serious scenes of torment, is worthless and has no effect on changing their fate. Therefore, the Quran adds in the final verse under discussion: "And that cry of theirs (Woe to us, we were wrongdoers) continued until We mowed them down and extinguished them" (فَما زالَتْ تِلْكَ دَعْواهُمْ حَتَّى جَعَلْناهُمْ حَصِيداً خامِدِينَ). Like a harvested crop (hasid), they fall to the ground, and their bustling and prosperous city turns into a ruined and silent graveyard (khamidin) [1].

[1]: "Khamid" (خامد) is originally derived from the root "khumud" (on the pattern of junud), meaning the extinguishing of fire. Subsequently, it has been applied to anything that loses its bustle, noise, and commotion.

Ayahs

وَمَا خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَآءَ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا لَـٰعِبِينَ (١٦)
We did not create the sky and the earth and whatever is between them for play. (16)
لَوْ أَرَدْنَآ أَن نَّتَّخِذَ لَهْوًا لَّٱتَّخَذْنَـٰهُ مِن لَّدُنَّآ إِن كُنَّا فَـٰعِلِينَ (١٧)
Had We desired to take up some diversion We would have taken it up with Ourselves, were We to do [so]. (17)
بَلْ نَقْذِفُ بِٱلْحَقِّ عَلَى ٱلْبَـٰطِلِ فَيَدْمَغُهُۥ فَإِذَا هُوَ زَاهِقٌ ۚ وَلَكُمُ ٱلْوَيْلُ مِمَّا تَصِفُونَ (١٨)
Indeed, We hurl the truth against falsehood, and it crushes its head, and behold, falsehood vanishes! And woe to you for what you allege [about Allah]. (18)

Commentary

The Creation of the Heavens and the Earth is Not a Plaything

Since the preceding verses reflected the reality that the faithless oppressors recognized no purpose for their creation other than indulgence and pleasure—effectively perceiving the world as aimless—the Holy Quran, in the verses under discussion, seeks to nullify this way of thinking and prove the existence of a valuable purpose for the creation of the entire universe, especially human beings, by stating:

"We did not create the heaven and the earth and that which is between them in play" (وَ ما خَلَقْنَا السَّماءَ وَ الْأَرْضَ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما لاعِبِينَ).

This vast earth, this expansive sky, and all these diverse and wondrous beings within them indicate that a significant purpose was at work. Indeed, the goal was, on one hand, to manifest that Great Creator and serve as a sign of His greatness, and on the other hand, to serve as a proof for the "Resurrection"; otherwise, all this commotion for these few days would be meaningless.

Is it possible for a person to build a fully equipped palace with all amenities in the middle of a desert, solely because he might pass by there once in his lifetime for an hour to rest?

In short: if we look at this magnificent world through the eyes of faithless people, it is hollow and aimless; it is only faith in the "Origin" and the "Resurrection" that makes it purposeful.

The next verse says: Now that it is established that the world is not aimless, it is also certain that the goal of this creation was not God's amusement with the act of creation, as such amusement is irrational: "Had We wished to take a pastime, We would have taken it from Our presence, if We were to do [so]" (لَوْ أَرَدْنا أَنْ نَتَّخِذَ لَهْواً لَاتَّخَذْناهُ مِنْ لَدُنَّا إِنْ كُنَّا فاعِلِينَ). In reality, la‘ib (play) refers to an aimless action, and lahw (pastime/amusement) refers to irrational goals or diversions.

The verse under discussion recounts two truths: First, by using the word law (if), which in Arabic grammar denotes impossibility, it indicates that it is impossible for the Lord's goal to be His own amusement.

Then it says: Assuming the goal were amusement, it would have to be an amusement suitable for His Essence, from the realm of abstract beings and the like, not the limited world of matter. [^1]

Then, with a decisive tone to nullify the delusions of the mindless who perceive the world as aimless or merely a source of amusement, it says: This world is a collection of truth and reality; it is not that its foundation is based on falsehood. "Nay, We hurl the truth against falsehood, so it knocks out its brains, and behold, it vanishes" (بَلْ نَقْذِفُ بِالْحَقِّ عَلَى الْباطِلِ فَيَدْمَغُهُ فَإِذا هُوَ زاهِقٌ). And at the end of the verse, it says: "And woe to you for what you describe" (وَ لَكُمُ الْوَيْلُ مِمَّا تَصِفُونَ).

That is to say, We constantly place Our intellectual proofs, clear arguments, and manifest miracles against the whims and delusions of the proponents of futility, so that in the eyes of thinkers and the wise, these delusions are crushed and destroyed.

The proofs for recognizing God are clear, the evidences for the existence of the Resurrection are manifest, the arguments for the truthfulness of the Prophets are obvious, and in truth, for those who are not stubborn and excuse-seeking, truth is completely distinguishable from falsehood.

It is noteworthy that the word naqdhif is from the root qadhf, meaning to throw, especially to throw from a distance. Since throwing from a distance possesses more momentum, speed, and force, this expression illustrates the power of the victory of truth over falsehood. The word ‘ala (upon/against) also supports this meaning, as this word is typically used in contexts of "superiority."

The phrase yadamghuhu, which according to Raghib means breaking the skull and the brain—considered the most sensitive part of the human body—is an eloquent expression for the dominance of the army of truth, a striking and decisive victory.

The use of idha (behold/suddenly) shows that even where it is not expected for truth to prevail, We carry out this plan.

The use of zahiq, which means something that has completely vanished or disintegrated, is another emphasis on this objective.

The fact that the words naqdhif and yadamghu appear in the present/future tense (mudari‘) is evidence of the continuity of this program.

Key Point: The Purpose of Creation

While materialists recognize no purpose for creation—since they consider the origin of creation to be nature, which lacks intellect, consciousness, and purpose, and for this reason, they advocate for nihilism in the totality of existence—divine philosophers and followers of religions all believe in the existence of a sublime goal for creation, because it is impossible for the Powerful and Wise Origin of the world to perform an aimless act.

Now the question arises: what is this goal?

Sometimes, due to comparing Allah to ourselves, we fall into the delusion: did God have a deficiency that He wanted to compensate for by creating existence, including man?!

Does He need our worship and prayers? Did He want to be known and thus created the creation so that He might be recognized?! But as we have said, this is a great mistake resulting from the comparison of "God" and "creation," whereas in the discussion of knowing the attributes of God, the greatest barrier is this very incorrect comparison (therefore, the primary principle in this discussion is that we know He resembles us in nothing).

We are beings limited in every respect, and for this reason, all our efforts are to remove our deficiencies. We study to become literate and eliminate our lack of knowledge; we pursue business to fight poverty and need; we prepare armies and forces to compensate for our lack of power against an opponent. Even in spiritual matters, self-purification, and spiritual stations, the efforts are still directed toward removing deficiencies.

But is it rational for that Being who is infinite in every respect—Whose knowledge, power, and forces are endless, and Who has no deficiency in any regard—to perform an action to remove a deficiency?

From this clear analysis, we reach the conclusion that, on one hand, creation is not aimless, and on the other hand, this goal does not return to the Creator.

Here, one can easily conclude that the goal is certainly and without doubt something related to ourselves.

With this introduction, it can be understood that the goal of creation is nothing but our perfection and transcendence. In other words, the universe is a university for our perfection in the field of knowledge.

It is a nursery from an educational perspective for the purification of our souls.

It is a marketplace for earning spiritual gains.

It is a land of fruitful cultivation for nurturing various types of human products.

Yes, "The world is the farm of the Hereafter... The world is a house of truth for him who believes it, a house of wealth for him who takes provision from it, and a house of admonition for him who takes heed from it." [^2] This caravan has set out from the realm of non-existence and is constantly advancing toward infinity.

The Holy Quran, with short and very meaningful allusions in various verses, points on one hand to the principle of the existence of a goal in creation, and on the other hand, specifies this goal.

In the first part, it says: "Does man think that he will be left aimless?" (أَ يَحْسَبُ الْإِنْسانُ أَنْ يُتْرَكَ سُدىً) (Al-Qiyamah: 36). "Did you then think that We created you in vain and that you would not be returned to Us?" (أَ فَحَسِبْتُمْ أَنَّما خَلَقْناکُمْ عَبَثاً وَ أَنَّکُمْ إِلَيْنا لا تُرْجَعُونَ) (Al-Mu'minun: 115).

"And We did not create the heaven and the earth and that which is between them in vain. That is the opinion of those who disbelieve" (وَ ما خَلَقْنَا السَّماءَ وَ الْأَرْضَ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما باطِلًا ذلِکَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ کَفَرُوا) (Sad: 27).

In the second part, sometimes in the verses of the Quran, the goal of creation is set as servitude and worship of God: "I did not create the jinn and mankind except to worship Me" (وَ ما خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَ الْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ) (Adh-Dhariyat: 56). It is obvious that worship is a school for nurturing man in various dimensions. Worship in the broad sense of the word—which is submission to the command of God—perfects the human soul and spirit in various fields, the explanation of which we have provided under the verses related to various acts of worship.

[^1]: A group of commentators have considered the above verses as an allusion to the negation of Christian beliefs; that is, they have taken lahw to mean a wife and child, saying the verse responds to them by stating that if We wanted to choose a child and a wife, We would not have chosen them from the human race. However, this interpretation does not seem appropriate for several reasons, including that the connection of the above verses with the preceding ones would be severed, and furthermore, the word lahw, especially when it follows the word la‘ib, means amusement, not wife and child.

[^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 131.

And sometimes it says: The goal of creation is your awareness, awakening, and the strengthening of your faith and belief: "Allah is He who created seven heavens and of the earth the like of them. The command descends between them so that you may know that Allah has power over all things" (اللَّهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ وَ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ مِثْلَهُنَّ يَتَنَزَّلُ الْأَمْرُ بَيْنَهُنَّ لِتَعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ) (At-Talaq: 12).

And sometimes it says: The goal of creation is the testing of your good conduct: "He who created death and life that He may test you [to see] which of you is best in conduct" (الَّذِي خَلَقُ الْمَوْتَ وَ الْحَياةَ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا) (Al-Mulk: 2).

The three aforementioned verses, each of which points to one of the three dimensions of human existence (the dimension of awareness and faith, the dimension of ethics, and the dimension of action), illustrate the evolutionary goal of creation which returns to the human beings themselves.

It is also necessary to mention that some imagine that because the word "evolution" (takamul) is not mentioned in these discussions in the Quranic verses, perhaps this is an imported idea? But the answer to this objection is clear, for we are not bound by specific words; the concept and instances of evolution are clearly evident in the above verses. Is not knowledge and awareness a clear instance of it, as well as progress in servitude and good conduct?! In verse 17 of Surah Muhammad, we read: "And those who are guided, He increases them in guidance" (وَ الَّذِينَ اهْتَدَوْا زادَهُمْ هُدىً). Is the expression "increase" anything other than evolution?

Here a question arises: if the goal was evolution, why did God not create man perfect in all dimensions from the beginning so that there would be no need to traverse the stages of evolution?

The source of this objection is the neglect of the point that the main branch of evolution is "voluntary evolution." In other words, evolution is that man traverses the path with his own feet, will, and decision. If they take his hand and lead him by force, it is neither an honor nor evolution. For example, if a person spends one rial of his wealth by his own decision and will, he has traversed the path of moral perfection to that extent; whereas if millions of his wealth are taken and spent by force, he has not moved even one step in this path. Therefore, in various verses of the Holy Quran, it is explicitly stated that if God willed, all people would have believed by compulsion, but this faith would have been of no benefit to them: "And had your Lord willed, those on earth would have believed, all of them together" (وَ لَوْ شاءَ رَبُّكَ لَآمَنَ مَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ كُلُّهُمْ جَمِيعاً) (Yunus: 99).

Ayahs

وَلَهُۥ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَمَنْ عِندَهُۥ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِۦ وَلَا يَسْتَحْسِرُونَ (١٩)
To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth, and those who are near Him do not disdain to worship Him, nor do they become weary. (19)
يُسَبِّحُونَ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ لَا يَفْتُرُونَ (٢٠)
They glorify [Him] night and day, and they do not flag. (20)
أَمِ ٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ ءَالِهَةً مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ هُمْ يُنشِرُونَ (٢١)
Have they taken gods from the earth who raise [the dead]? (21)
لَوْ كَانَ فِيهِمَآ ءَالِهَةٌ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ لَفَسَدَتَا ۚ فَسُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَرْشِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ (٢٢)
Had there been any gods in them other than Allah, they would surely have fallen apart. Clear is Allah, the Lord of the Throne, of what they allege [concerning Him]. (22)
لَا يُسْـَٔلُ عَمَّا يَفْعَلُ وَهُمْ يُسْـَٔلُونَ (٢٣)
He is not questioned concerning what He does, but they are questioned. (23)
أَمِ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ ءَالِهَةً ۖ قُلْ هَاتُوا۟ بُرْهَـٰنَكُمْ ۖ هَـٰذَا ذِكْرُ مَن مَّعِىَ وَذِكْرُ مَن قَبْلِى ۗ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ ٱلْحَقَّ ۖ فَهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ (٢٤)
Have they taken gods besides Him? Say, ‘Produce your evidence! This is a precept of those who are with me, and a precept of those [who went] before me.’ But most of them do not know the truth, and so they are disregardful. (24)
وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا نُوحِىٓ إِلَيْهِ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنَا۠ فَٱعْبُدُونِ (٢٥)
We did not send any apostle before you but that We revealed to him that ‘There is no god except Me; so worship Me.’ (25)

Commentary

Polytheism Originates from Illusion

In the preceding verses, the discussion centered on the reality that the universe is not aimless; it is neither a joke nor a plaything, nor is it mere diversion and entertainment. Rather, it possesses a calculated evolutionary goal for human beings.

Since the misconception might arise—what need does Allah have for our faith and worship?—the verses under discussion first address this point, stating:

"To Him belongs whoever is in the heavens and the earth" (وَلَهُ مَنْ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ).

"And those who are near Him (the angels) are never too proud to worship Him, nor do they ever weary" (وَمَنْ عِنْدَهُ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِ وَلَا يَسْتَحْسِرُونَ) [1].

"They exalt Him night and day and do not flag [in their devotion]" (يُسَبِّحُونَ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ لا يَفْتُرُونَ).

Given this, what need does He have for your obedience and worship? All these great angels are busy exalting Him night and day; He does not even need their worship. Therefore, if He has commanded you to have faith, perform righteous deeds, and practice servitude and devotion, the benefit and profit return to you yourselves.

It is also noteworthy that in the system of worldly servants and masters, the closer a servant is to the master, the less his humility before him becomes, because he enjoys greater intimacy and the master has a greater need for him! However, in the system of servitude between the "creation" and the "Creator," the matter is the opposite: the closer the angels and the saints of Allah become to Him, the greater their station of servitude becomes [2].

After the futility and aimlessness of the universe were refuted in the previous verses, establishing that this world has a sacred purpose, the verses under discussion address the issue of the Oneness of the Deity and the Manager and Disposer of this world, saying: "Or have they taken gods from the earth who can raise the dead [and spread life]?" (أَمِ اتَّخَذُوا آلِهَةً مِنَ الْأَرْضِ هُمْ يُنْشِرُونَ).

[^1]: "Yastahsirun" is derived from the root "hasr," which originally means to uncover something hidden or to set aside a shield. Subsequently, it is used to mean fatigue and weakness, as if a person's energies in such a state are all revealed and consumed, leaving nothing hidden within the body. [^2]: Al-Mizan, under the verses in question.

This sentence actually points to the fact that the Deity must be the Creator, especially the Creator of life, which is the most brilliant aspect of creation. This is essentially similar to what we read in Surah Al-Hajj, verse 73: "Indeed, those you invoke besides Allah will never create [as much as] a fly, even if they gathered together for that purpose" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ لَنْ يَخْلُقُوا ذُباباً وَ لَوِ اجْتَمَعُوا لَهُ). How then are they worthy of worship? [1] The expression "gods from the earth" (آلِهَةً مِنَ الْأَرْضِ) refers to the idols and deities they fashioned from stone, wood, and the like, yet imagined them to be rulers over the heavens.

The next verse presents one of the clear proofs for the negation of "gods" and the deities of the polytheists in this manner: "Had there been within the heavens and earth gods besides Allah, they both would have been ruined [and the order of the universe would have collapsed]" (لَوْ كانَ فِيهِما آلِهَةٌ إِلَّا اللَّهُ لَفَسَدَتا).

"So exalted is Allah, Lord of the Throne, above what they describe" (فَسُبْحانَ اللَّهِ رَبِّ الْعَرْشِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ).

These improper attributions, these fabricated gods, and these illusory deities are nothing more than fantasies, and the threshold of His Pure Essence's majesty is not stained by such false descriptions.

[^1]: "Yunshirun" is from the root "nashr," meaning to unfold something folded, and it is also used metaphorically for the creation and dispersal of creatures across the expanse of the earth and sky. Some commentators insist on interpreting this sentence as a reference to the Resurrection and the return of the dead to new life, whereas, considering the subsequent verses, it becomes clear that the discussion is about the Oneness of Allah's Pure Essence and the True Deity, not the Resurrection and life after death.

The Argument of Mutual Exclusion (Burhan al-Tamanu')

The proof for Monotheism (Tawhid) and the negation of multiple gods mentioned in the above verse is, while simple and clear, one of the precise philosophical arguments in this field, which scholars refer to as the "Argument of Mutual Exclusion." This argument can be summarized as follows:

We undoubtedly observe a single, unified system governing this world—a system that is harmonious in all directions, its laws are constant and applicable in both heaven and earth, its programs are consistent with one another, and its parts are proportionate.

This harmony of the laws and systems of creation indicates that they originate from a single source. For if there were multiple sources and differing wills, this harmony would never exist, and that which the Quran describes as "corruption" (fasad) would be clearly visible in the world.

If we are inclined toward research and study, we can easily tell from examining a book whether it was written by one person or several. A book authored by one person possesses a specific harmony and cohesion among its expressions, sentence structures, various terms, metaphors, allusions, titles, and the manner of entering and exiting discussions; in short, all its parts are integrated and in unison because they are the product of one mind and the flow of one pen.

However, if two or more people—even if they are all scholars, sincere, and possess a spirit of close cooperation—each take responsibility for authoring a section of it, the traces of this duality or plurality will still appear in the depths of the expressions, words, and methods of discussion.

The reason is clear: no matter how much two people share the same thoughts and tastes, they are ultimately two people. If everything about them were one, they would be one person. Therefore, there must certainly be differences for them to be two people, and these differences will eventually manifest in their work and leave their mark on their writings.

Now, the larger and more detailed this book is, and the more diverse the subjects it discusses, the sooner this lack of harmony will be felt.

The great book of the universe, whose grandeur is such that we are lost within its expressions with our entire being, is also subject to this same law.

It is true that we cannot study this entire book even in a whole lifetime, but the extent that we and all the world's scientists have been privileged to study exhibits such harmony that it clearly indicates the unity of the Author. No matter how much we flip through the pages of this wondrous book, the traces of a sublime order and an indescribable cohesion and harmony among its words, lines, and pages are visible everywhere.

If multiple wills and sources were involved in the administration of this world and its systems, this harmony would not be possible.

Truly, why can space scientists launch spacecraft into space with complete precision and land the lunar module exactly at the location they scientifically predicted, then move it from there and bring it down to the predicted spot on Earth? Is this precision in calculations not because the system governing all existence—which is the basis for these scientists' calculations—is precise, cohesive, and harmonious? If there were even a tiny bit of disharmony (even one-hundredth of a second in terms of time) or any fluctuation in it, all their calculations would be disrupted.

In short: if two or more wills governed the world, each would have its own requirements, each would neutralize the effect of the other, and eventually, the world would fall into ruin.

Question

A question arises here, the answer to which can be understood from the previous explanations. The question is: multiple gods would only cause corruption in the world if they rose to fight one another; but if we accept that they are wise and knowledgeable beings, they would surely manage the world together with each other's help.

Answer

The answer to this question is not very complex. Their being wise does not eliminate their plurality. When we say they are multiple, it means they are not one in every respect; for if they were one in all aspects, they would be one God. Therefore, wherever there is plurality, there must be differences and disagreements, which inevitably affect will and action, leading the universe into chaos and corruption.

(Reflect carefully).

This "Argument of Mutual Exclusion" has been expressed in other forms as well, which are beyond the scope of our discussion, and what we have stated above is the best way to present it.

In some of these arguments, it is emphasized that if two wills governed creation, the world would not have existed at all, whereas the above verse speaks of the "corruption" of the world and the disruption of order, not the non-existence of the world (Reflect carefully).

Interestingly, in a tradition narrated by Hisham ibn al-Hakam from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that the Imam عليه السلام, in response to an atheist who spoke of multiple gods, said: "These two gods you speak of are either both eternal and powerful, or both are weak and incapable, or one is strong and the other is weak?

If both are strong, why does neither push the other aside to take charge of the world's administration alone? And if you suppose that one is strong and the other is weak, you have accepted the Oneness of God, because the second one is weak and incapable, and therefore is not God.

And if you say they are two, it must be one of two cases: either they agree in all respects or they differ. But when we see creation as organized, the stars of heaven each traveling in their specific paths, night and day succeeding each other with a specific order, and the sun and moon each having their own specific program, this harmony in the administration of the world and the cohesion of its affairs is proof that the Administrator is One.

Furthermore, if you claim God is two, there must necessarily be a gap (a distinction) between them for duality to be established. In this case, that gap (distinction) would itself be a third eternal being, and thus the gods would become three. And if you say they are three, there must be two gaps (distinctions) between them, in which case you must believe in five eternal beings, and in this way, the number increases and ends up in infinity" [1]. The beginning of this tradition refers to the Argument of Mutual Exclusion, and its conclusion refers to another argument called the "Argument of the Gap" (Burhan al-Furjah) or "The difference between what is shared and what is distinct."

In another tradition, we read that Hisham ibn al-Hakam asked Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"What is the proof that Allah is One?" He replied: "The continuity of administration and the perfection of creation, just as Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, said: 'Had there been within the heavens and earth gods besides Allah, they both would have been ruined.'"

Hisham says: "I said: 'What is the proof for the Oneness of God?' He replied: 'The interconnectedness and cohesion of the world's administration, and the perfection of creation, just as Allah the Almighty said: لَوْ كانَ فِيهِما آلِهَةٌ إِلَّا اللَّهُ لَفَسَدَتا (Had there been within the heavens and earth gods besides Him, the world would have been led to ruin)'" [1].

[^1]: Tawhid al-Saduq (as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, pp. 417-418).

After the Oneness of the Administrator and Governor of this world was proven by the argument in the verse, the next verse says: "He has organized the world so wisely that there is no room for objection or debate. Indeed, 'He cannot be questioned about what He does, while they will be questioned'" (لَا يُسْأَلُ عَمَّا يَفْعَلُ وَهُمْ يُسْأَلُونَ).

Although commentators have said much regarding the interpretation of this verse, what was stated above seems closest to the truth.

To explain: we have two types of questions. One type is the explanatory question, where a person is unaware of matters and wishes to understand their reality; even with the knowledge and faith that the action performed was correct, they still want to know the main point and the real objective. This type of question is permissible regarding Allah's actions; rather, it is this very type of question that is considered the source of inquiry and research into the world of creation and scientific issues. The companions of the Prophet and the Imams had many such questions regarding both the creative (takwini) and legislative (tashri'i) realms.

However, the other type of question is the protest-oriented question, the meaning of which is that the action performed was incorrect or wrong. For example, we say to someone who has broken their promise without reason: "Why do you break your promise?" The goal is not to seek an explanation but to raise an objection.

Certainly, this type of question is meaningless regarding the actions of the Wise Allah, and if it ever comes from someone, it is surely due to ignorance. However, there is much room for this type of question in the actions of others.

In a tradition from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read that in response to Jabir al-Ju'fi's question about this verse, he said:

"Because He does not do anything except that which is wisdom and correctness" [1].

Furthermore, it can be clearly concluded from this statement that if someone has a question of the second type, it is proof that they have not yet known Allah properly and are unaware of His being Wise.

[^1]: Tawhid al-Saduq (Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 419).

The next verse contains two other proofs regarding the negation of polytheism, which, together with the previous proof, make a total of three. First, it says: "Or have they taken gods besides Him? Say, 'Produce your proof'" (أَمِ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ آلِهَةً قُلْ هاتُوا بُرْهانَكُمْ).

This indicates that if you set aside the previous proof—that the system of the universe is proof of Monotheism—at the very least, there is no proof whatsoever to establish polytheism and the divinity of these gods. How can a rational person accept a matter without proof?

Then it points to the final proof, saying: "It is not only I and my companions who speak of Monotheism; rather, all the prophets and believers of the past were also monotheists" (هذا ذِكْرُ مَنْ مَعِيَ وَ ذِكْرُ مَنْ قَبْلِي).

This is the same proof that theologians mention under the title of the consensus and agreement of the prophets on the issue of the Oneness of God.

And since the large number of idolaters (especially in the living conditions of Muslims in Mecca, which Surah al-Anbiya addresses) might sometimes prevent some from accepting Monotheism, it adds: "But most of them do not know the truth, so they are turning away" (بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ الْحَقَّ فَهُمْ مُعْرِضُونَ).

The opposition of an ignorant majority in many societies has always been a reason for the turning away of the uninformed. In many verses, whether revealed in "Meccan" or "Medinan" surahs, the Quran has severely condemned relying on this majority and does not grant it any value; rather, it considers proof and logic to be the criteria.

And since some uninformed people might say we have prophets like Jesus عليه السلام who called to multiple gods, the Quran, in the final verse under discussion, states with complete clarity: "And We sent no messenger before you but We revealed to him that, 'There is no deity except Me, so worship Me'" (وَ ما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا نُوحِي إِلَيْهِ أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنَا فَاعْبُدُونِ).

In this way, it is proven that neither Jesus nor anyone else ever called to polytheism, and such attributions are slanders.

Ayahs

وَقَالُوا۟ ٱتَّخَذَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ وَلَدًا ۗ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ ۚ بَلْ عِبَادٌ مُّكْرَمُونَ (٢٦)
They say, ‘The All-beneficent has taken offsprings.’ Immaculate is He! Indeed, they are [His] honoured servants. (26)
لَا يَسْبِقُونَهُۥ بِٱلْقَوْلِ وَهُم بِأَمْرِهِۦ يَعْمَلُونَ (٢٧)
They do not venture to speak ahead of Him, and they act by His command. (27)
يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ وَلَا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ٱرْتَضَىٰ وَهُم مِّنْ خَشْيَتِهِۦ مُشْفِقُونَ (٢٨)
He knows that which is before them and that which is behind them, and they do not intercede except for someone He approves of, and they are apprehensive for the fear of Him. (28)
۞ وَمَن يَقُلْ مِنْهُمْ إِنِّىٓ إِلَـٰهٌ مِّن دُونِهِۦ فَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِيهِ جَهَنَّمَ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٢٩)
Should any of them say, ‘I am a god besides Him,’ We will requite him with hell. Thus do We requite the wrongdoers. (29)

Commentary

Angels are Worthy and Obedient Servants

Since the final verse of the previous discussion spoke of the Divine Prophets and the negation of any form of polytheism (and implicitly the negation of Prophet Jesus being a son), the verses under discussion are all regarding the negation of angels being children [of God].

To explain: many Arab polytheists believed that angels were the daughters of Allah, and for this reason, they would sometimes worship them. In the above verses, the Quran explicitly condemns this superstitious and baseless belief and demonstrates its falsehood through various arguments.

First, It says: "And they say: 'The Most Compassionate has taken a son'" (وَ قالُوا اتَّخَذَ الرَّحْمنُ وَلَداً).

If their intention was a literal son, it would necessitate Him having a physical body; and if it was "adoption" (tabanni), which was common among the Arabs, that too would be evidence of weakness and need. Beyond all this, fundamentally, one needs a child who is mortal; so that for the survival of his generation, status, and legacy, his child may continue his life in the long term. Or it is to avoid feeling lonely and to find companionship, or to gain power. However, for an Eternal and Everlasting Being who is non-corporeal and needless in every respect, a child is meaningless in His case.

Therefore, He immediately states: "Glory be to Him! [He is pure from such defects]" (سُبْحانَهُ‌).

Then, He describes the attributes of the angels in six parts, which together constitute a clear proof for the negation of them being children:

1- "They are [only] servants of Allah" (بَلْ عِبادٌ).

2- "Worthy and honored servants" (مُكْرَمُونَ‌).

They are not like runaway slaves who submit to service only under the pressure of a master; rather, they are servants who are worthy in every respect, who know the way and custom of servitude well, and take pride in it. Allah has also honored them because of their sincerity in servitude and has increased His bounties upon them.

3- They are so disciplined, submissive, and obedient to Allah's command that "they do not precede Him in speech" (لا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ‌).

4- And regarding action as well, "they only act by His command" (وَ هُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ‌).

Are these the attributes of children or the attributes of servants?!

Then, referring to the Lord's encompassing knowledge over them, He says: "Allah knows their present and future deeds, as well as their past deeds; He is aware of both their world and their hereafter, both before their existence and after their existence" (يَعْلَمُ ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ ما خَلْفَهُمْ‌) [1]. Certainly, the angels are aware that Allah has such an encompassing knowledge over them, and this very gnosis (ma'rifah) causes them to neither speak before Him nor disobey His command. In this way, this sentence can serve as a rationale for the previous verse.

5- Undoubtedly, those who are the honored and worthy servants of Allah will intercede for the needy, but it must be noted that "they never intercede for anyone unless they know that Allah is pleased with him" and has granted permission for his intercession (وَ لا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضى‌).

Certainly, Allah's pleasure and His granting of permission for intercession cannot be without reason. It is surely due to true faith or deeds that preserve a person's connection with Allah. In other words, a person may be stained by sin, but if he does not completely sever his link with the Lord and the Divine Awliya (Saints), there is hope for intercession for him.

However, if he completely severs his link in terms of thought and ideology, or if he is so polluted in practice that he loses the merit for intercession, at that point, no Messenger Prophet or Archangel will intercede for him.

This is the same point we mentioned in the discussion on the philosophy of intercession in previous sections: that intercession is a human-building school and a means to bring the polluted back from halfway and to prevent despair and hopelessness—which itself is a factor for drowning in deviation and sin. Belief in this kind of intercession causes sinful individuals not to sever their relationship with Allah, the Prophets, and the Imams, not to destroy all bridges behind them, and to maintain a line of return [2]. Additionally, this sentence is a response to those who said: "We worship the angels so they may intercede for us before Allah." The Quran says they can do nothing of their own accord, and whatever you want, you must ask directly from Allah, even the permission for the intercessors to intercede.

6- It is because of this very gnosis and awareness that "they stand in awe of Him alone" and only allow fear of Him into their hearts (وَ هُمْ مِنْ خَشْيَتِهِ مُشْفِقُونَ‌).

They do not fear because they have committed a sin, but rather they are apprehensive of falling short in worship or committing a "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla).

Interestingly, "khashyah" (خشیت) in its linguistic root does not mean just any kind of fear; rather, it is a fear accompanied by veneration and respect.

"Mushfiq" (مشفق) is from the root "ishfaq" (اشفاق), meaning an attention mixed with fear (since it is originally derived from the root "shafaq" (شفق), which is light mixed with darkness).

Therefore, their fear of Allah is not like a human's fear of a terrifying accident, nor is their "ishfaq" like a person's dread of a dangerous creature. Rather, their fear and ishfaq are a blend of respect, care, attention, gnosis, and a sense of responsibility [3].

It is clear that angels with these outstanding and distinguished attributes and the station of pure servitude would never claim divinity. However, if hypothetically "any of them were to say, 'I am a god besides Him,' We would recompense him with Hell; thus do We recompense the wrongdoers" (وَ مَنْ يَقُلْ مِنْهُمْ إِنِّي إِلهٌ مِنْ دُونِهِ فَذلِكَ نَجْزِيهِ جَهَنَّمَ كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الظَّالِمِينَ‌). In reality, claiming divinity is a clear instance of wrongdoing (zulm) against oneself and against society, and it is included in the general law: "thus do We recompense the wrongdoers."

[1]: Great commentators have three explanations for this sentence, all of which we have mentioned together in the above text as they are not contradictory. [2]: We have discussed "Intercession" in detail under verses 48 and 254 of Surah Al-Baqarah; please refer there. [3]: Mufradat al-Raghib, roots "khashyah" and "shafaq"; and Tafsir al-Safi, under the verses discussed.

Ayahs

أَوَلَمْ يَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ كَانَتَا رَتْقًا فَفَتَقْنَـٰهُمَا ۖ وَجَعَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلْمَآءِ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ حَىٍّ ۖ أَفَلَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٣٠)
Have the faithless not regarded that the heavens and the earth were interwoven and We unravelled them, and We made every living thing out of water? Will they not then have faith? (30)
وَجَعَلْنَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ رَوَٰسِىَ أَن تَمِيدَ بِهِمْ وَجَعَلْنَا فِيهَا فِجَاجًا سُبُلًا لَّعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ (٣١)
We set firm mountains in the earth lest it should shake with them, and We made broad ways in them so that they may be guided [to their destinations]. (31)
وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلسَّمَآءَ سَقْفًا مَّحْفُوظًا ۖ وَهُمْ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِهَا مُعْرِضُونَ (٣٢)
We made the sky a preserved roof and yet they are disregardful of its signs. (32)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ ۖ كُلٌّ فِى فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ (٣٣)
It is He who created the night and the day, the sun and the moon, each swimming in an orbit. (33)

Commentary

More Signs of Allah in the Universe

Following the previous discussions regarding the superstitious beliefs of the polytheists and the proofs provided for Monotheism (Tawhid), the verses under discussion present a series of Allah’s signs in the system of the universe and its orderly governance, serving as an emphasis on those earlier points.

First, it says: "Have those who disbelieve not seen that the heavens and the earth were joined together, and We clove them asunder?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَرَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنَّ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ كانَتا رَتْقاً فَفَتَقْناهُما).

"And We made from water every living thing?" (وَ جَعَلْنا مِنَ الْماءِ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ حَيٍ‌).

"Will they not then believe?" (أَ فَلا يُؤْمِنُونَ‌) despite observing these verses and signs.

Regarding what is meant by "Ratq" (joining/sewing) and "Fatq" (splitting/unsewing) mentioned here in relation to the heavens and the earth, commentators have offered many views. Among them, three interpretations seem closest to the truth, and as we shall explain, it is possible for all three to be encompassed within the concept of the verse: [^1]

  1. The joining of the heaven and the earth refers to the beginning of creation when, according to scientific theories, the entirety of this world was a single, massive mass of burning vapor. Due to internal explosions and motion, it gradually disintegrated, and the planets and stars—including the solar system and the Earth—came into existence, and the universe is still expanding.

  2. The "joining" refers to the uniformity of the materials of the universe, such that

[^1]: Fakhr al-Razi in Tafsir al-Kabir and some other commentators.

everything was merged together and appeared as a single substance. However, with the passage of time, the materials separated and formed new compositions. Various types of plants, animals, and other beings appeared in the heavens and the earth—beings each having a specific system, unique effects, and properties, and each being a sign of the greatness of the Lord and His infinite knowledge and power. [^1]

  1. The "joining" of the heaven means that in the beginning, no rain fell, and the "joining" of the earth means that at that time, no plants grew. However, Allah opened both; He sent down rain from the heaven and caused various plants to grow from the earth.

Numerous narrations from the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام point to this latter meaning, and some of them allude to the first interpretation. [^2] Undoubtedly, the last interpretation is something visible to the eye—how rain descends from the sky, the earth splits, and plants grow—and it is fully consistent with the phrase "Have those who disbelieve not seen..." (أَ وَ لَمْ يَرَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا) and harmonizes perfectly with the phrase "And We made from water every living thing" (وَ جَعَلْنا مِنَ الْماءِ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ حَيٍ‌).

However, the first and second interpretations are also not contrary to the broad meaning of these phrases, as "seeing" (ru'yah) sometimes means "knowledge." It is true that this knowledge and awareness are not for everyone; only scientists can gain insights into the past of the earth and heaven, their joining, and then their separation. But we know that the Quran is not a book for one century or one era; rather, it is a guide and a pathfinder for humans in all centuries and eras.

For this reason, it possesses such deep content that it is usable for every group and every age.

[^1]: Al-Mizan, under the verse. [^2]: Refer to Tafsir al-Safi and Nur al-Thaqalayn under the verse in question.

On this basis, we believe there is no obstacle to the above verse having all three interpretations, each being correct and complete in its own place. We have repeatedly stated that using a word in more than one meaning is not only unobjectionable but is sometimes a proof of the perfection of eloquence. The fact that we read in narrations that "the Quran has various layers (butun)" may also refer to this very concept.

As for the origin of all living beings from water, mentioned at the end of the verse, there are two famous interpretations:

  1. The life of all living beings—including plants and animals—depends on water; the very water whose ultimate source is the rain that descends from the sky.

  2. Alternatively, "water" (ma') here refers to the seminal fluid from which living beings are usually created.

Interestingly, modern scientists believe that the first spark of life appeared in the depths of the seas; for this reason, they consider the beginning of life to be from water. If the Quran considers the creation of man to be from dust, we must not forget that "dust" refers to "clay" (tin), which is a combination of water and soil.

It is also noteworthy that according to scientific research, a major part of the body of humans and many animals is composed of water (about seventy percent!).

As for the objection raised by some that the creation of angels and Jinn—despite being living beings—is certainly not from water, the answer is clear: the objective is the living beings that are perceptible to us.

In a narration, we read that a person asked Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "What does water taste like?" The Imam first said: Sal tafaqquhan wa la tas'al ta'annutan: "Ask for the purpose of learning, not for the purpose of finding fault!" Then he added: Ta'm al-ma'i ta'm al-hayat! Qala Allahu Subhanahu: "Wa ja'alna min al-ma'i kulla shay'in hayy": "The taste of water is the taste of life! Allah says: 'We made from water every living thing.'"

Especially when a person in summer, after a long and continuous thirst in that scorching weather, reaches refreshing water—the moment he swallows the first gulps of water, he feels as if spirit and life are being breathed into his body. In fact, the Imam wants to define the connection and bond between life and water with this beautiful expression.

The next verse points to another part of the signs of Monotheism and His great blessings, saying: "And We placed in the earth firm mountains, lest it should shake with them" (وَ جَعَلْنا فِي الْأَرْضِ رَواسِيَ أَنْ تَمِيدَ بِهِمْ‌). [^1] We have previously stated that mountains encompass the Earth like armor, and this significantly prevents severe tremors of the earth caused by the pressure of internal gases. Additionally, this state of the mountains minimizes the movements of the Earth's crust in response to the tides caused by the moon.

On the other hand, if there were no mountains, the surface of the earth would be constantly exposed to gales, and no tranquility would be seen upon it, just as it is in dry and scorching deserts.

[^1]: "Rawasi" is the plural of "Rasiyah," meaning hard and firm mountains. Since these types of mountains are linked together at their roots, it may refer to this connectivity. It has been scientifically proven that this root-level connectivity of mountains has a profound effect on preventing earth tremors. "Tamid" is from the root "Mayd," meaning the shaking and irregular movement of large objects.

Then He points to another blessing, which is also among the signs of His greatness, saying: "And We placed therein broad pathways, that they may be guided" (وَ جَعَلْنا فِيها فِجاجاً سُبُلًا لَعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ‌).

Truly, if these valleys and gaps did not exist, the massive mountain ranges on earth would have separated different regions such that their terrestrial connection would be completely severed. This shows that all these phenomena are according to a plan and calculation.

Since the tranquility of the earth alone is not sufficient for the tranquility of human life—rather, there must also be safety from above—He adds in the next verse: "And We made the sky a protected roof, yet they turn away from its signs" (وَ جَعَلْنَا السَّماءَ سَقْفاً مَحْفُوظاً وَ هُمْ عَنْ آياتِها مُعْرِضُونَ‌). The meaning of "sky" here—as we have said before—is the atmosphere that surrounds the earth, the thickness of which, according to scientific research, is hundreds of kilometers. This seemingly delicate layer, composed of air and gases, is so strong and resistant that any intrusive object coming toward the earth from outside is destroyed. It protects the Earth against the day-and-night bombardment of "meteorites," which are more dangerous than any bullet.

Furthermore, the sun's rays, which contain lethal components, are filtered by it, and it prevents the penetration of deadly cosmic rays that pour toward the earth from outside the atmosphere.

Yes, this sky is a very strong and stable roof that Allah has protected from destruction. [^1]

In the last verse under discussion, He addresses the creation of night and day and the sun and the moon, saying: "And He it is Who created the night and the day, and the sun and the moon" (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ اللَّيْلَ وَ النَّهارَ وَ الشَّمْسَ وَ الْقَمَرَ).

"Each floating in an orbit" (كُلٌّ فِي فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ‌).

Key Points

1- Commentary on "كُلٌّ فِي فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ‌"

Commentators have various explanations for this phrase, but what is consistent with the established research of astronomers is that the movement of the sun in the above verse refers either to its rotational movement around itself or the movement it has along with the solar system.
