It is also necessary to mention that the word "Kull" (each/all) may refer to the moon and the sun, as well as the stars, which is inferred from the word "Layl" (night).

Some great commentators have also suggested the possibility that it refers to "night," "day," "moon," and "sun" (all four), because the night—which is the conical shadow of the earth—also has an orbit of its own. If someone were to look at the Earth from a distance outside, they would see this dark conical shadow constantly in motion around the earth. Similarly, the sunlight that shines on the earth and forms the day is like a cylinder

[^1]: Some commentators consider the above verse to be in harmony with the verses in the Holy Quran regarding the protection of the sky from the ascent of devils by means of "Shihab" (meteors) (such as: "And a protection against every rebellious devil" - Al-Saffat: 7). However, it is clear that this interpretation is not consistent with the word "roof" (saqf), because a roof is a covering for those who are under it, not those who are above it (reflect on this).

that constantly changes location around this sphere. Therefore, night and day each have their own path and place. [^1] The possibility has also been raised that the movement of the sun refers to its movement as perceived by us, because to terrestrial observers, both the sun and the moon appear to be in motion.

2- The Sky is a Strong Roof

We have previously mentioned that "Sama'" (sky/heaven) in the Quran appears with various meanings. Sometimes it refers to the Earth's atmosphere—the thick layer of air surrounding the terrestrial globe—as in the above verse. Here, it is not out of place to hear more explanation about the strength of this great roof from the words of natural scientists:

"Frank Allen," a professor of biophysics, writes: "The atmosphere, composed of life-sustaining gases on the Earth's surface, is thick (and dense) enough to protect the Earth like armor from the lethal collection of twenty million celestial stones per day, which strike it at a speed of about 50 kilometers per second.

The Earth's atmosphere, in addition to its other functions, maintains the temperature on the Earth's surface at a level suitable for life, and also transfers the essential supply of 'water and water vapor' from the oceans to the lands. If this were not the case, all continents would become dry, uninhabitable deserts. Thus, it must be said that the oceans and the Earth's atmosphere act as a flywheel for the Earth.

The weight of some of these meteors pouring toward the earth is as small as one-thousandth of a gram, but their force, due to that extraordinary speed, is equivalent to the force of

[^1]: Adapted from Al-Mizan, under the verse.

atomic particles that form a destructive bomb! And the volume of those meteors is sometimes no larger than a grain of sand! Every day, millions of these meteors burn or turn into vapor before reaching the Earth's surface. However, occasionally the volume and weight of some meteors are so great that they pass through the gaseous layer and strike the Earth's surface.

Among the meteors that passed through the clutches of the aforementioned gaseous envelope and reached the Earth is the massive and famous 'Siberian' meteor that struck the Earth in 1908 AD. Its diameter was such that it occupied about 40 kilometers of land and caused many casualties! Another is the meteor that landed in 'Arizona,' USA, which was 1 kilometer in diameter and 200 meters deep, and upon its fall, it created a deep rift in the ground, and its explosion produced many small meteors that occupied a relatively large area of land.

A. Cressy Morrison writes: 'If the air surrounding the Earth were slightly thinner than it is, the celestial bodies and piercing meteors that strike it by the millions every day and explode and are destroyed in that outer space would constantly reach the Earth's surface and strike every corner of it.'

These celestial bodies move at speeds of 6 to 40 miles per second! And whatever they hit, they cause an explosion and fire. If the speed and movement of these bodies were less than they are—for example, the speed of a bullet—they would all fall to the Earth's surface, and the result of their destruction would be obvious. For instance, if a human were in the path of the smallest piece of these celestial bodies, the intensity of their heat—moving at ninety times the speed of a bullet—would tear him to pieces

and disintegrate him! The density of the air surrounding the Earth is such that it allows cosmic rays to pass toward the Earth only to the extent necessary for the growth of plants, destroys all harmful germs in that space, and produces beneficial vitamins." [^1]

Ayahs

وَمَا جَعَلْنَا لِبَشَرٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ ٱلْخُلْدَ ۖ أَفَإِي۟ن مِّتَّ فَهُمُ ٱلْخَـٰلِدُونَ (٣٤)
We did not give immortality to any human before you. If you are fated to die, will they live on forever? (34)
كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذَآئِقَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۗ وَنَبْلُوكُم بِٱلشَّرِّ وَٱلْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً ۖ وَإِلَيْنَا تُرْجَعُونَ (٣٥)
Every soul shall taste death, and We will test you with good and ill by way of test, and to Us you will be brought back. (35)

Commentary

Everyone Dies!

In a portion of the previous verses, we read that the polytheists resorted to the issue of the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله humanity to cast doubt on his prophethood, believing that a prophet must necessarily be an angel and free from any human characteristics.

The verses under discussion point to some of their other objections: sometimes they would say that the commotion caused by the Prophet—whom they referred to as a poet—would not last, and everything would end with his death, as we read in Surah At-Tur, verse 30: "أَمْ يَقُولُونَ شاعِرٌ نَتَرَبَّصُ بِهِ رَيْبَ الْمَنُونِ" (Or do they say, 'A poet for whom we await a misfortune of time'?).

And sometimes they imagined that since this man believes he is the Seal of the Prophets, he must never die so that he may remain the guardian of his own religion; therefore, his future death would be proof of the invalidity of his claim! In the first of the above verses, the Quran answers them in a short sentence, saying: "We did not grant eternal life to any human before you" (وَ ما جَعَلْنا لِبَشَرٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ الْخُلْدَ).

This is the unchangeable law of creation: no one possesses eternal life.

Furthermore, those who are currently rejoicing at your death, "if you die, will they then live forever?" (أَ فَإِنْ مِتَّ فَهُمُ الْخالِدُونَ).

Perhaps it needs no explanation that the survival of a Sharia, religion, and creed does not depend on the survival of its bringer. The religions of Ibrahim عليه السلام, Musa عليه السلام, and 'Isa عليه السلام, although they were not eternal, remained for centuries after the passing of these great prophets (and in the case of Prophet Christ, after his ascension to heaven).

Therefore, the immortality of a religion does not require the permanent guardianship of the Prophet; it is possible for his successors to continue his path.

As for their notion that everything would end with the passing of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, they have indeed miscalculated. This statement is only true regarding matters that depend solely on an individual. Islam was neither dependent on the person of the Prophet nor on his companions; it was a living and dynamic faith that moves forward based on its internal momentum, breaking the boundaries of time and space to continue its journey.

Then, it recounts the general law of death for all souls, without exception, as follows: "Every soul shall taste death" (كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ).

It is necessary to mention that the word "Nafs" (soul/self) has been used in various meanings in the Holy Quran. The first meaning of Nafs is "Essence" or the self itself; this is a broad meaning that is even applied to the Pure Essence of Allah, as we read: "كَتَبَ عَلى‌ نَفْسِهِ الرَّحْمَةَ" (He has prescribed mercy upon Himself) (Al-An'am, verse 12).

Secondly, this word is used for a human being, meaning the combination of his body and spirit, such as: "مَنْ قَتَلَ نَفْساً بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ أَوْ فَسادٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَكَأَنَّما قَتَلَ النَّاسَ جَمِيعاً" (Whoever kills a soul unless for a soul or for corruption [done] in the land—it is as if he had slain mankind entirely) (Al-Ma'idah, verse 32).

And sometimes it is used specifically for the human spirit, such as: "أَخْرِجُوا أَنْفُسَكُمُ" (The angels of death say: "Discharge your souls") (Al-An'am, verse 93).

It is clear that the meaning of "Nafs" in the verses under discussion is the second meaning. Therefore, the intent is to state the general law of death regarding human beings. In this way, no room remains for the objection that the term "Nafs" includes Allah or the angels, which would require explaining how the verse must be specified to exclude them [1]. After mentioning the universal law of death, the question arises: what is the purpose of this unstable life and what benefit does it have?

Following this, the Quran says: "We test you with evil and with good as a trial; and to Us you will be returned" (وَ نَبْلُوكُمْ بِالشَّرِّ وَ الْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً وَ إِلَيْنا تُرْجَعُونَ).

Your original place is not this world, but rather another place. You only come here to take an exam, and after the end of the exam and gaining the necessary perfection, you will go to your original place, which is the abode of the Hereafter.

It is noteworthy that among the subjects of the test, "evil" is mentioned before "good," and it should be so, because although divine testing is sometimes with blessings and sometimes with calamities, testing by means of calamities is certainly harder and more difficult.

It is also necessary to mention that "evil" here does not mean absolute evil, because the premise is that this evil is a means of testing and perfection. Therefore, the intent is relative evil, and fundamentally, in the entirety of the universe, with a correct monotheistic vision, absolute evil does not exist! (Reflect on this).

Hence, in a Hadith from Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام, we read that the Imam had fallen ill. A group of brothers (and companions) came to visit him and asked:

"How do you find yourself, O Amir al-Mu'minin?" He replied: "With evil (Shar)!!" They said: "This is not the kind of speech expected from someone like you."

The Imam replied: "Allah the Almighty says: 'We test you with evil and with good as a trial.' Thus, 'good' (Khayr) is health and wealth, and 'evil' (Shar) is illness and poverty" (and this is an expression I have chosen from the Holy Quran).

A significant question remains here: why does Allah test His servants, and fundamentally, what does "testing" mean in relation to Allah?! We have provided the answer to this question in the first volume of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 155 of Surah Al-Baqarah, stating that testing in relation to Allah means "nurturing and development" (please study the full explanation of this subject there).

[1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 14, p. 312.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا رَءَاكَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِن يَتَّخِذُونَكَ إِلَّا هُزُوًا أَهَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى يَذْكُرُ ءَالِهَتَكُمْ وَهُم بِذِكْرِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ هُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٣٦)
Whenever the faithless see you they only take you in derision: ‘Is this the one who remembers your gods?’ while they remain defiant towards the remembrance of the All-beneficent. (36)
خُلِقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مِنْ عَجَلٍ ۚ سَأُو۟رِيكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِى فَلَا تَسْتَعْجِلُونِ (٣٧)
Man is a creature of haste. Soon I will show you My signs. So do not ask Me to hasten. (37)
وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٣٨)
And they say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’ (38)
لَوْ يَعْلَمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ حِينَ لَا يَكُفُّونَ عَن وُجُوهِهِمُ ٱلنَّارَ وَلَا عَن ظُهُورِهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ (٣٩)
If only the faithless knew about the time when they will not be able to keep the Fire off their faces and their backs, nor will they be helped! (39)
بَلْ تَأْتِيهِم بَغْتَةً فَتَبْهَتُهُمْ فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ رَدَّهَا وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ (٤٠)
Indeed, it will overtake them suddenly, dumbfounding them. So they will neither be able to avert it, nor will they be granted any respite. (40)

Commentary

Man is Created of Haste!

In these verses, we once again encounter other discussions regarding the stance of the polytheists toward the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), which reflect their deviant way of thinking concerning fundamental issues. First, it says: "And when those who disbelieve see you, they take you not except for mockery" (وَ إِذا رَآكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِنْ يَتَّخِذُونَكَ إِلَّا هُزُواً).

They point to you with indifference, saying: "Is this the one who mentions your gods [and idols] with evil?" (أَ هذَا الَّذِي يَذْكُرُ آلِهَتَكُمْ) [^1] "While they themselves are disbelievers in the mention of the Most Compassionate (Allah)" (وَ هُمْ بِذِكْرِ الرَّحْمنِ هُمْ كافِرُونَ).

The wonder lies here: if someone speaks ill of these stone and wooden idols—or rather, speaks the truth and says they are soulless, mindless, and worthless entities—they are astonished. Yet, if someone denies the Gracious and Merciful God, whose signs of mercy have encompassed the entire world and in everything there is a proof of His greatness and mercy, it is not a cause for wonder to them.

Indeed, when a person becomes accustomed to something, grows fond of it, and becomes prejudiced, it will appear beautiful in his eyes, even if it is the worst of things. Conversely, whenever he harbors enmity toward something, it gradually becomes ugly in his sight, even if it is the most beautiful and beloved of matters.

[^1]: It is interesting that in their phrasing they would say: "Is this the one who speaks of your gods," as they were not even willing to include the word "evil" in their statement and say "speaks ill of your gods!"

Then, referring to another of the ugly and unrestrained behaviors of these undisciplined individuals, He says: "Man is created of haste" (خُلِقَ الْإِنْسانُ مِنْ عجَلٍ). Although commentators have provided various explanations for the words "Man" (insan) and "Haste" (ajal) here, it is evident that "Man" here refers to the human species (specifically, those who are unrefined and outside the realm of guidance of divine leaders).

And "Haste" refers to hurry and precipitancy, as the subsequent verses are a clear witness to this fact. Elsewhere in the Quran, we read: "And man is ever hasty" (وَ كانَ الْإِنْسانُ عَجُولًا) (Surah Al-Isra, 11).

In reality, the expression "Man is created of haste" is a form of emphasis; meaning, man is so hasty that it is as if he were created from haste, and the warp and woof of his existence are formed by it! Truly, many ordinary people are like this: they are hasty in seeking both good and evil. Even when they are told that if they become tainted with disbelief and sin, divine punishment will seize them, they ask, "Then why does this punishment not come sooner?!" At the end of the verse, He adds: "Do not be in haste; I will show you My signs soon" (سَأُرِيكُمْ آياتِي فَلا تَسْتَعْجِلُونِ).

The term "My signs" (آياتِي) here may refer to the signs and tokens of punishment, calamities, and penalties with which the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) threatened the opponents, and these shallow-minded people repeatedly said: "So what happened to these calamities you frighten us with?"

The Quran says: Do not be in haste; it will not be long before they seize you.

It may also refer to the miracles that serve as proof of the truthfulness of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله); meaning, if you wait a little, sufficient miracles will be presented to you.

These two interpretations are not contradictory, as the polytheists were hasty in both regards, and Allah presented both to them, although the first interpretation seems closer and more consistent with the following verses.

Again, pointing to one of their hasty demands, it says: "And they say: 'When is this promise [of Resurrection], if you are truthful?'" (وَ يَقُولُونَ مَتى‌ هذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ). With total impatience, they were waiting for the arrival of the Day of Resurrection, oblivious to the fact that the arrival of the Resurrection is synonymous with their absolute misery and misfortune. But what can be done? A hasty person is even in a hurry for his own misfortune and destruction.

The expression "if you (plural) are truthful" (إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ), even though the addressee was the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), is because his true followers were also included in this address. Furthermore, they wanted to imply that the non-arrival of the Resurrection is proof that all of you are liars!

The next verse answers them, saying: "If those who disbelieved but knew the time when they will not be able to ward off the fire from their faces nor from their backs, and they will not be helped, [they would not have hastened the punishment and asked: When is the Resurrection?]" (لَوْ يَعْلَمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا حِينَ لا يَكُفُّونَ عَنْ وُجُوهِهِمُ النَّارَ وَ لا عَنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ وَ لا هُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ).

The mention of "faces" and "backs" in the above verse indicates that the fire of Hell is not such that it surrounds them from only one side; both their front and their backs are in the fire, as if they are drowned and buried in the midst of the flames! And the phrase "and they will not be helped" (وَ لا هُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ) indicates that these idols, which they imagine will be their intercessors or helpers, can do nothing for them.

And interestingly, "this divine punishment and burning fire will come upon them suddenly, so that it will confound them!" (بَلْ تَأْتِيهِمْ بَغْتَةً فَتَبْهَتُهُمْ). "And it will catch them so off-guard and overpower them that they will not have the power to repel it" (فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ رَدَّها).

Even if they ask for a respite and—contrary to their previous haste—request a delay, "they will not be given respite" (وَ لا هُمْ يُنْظَرُونَ).

Key Points [1- If man is inherently created hasty, why is haste forbidden?]

1- In light of the above verses, the question arises: if man is naturally hasty, why does the prohibition of haste follow it, saying: "Do not be in haste" (فَلا تَسْتَعْجِلُونِ)? Are these two not contradictory?

In response, we say that considering the principle of choice and free will of man, and the fact that attributes, spirits, and moral characteristics are changeable, there is no contradiction. This is because through training and purification of the soul (tazkiyah), this state can be transformed.

2- The phrase "Nay, it will come upon them suddenly and confound them" (بَلْ تَأْتِيهِمْ بَغْتَةً فَتَبْهَتُهُمْ) may indicate that everything about the punishments of the Resurrection is different from the punishments of this world. For example, regarding the fire of Hell, we read: "The fire of Allah, kindled, which leaps up over the hearts" (نارُ اللَّهِ الْمُوقَدَةُ الَّتِي تَطَّلِعُ عَلَى الْأَفْئِدَةِ) (Surah Al-Humazah, verses 6-7).

Or regarding the fuel of Hell, we read: "Whose fuel is men and stones" (وَقُودُهَا النَّاسُ وَ الْحِجارَةُ) (Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 24).

Such expressions show that the fire of Hell is unexpected, sudden, and confounding. [^1]

[^1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 14, p. 417.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدِ ٱسْتُهْزِئَ بِرُسُلٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَحَاقَ بِٱلَّذِينَ سَخِرُوا۟ مِنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٤١)
Apostles were certainly derided before you; but those who ridiculed them were besieged by what they had been deriding. (41)
قُلْ مَن يَكْلَؤُكُم بِٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ مِنَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ۗ بَلْ هُمْ عَن ذِكْرِ رَبِّهِم مُّعْرِضُونَ (٤٢)
Say, ‘Who can guard you, day and night, against [the punishment of] the All-beneficent [should He want to punish you]?’ Indeed, they are disregardful of their Lord’s remembrance. (42)
أَمْ لَهُمْ ءَالِهَةٌ تَمْنَعُهُم مِّن دُونِنَا ۚ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَلَا هُم مِّنَّا يُصْحَبُونَ (٤٣)
Do they have gods besides Us to defend them? Neither can they help themselves, nor can they shield [the idolaters] from Us. (43)
بَلْ مَتَّعْنَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ وَءَابَآءَهُمْ حَتَّىٰ طَالَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْعُمُرُ ۗ أَفَلَا يَرَوْنَ أَنَّا نَأْتِى ٱلْأَرْضَ نَنقُصُهَا مِنْ أَطْرَافِهَآ ۚ أَفَهُمُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبُونَ (٤٤)
Indeed, We have provided for them and their fathers until they lived on for long years. Do they not see how We visit the land diminishing it at its edges? Are they the ones who will prevail? (44)
قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أُنذِرُكُم بِٱلْوَحْىِ ۚ وَلَا يَسْمَعُ ٱلصُّمُّ ٱلدُّعَآءَ إِذَا مَا يُنذَرُونَ (٤٥)
Say, ‘I indeed warn you by the means of revelation.’ But the deaf do not hear the call when they are warned. (45)

Commentary

If the Ear is Your Ear...

In the preceding verses, we saw that the polytheists and disbelievers subjected the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله to ridicule—a long-standing habit of all arrogant, ignorant people who treat important and serious realities with mockery and jest.

In the first verse under discussion, as a means of comforting the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and consoling his heart, it says: You are not the only one who has been mocked; "Messengers before you were also mocked" (وَلَقَدِ اسْتُهْزِئَ بِرُسُلٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ).

"But finally, that which they used to mock of Divine punishments encompassed those who scoffed at them" (فَحاقَ بِالَّذِينَ سَخِرُوا مِنْهُمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ).

Therefore, do not allow grief or sorrow to find its way into you; such actions by the ignorant should not have the slightest effect on your great soul or cause any crack in your iron will.

In the next verse, it states: Not only will no one defend you against Allah's punishment in the Hereafter, but the same applies in this world: "Say, 'Who protects you by night and by day from the punishment of the Most Compassionate (Ar-Rahman)?'" (قُلْ مَنْ يَكْلَؤُكُمْ بِاللَّيْلِ وَ النَّهارِ مِنَ الرَّحْمنِ).

In fact, if Allah had not made the sky—the "Earth's atmosphere"—a protected roof (as mentioned in previous verses), that alone would have been enough to expose you day and night to the bombardment of celestial stones.

The Most Compassionate Lord has such love for you that He has appointed various guardians for your protection; if they were to leave you for a single moment, a flood of calamities would pour down upon you.

It is noteworthy that in this verse, "Ar-Rahman" (the Most Compassionate) is used instead of "Allah," meaning: see how many sins you have committed that you have even provoked the anger of the One who is the very source of universal mercy.

Then it adds: Yet they turn away from the remembrance of their Lord; they neither listen to the exhortations and advice of His Prophets, nor does the remembrance of Allah and His blessings move their hearts, nor do they employ their intellect for a moment in this path; "rather, they are turning away from the remembrance of their Lord" (بَلْ هُمْ عَنْ ذِكْرِ رَبِّهِمْ مُعْرِضُونَ).

Again, it asks as a question: What are these oppressive and sinful disbelievers relying upon against Divine retribution? "Or do they have gods who can defend them against Us?" (أَمْ لَهُمْ آلِهَةٌ تَمْنَعُهُمْ مِنْ دُونِنا). "These fabricated gods of theirs cannot even help themselves or defend their own beings" (لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَ أَنْفُسِهِمْ).

"Nor are they aided or accompanied by Us through mercy and spiritual power" (وَ لا هُمْ مِنَّا يُصْحَبُونَ) [1].

The next verse points to one of the major causes of rebellion and transgression in faithless individuals, saying: "Nay, We gave these people and their fathers various blessings until long lives passed over them" (بَلْ مَتَّعْنا هؤُلاءِ وَ آباءَهُمْ حَتَّى طالَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْعُمُرُ). But instead of this long life and abundant blessing stimulating a sense of gratitude in them to bow their heads at the threshold of servitude to the Truth, it became the source of their arrogance and rebellion.

But do they not see that this world and its blessings are not permanent? "Do they not see that We gradually diminish the land from its outlying borders?" (أَ فَلا يَرَوْنَ أَنَّا نَأْتِي الْأَرْضَ نَنْقُصُها مِنْ أَطْرافِها).

Nations and tribes come and go one after another; neither the small nor the great possess eternal life, and all eventually disappear behind the veil of annihilation. Nations that were stronger, more powerful, and more rebellious than them have all been drawn into the dark earth, and even the scholars, greats, and learned ones—upon whom the stability of the earth rested—have closed their eyes to this world! "In such a state, are they the victors, or are We the victors?" (أَ فَهُمُ الْغالِبُونَ).

Regarding what is meant by the phrase "We come to the land and gradually diminish it from its borders" (أَنَّا نَأْتِي الْأَرْضَ نَنْقُصُها مِنْ أَطْرافِها), commentators have various discussions.

[1]: "Yushabun" (يصحبون) from the form of If'al originally means to place something at someone's disposal for help and protection. Here it indicates that these idols neither possess the power of defense inherently, nor has such power been granted to them by the Lord. We know that any defensive power in the universe must either spring from the essence of a thing or be granted by Allah.

  1. Some have said it means that Allah gradually diminishes the lands and territories of the polytheists and adds them to the lands of the Muslims.

However, considering that this Surah was revealed in Makkah and at that time the Muslims did not have such conquests, this interpretation does not seem appropriate.

  1. Others have said: It refers to the gradual ruin and destruction of lands.

  2. Some consider it a reference to the inhabitants of the earth.

  3. And some have specifically mentioned scholars and the learned here.

However, the most appropriate view is that "the land" refers to the people of various regions of the world—the diverse nations and individuals who gradually and constantly hasten toward the realm of non-existence and bid farewell to the life of this world. In this way, the earth is constantly diminished from its borders.

In some narrations transmitted from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, this verse has been interpreted as the death of scholars and the learned. Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says:

"Its diminution is the passing of its scholars" [1].

Of course, we know that such narrations are usually intended to explain the most prominent examples, not to restrict the concept of the verse to specific individuals.

Thus, the verse intends to present the gradual death of the greats, populous nations, and even scholars as a lesson for the arrogant and oblivious disbelievers, showing them that in the struggle against Allah, there is no victory for them.

Then it recounts the reality that the duty of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is to warn the people through Divine revelation. Addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it says:

"Say, 'I only warn you by the revelation'" (قُلْ إِنَّما أُنْذِرُكُمْ بِالْوَحْيِ).

And if it does not affect your hardened hearts, it is no surprise, nor is it evidence of any deficiency in the Divine revelation; rather, it is because "the deaf do not hear the call when they are warned!" (وَ لا يَسْمَعُ الصُّمُّ الدُّعاءَ إِذا ما يُنْذَرُونَ).

A hearing ear is required to hear the word of Allah, not ears upon which such veils of sin, negligence, and arrogance have fallen that they have completely lost the ability to hear the Truth.

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 429.

Ayahs

وَلَئِن مَّسَّتْهُمْ نَفْحَةٌ مِّنْ عَذَابِ رَبِّكَ لَيَقُولُنَّ يَـٰوَيْلَنَآ إِنَّا كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ (٤٦)
Should a whiff of your Lord’s punishment touch them, they will surely say, ‘Woe to us! We have indeed been wrongdoers!’ (46)
وَنَضَعُ ٱلْمَوَٰزِينَ ٱلْقِسْطَ لِيَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فَلَا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَإِن كَانَ مِثْقَالَ حَبَّةٍ مِّنْ خَرْدَلٍ أَتَيْنَا بِهَا ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِنَا حَـٰسِبِينَ (٤٧)
We shall set up just scales on the Day of Resurrection, and no soul will be wronged in the least. Even if it be the weight of a mustard seed We shall produce it and We suffice as reckoners. (47)

Commentary

The Scales of Justice on the Day of Resurrection

Following the previous verses which reflected the state of arrogance and heedlessness of the faithless individuals, the first verse under discussion says: "These arrogant, heedless ones, who never serve Allah while in a state of blessing and tranquility—if even a small and insignificant portion of Your Lord’s punishment were to touch them, they would become so terrified that they would cry out: 'Woe to us! We were indeed wrongdoers!'" (وَلَئِنْ مَسَّتْهُمْ نَفْحَةٌ مِنْ عَذابِ رَبِّكَ لَيَقُولُنَّ يا وَيْلَنا إِنَّا كُنَّا ظالِمِينَ).

According to commentators and lexicographers, the word nafḥah (نفحة) means something small or a gentle breeze. Although this word is often used for the breezes of mercy and blessing, it is also used in the context of punishment [^1]. According to the commentary Al-Kashshaf, the phrase "لَئِنْ مَسَّتْهُمْ نَفْحَةٌ..." contains three expressions that all point to insignificance and smallness: the use of mass (touching), the use of nafḥah (in terms of its linguistic root), and also its weight and morphological form [^2]. In short, the Quran intends to say: these blind-hearted people hear the words of the Prophet and the logic of revelation for many years, and it has the least effect on them, until the whip of punishment—however light and brief—is struck upon their backs. Only then do they lose their composure and raise the cry of "إِنَّا كُنَّا ظالِمِينَ" (Indeed, we were wrongdoers). Must they only wake up under the whips of punishment?

But what use is it? This forced awakening does them no good, and if the storm of punishment subsides and they find tranquility, they return to the same path and the same program.

The last verse under discussion refers to the precise and just accounting and recompense of the Day of Resurrection, so that the faithless and oppressive individuals know that even if the punishment of this world does not seize them, the punishment of the Hereafter is certain, and all their deeds will be precisely calculated. It says: "We shall set up the scales of justice for the Day of Resurrection" (وَ نَضَعُ الْمَوازِينَ الْقِسْطَ لِيَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ).

Qisṭ (قسط) sometimes means lack of discrimination, and sometimes it means justice in an absolute sense, and the second meaning is appropriate here. Interestingly, qisṭ is mentioned here as an "adjective" for mawāzīn (scales).

[^1] and [^2]: Tafsir Fakhr Razi, Tafsir Fi Zilal, and Mufradat al-Raghib, under the verse discussed, and the root n-f-ḥ.

These measuring scales are so precise and orderly that they are as if they are justice itself [^1]. For this reason, it immediately adds: "So no soul shall be wronged in the least" (فَلا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْئاً).

Neither will anything be diminished from the reward of the doers of good, nor will anything be added to the punishment of the evildoers.

However, this negation of wrongdoing does not mean that there is no precision in the accounting; rather, "even if there be the weight of a mustard seed of a good or evil deed, We shall bring it forth" and subject it to measurement (وَ إِنْ كانَ مِثْقالَ حَبَّةٍ مِنْ خَرْدَلٍ أَتَيْنا بِها).

"And sufficient are We as accountants of the deeds of the servants" (وَ كَفى‌ بِنا حاسِبِينَ‌).

Khardal (mustard) is a plant that has very small black seeds, and it is a proverb for smallness and insignificance. A similar expression appears elsewhere in the Quran as "مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ" (the weight of an atom/speck)—the weight of a very small ant or a tiny particle of dust and grime (Surah Az-Zalzalah, verse 7).

It is noteworthy that in the Holy Quran, the expression "مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ" is used in six places, and the expression "مِثْقالَ حَبَّةٍ مِنْ خَرْدَلٍ‌" is used in two places.

In reality, in the above verse, through six different expressions, emphasis is placed on the issue of the precise accounting and reckoning of the Day of Resurrection. The word mawāzīn (scales) in its plural form, then the mention of the description qisṭ (justice), followed by the emphasis on the negation of wrongdoing "فَلا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ‌", then the mention of the word shay’an (anything/in the least), then the example of the "mustard seed," and finally the sentence "كَفى‌ بِنا حاسِبِينَ‌" (Sufficient are We as accountants).

All of these are evidence that the accounting of the Day of Resurrection is extraordinarily precise and free from any kind of injustice or oppression.

Regarding what is meant by the "measuring scale," some have imagined that scales like the scales of this world will be set up there, and consequently assumed that human deeds there possess heaviness and weight so as to be weighable with those scales.

However, the truth is that mīzān (scale) here means "means of measurement." We know that everything has a means of measurement suited to itself: a thermometer (mīzān al-ḥarārah), a barometer (mīzān al-hawā), and other scales are each harmonious with the subject they wish to measure.

In Islamic hadiths, we read that the scales of measurement on the Day of Resurrection are the Prophets, the Imams, and the pure and righteous ones in whose record of deeds there is no dark spot [^1]. We read: "Peace be upon the scale of deeds" (As-salāmu ‘alā mīzān al-a‘māl). (We have provided more explanation and detail on this subject in Volume 6, page 90 onwards).

The mention of mawāzīn in the "plural" form (plural of mīzān) may also refer to this same meaning, because the men of truth are each a scale of measurement for the deeds of humans. Furthermore, although they are all excellent, each of them possesses a specific excellence such that in that particular aspect, they are considered the model and the standard of measurement.

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 7, p. 252 (New Edition).

In other words, to the extent that a person resembles them and is harmonious with the attributes and deeds of these noble ones, their weight will be heavy; and in whatever proportion they are distant and discordant, they will be light in weight.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ ٱلْفُرْقَانَ وَضِيَآءً وَذِكْرًا لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ (٤٨)
Certainly We gave Moses and Aaron the Criterion, a light and reminder for the Godwary (48)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُم بِٱلْغَيْبِ وَهُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّاعَةِ مُشْفِقُونَ (٤٩)
—those who fear their Lord in secret, and who are apprehensive of the Hour. (49)
وَهَـٰذَا ذِكْرٌ مُّبَارَكٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ ۚ أَفَأَنتُمْ لَهُۥ مُنكِرُونَ (٥٠)
This [too] is a blessed reminder, which We have sent down. Will you then deny it? (50)

Commentary

A Glimpse into the Stories of the Prophets

From these verses onward, segments of the lives of the Prophets are presented, which are intertwined with highly instructive points. These accounts clarify the previous discussions regarding the prophethood of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and his struggle against opponents, by highlighting the common principles that govern them.

In the first verse, He says: "We gave Moses and Aaron the 'Furqan' (the criterion for distinguishing truth from falsehood), and a light and a reminder for the God-fearing" (وَ لَقَدْ آتَيْنا مُوسى‌ وَ هارُونَ الْفُرْقانَ وَ ضِياءً وَ ذِكْراً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ‌).

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 13; p. 423

"Furqan" originally means something that separates truth from falsehood and is a means of identifying the two. Regarding what is meant by it here, several interpretations have been mentioned:

Some have interpreted it as the Torah.

Others have considered it to be the parting of the sea for the Children of Israel, which was a clear sign of the greatness of Truth and the legitimacy of Moses.

Meanwhile, some believe it refers to other miracles and proofs that were at the disposal of Moses and Aaron.

However, these interpretations are by no means contradictory, as "Furqan" could refer to both the "Torah" and the "other miracles and proofs" of Moses عليه السلام.

In other verses, "Furqan" is sometimes applied to the "Quran" itself, such as: تَبَارَكَ الَّذِي نَزَّلَ الْفُرْقَانَ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِ لِيَكُونَ لِلْعَالَمِينَ نَذِيرًا: "Blessed is He who sent down the Criterion upon His Servant that he may be to the worlds a warner" (Surah Al-Furqan, Verse 1).

And sometimes it refers to the miraculous victories granted to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, just as He said regarding the Battle of Badr: "يَوْمَ الْفُرْقانِ‌" (The Day of the Criterion) (Anfal, 41).

As for the word "Diya" (ضياء), it means light and radiance that emanates from the essence of a thing, and certainly, the Quran, the Torah, and the miracles of the Prophets were such [^1]. "Dhikr" (ذکر) refers to any subject that keeps a person away from negligence and ignorance, and this too is among the clear effects of the Heavenly Books and divine miracles.

The mention of these three terms in succession seemingly indicates that to reach the destination, a person first needs "Furqan"—meaning that at crossroads and junctions, he must find the main path. Once he finds his way, he requires light and radiance (Diya) to begin moving. Then, as he continues the path, obstacles sometimes arise—the most important of which is negligence—requiring a means that constantly warns him and serves as a reminder (Dhikr).

It is noteworthy that "Furqan" appears in the "definite" form (with 'al'), while "Diya" and "Dhikr" appear in the "indefinite" form, and He specifies their effect for the "Muttaqin" (the God-fearing). This difference in expression may indicate that miracles and heavenly addresses illuminate the path for everyone, but those who decide to utilize the light and the reminder are not everyone; they are only those who feel a sense of responsibility and possess a degree of piety (Taqwa).

The next verse introduces these "God-fearing" people as follows: "They are those who fear their Lord in the unseen" (الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَيْبِ‌). "And they are apprehensive of the Hour [of Resurrection]" (وَ هُمْ مِنَ السَّاعَةِ مُشْفِقُونَ‌).

The word "Ghayb" (unseen) here has two interpretations: First, it refers to the Pure Essence of the Lord; meaning that although God is hidden from sight, they have believed in Him through reason and feel responsible before His Pure Essence.

Second, the God-fearing do not fear God only in public and among society, but they also consider Him present and watchful in their private moments.

It is noteworthy that regarding Allah, the term "Khashyat" (خشیت) is used, while regarding the Resurrection, the term "Ishfaq" (اشفاق) is used. Although both words mean fear, according to Raghib in the book Al-Mufradat, "Khashyat" is used when fear is mixed with respect and veneration, similar to the fear a child has for an honorable father. Therefore, the fear the God-fearing have for God is coupled with knowledge (Ma'rifah).

As for the word "Ishfaq," it means attention and affection coupled with apprehension. For example, this term is sometimes used regarding children or friends for whom a person has affection, yet at the same time fears they may be exposed to harm or injury.

In reality, the God-fearing are very fond of the Day of Resurrection because it is the center of divine reward and mercy, yet they are nonetheless apprehensive of God's reckoning.

Of course, sometimes these two words are used interchangeably.

The final verse under discussion places the Quran in comparison with previous books, saying: "And this is a blessed reminder which We have sent down" (وَ هذا ذِكْرٌ مُبارَكٌ أَنْزَلْناهُ‌). "Will you then deny it?" (أَ فَأَنْتُمْ لَهُ مُنْكِرُونَ‌).

Why denial? This is a reminder and a source of awakening, awareness, and recollection for you. It is a center of blessing, containing the good of this world and the hereafter, and it is the source of all victories and happiness.

Does such a book deserve denial? The proofs of its truthfulness are inherent within it, its radiance is manifest, and those who walk its path are prosperous and victorious.

To understand to what extent this Quran is a source of awareness and blessing, it is enough to consider the state of the inhabitants of the Arabian Peninsula before the revelation of the Quran—who lived in savagery, ignorance, poverty, misery, and fragmentation—and compare it with their condition after the revelation of the Quran, when they became a model and example for others. Likewise, consider the state of other nations before and after the entry of the Quran among them.

[^1]: Regarding the meaning of "Diya" and its difference from "Nur," we have provided more detailed explanations in Volume 8, page 226, under Verse 5 of Surah Yunus.

Ayahs

۞ وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَآ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ رُشْدَهُۥ مِن قَبْلُ وَكُنَّا بِهِۦ عَـٰلِمِينَ (٥١)
Certainly We had given Abraham his probity before, and We knew him (51)
إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِۦ مَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلتَّمَاثِيلُ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنتُمْ لَهَا عَـٰكِفُونَ (٥٢)
when he said to his father and his people, ‘What are these images to which you keep on clinging?’ (52)
قَالُوا۟ وَجَدْنَآ ءَابَآءَنَا لَهَا عَـٰبِدِينَ (٥٣)
They said, ‘We found our fathers worshipping them.’ (53)
قَالَ لَقَدْ كُنتُمْ أَنتُمْ وَءَابَآؤُكُمْ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٥٤)
He said, ‘Certainly you and your fathers have been in manifest error.’ (54)
قَالُوٓا۟ أَجِئْتَنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ أَمْ أَنتَ مِنَ ٱللَّـٰعِبِينَ (٥٥)
They said, ‘Are you telling the truth, or are you [just] kidding?’ (55)
قَالَ بَل رَّبُّكُمْ رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلَّذِى فَطَرَهُنَّ وَأَنَا۠ عَلَىٰ ذَٰلِكُم مِّنَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ (٥٦)
He said, ‘Indeed, your Lord is the Lord of the heavens and the earth, who originated them, and I affirm that. (56)
وَتَٱللَّهِ لَأَكِيدَنَّ أَصْنَـٰمَكُم بَعْدَ أَن تُوَلُّوا۟ مُدْبِرِينَ (٥٧)
By Allah, I will devise a stratagem against your idols after you have gone away.’ (57)
فَجَعَلَهُمْ جُذَٰذًا إِلَّا كَبِيرًا لَّهُمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ إِلَيْهِ يَرْجِعُونَ (٥٨)
So he broke them into pieces—all except the biggest of them—so that they might come back to it. (58)

Commentary

Abraham Plots the Destruction of the Idols

We have stated that in this Surah, as is evident from its name, many episodes from the lives of the Prophets (sixteen Prophets) are mentioned. In the preceding verses, a brief reference was made to the mission of Musa (peace be upon him) and Harun (peace be upon him). In the verses under discussion and a portion of the upcoming verses, a significant part of the life and struggles of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) against idolaters is reflected. First, He says: "We had already given Ibrahim his integrity and guidance before, and We were well aware of him" (وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا إِبْرَاهِيمَ رُشْدَهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَكُنَّا بِهِ عَالِمِينَ).

"Rushd" (integrity/guidance) originally means finding the way to the destination. Here, it may refer to the reality of Monotheism (Tawhid) which Ibrahim became aware of from childhood, or it could refer to every kind of goodness and righteousness in the broad sense of the word.

The expression "min qablu" (before) refers to the time before Musa and Harun.

The phrase "We were well aware of him" (وَكُنَّا بِهِ عَالِمِينَ) refers to Ibrahim's worthiness to receive these blessings. In reality, Allah does not grant any blessing to anyone without a reason; these merits constitute the readiness to receive Divine blessings, even though the station of Prophethood is a gifted station.

Then, referring to one of the most important programs of Ibrahim (peace be upon him), it says: This growth and courage of Ibrahim became apparent when he said to his father (referring to his uncle "Azar," as Arabs sometimes refer to an uncle as "Ab" [father]) and his people: "What are these statues to which you are so devoted, circling them day and night and refusing to leave them?" (إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِ مَا هَذِهِ التَّمَاثِيلُ الَّتِي أَنْتُمْ لَهَا عَاكِفُونَ).

With this expression, Ibrahim severely belittled the idols that held extraordinary greatness in their eyes. Firstly, with the expression "Ma hadhihi" (What are these?) [1]. Secondly, with the term "Tamathil," as "Tamathil" is the plural of "Timthal," meaning a soulless image or statue. (The history of idol worship suggests that these statues and images initially served as memorials for Prophets and scholars, but gradually took on a sacred form and became objects of worship).

The phrase "Antum laha 'akifun" (to which you are devoted), considering the meaning of "Ukuf"—which implies constant presence coupled with respect—shows that they had become so attached to these idols, rubbing their foreheads on their thresholds and circling them, as if they were perpetually inseparable from them.

[1]: The expression "Ma" (What) in such cases usually refers to non-rational beings, and the demonstrative pronoun for "near" (hadhihi) in such instances also conveys a type of belittlement; otherwise, a demonstrative for "far" would have been more appropriate.

This statement of Ibrahim is, in fact, a clear argument for the invalidity of idol worship. For what we see of the idols are merely these statues and images; the rest is imagination, illusion, and fancy. What rational person allows himself to attribute such greatness, respect, and power to a handful of stone and wood? Why should a human being, who is the noblest of creatures, show such humility and prostration before his own handiwork and seek the solution to his problems from it?!

However, the idolaters actually had no answer to this eloquent logic except to deflect the matter from themselves and link it to their ancestors. Therefore, "They said: We found our fathers and ancestors worshipping them, and we are loyal to the tradition of our ancestors" (قَالُوا وَجَدْنَا آبَاءَنَا لَهَا عَابِدِينَ).

Since merely being the tradition and way of ancestors solves no problem, and we have no evidence that ancestors were wiser or more knowledgeable than subsequent generations—in fact, the case is often the opposite as science and knowledge expand over time—Ibrahim immediately "answered them: Certainly you and your fathers have been in manifest error" (قَالَ لَقَدْ كُنْتُمْ أَنْتُمْ وَآبَاؤُكُمْ فِي ضَلَالٍ مُبِينٍ).

This expression, which is accompanied by various forms of emphasis and reflects total decisiveness, caused the idolaters to come to their senses a little and seek investigation. Turning toward Ibrahim, "They said: Have you brought us the truth, or are you one of those who jest?" (قَالُوا أَجِئْتَنَا بِالْحَقِّ أَمْ أَنْتَ مِنَ اللَّاعِبِينَ). Because they were accustomed to worshipping idols and considered it a definitive reality, they could not believe that someone would seriously oppose idol worship. Thus, they asked Ibrahim this question out of astonishment.

But Ibrahim answered them explicitly: "What I say is serious and the very reality, that your Lord is the Lord of the heavens and the earth" (قَالَ بَلْ رَبُّكُمْ رَبُّ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ). The same God Who created them, and I am among the witnesses to this belief (الَّذِي فَطَرَهُنَّ وَأَنَا عَلَى ذَلِكُمْ مِنَ الشَّاهِدِينَ).

With this decisive statement, Ibrahim showed that the One worthy of worship is the Creator of them, the earth, and all beings. However, pieces of stone and wood, which are themselves insignificant creatures, are not worth worshipping. Especially with the phrase "And I am among the witnesses to this," he proved that I am not the only one who bears witness to this truth; rather, all those who are aware and understanding—those who have broken the chains of blind imitation—bear witness to this reality.

To prove that this matter is one hundred percent serious and that he stands by his belief to the very end, accepting its consequences and requirements with all his soul, Ibrahim added: "By Allah, I will surely plot against your idols after you have turned your backs and gone away!" (وَتَاللَّهِ لَأَكِيدَنَّ أَصْنَاَمكُمْ بَعْدَ أَنْ تُوَلُّوا مُدْبِرِينَ). "Akidanna" is derived from "Kayd," which means a secret plan and hidden remedy. His intention was to make them understand clearly that he would eventually seize an opportunity and shatter them!

However, the greatness and awe of the idols in their eyes were perhaps such that they did not take this speech seriously and showed no reaction. Perhaps they thought: how could a human allow himself to play like this with the sanctities of a people and nation whose government also fully supports them? With what courage? And with what power?

From here it becomes clear that what some have said—that he said this sentence in his heart or shared it privately with some—is in no way necessary, especially since it is completely contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse. Furthermore, in a few verses later, we read that the idolaters remembered this statement of Ibrahim and said: "We heard a youth speaking [ill] of the idols."

In any case, on a day when the temple was empty and none of the idolaters were present, Ibrahim carried out his plan. To explain: according to the reports of some commentators, the idolaters celebrated a specific day each year as a festival for the idols. They would prepare food in the temple, then move together outside the city, and at the end of the day, they would return and come to the temple to eat from that food which they believed had been blessed.

They suggested to Ibrahim that he go with them, but he did not go with them on the pretext of illness. In any case, without fearing the dangers of this act or harboring dread in his heart for the storm that would follow this deed, he entered the field manfully. With a world of heroism, he went to war against these hollow gods who had so many fanatical and ignorant devotees, such that the Quran says: "He reduced them to fragments, except for a large one they had!" (فَجَعَلَهُمْ جُذَاذًا إِلَّا كَبِيرًا لَهُمْ).

And his goal was "that perhaps the idolaters would return to it [or him] and he would say all that needed to be said" (لَعَلَّهُمْ إِلَيْهِ يَرْجِعُونَ) [1].

Key Points

1- Idol Worship in Various Forms It is true that from the term "idol worship" we mostly think of stone and wooden idols, but from one perspective, idol and idol worship have a broad meaning that includes any kind of attention to other than God, in any shape or form. According to the famous Hadith: "Whatever occupies you and keeps you away from Allah is your idol!" (كلما شغلك عن الله فهو صنمك). In a Hadith from "Asbagh ibn Nubata," who is one of the famous companions of Ali (peace be upon him), we read:

"Ameer al-Mu'minin Ali (peace be upon him) passed by a group of people playing chess and said: 'What are these statues (and idols) to which you are devoted? You have disobeyed Allah and His Messenger!'" [2]

2- The Speech of the Idolaters and Ibrahim's Response Interestingly, in response to Ibrahim, the idolaters relied on both the large number of people and the length of time, saying: "We found our fathers following this religion and custom."

[1]: Many commentators have said the pronoun "ilayhi" (to it/him) refers to Ibrahim himself, and some have said it refers to the large idol, but the first meaning seems more correct. As for the verse mentioning this was their "large" one, it may refer to its physical size, or its respect in the eyes of the superstitious idolaters, or both.

[2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed.

He answered them in both parts: that both you and your fathers were always in manifest error! That is, a rational person who possesses intellectual independence never binds himself to these delusions; he considers neither the large number of supporters of a plan or tradition as proof of its authenticity, nor its duration and deep-rootedness.

Ayahs

قَالُوا۟ مَن فَعَلَ هَـٰذَا بِـَٔالِهَتِنَآ إِنَّهُۥ لَمِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٥٩)
They said, ‘Whoever has done this to Our gods?! He is indeed a wrongdoer!’ (59)
قَالُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا فَتًى يَذْكُرُهُمْ يُقَالُ لَهُۥٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمُ (٦٠)
They said, ‘We heard a young man mentioning them. He is called ‘‘Abraham.’’ ’ (60)
قَالُوا۟ فَأْتُوا۟ بِهِۦ عَلَىٰٓ أَعْيُنِ ٱلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَشْهَدُونَ (٦١)
They said, ‘Bring him before the people’s eyes so that they may bear witness [against him].’ (61)
قَالُوٓا۟ ءَأَنتَ فَعَلْتَ هَـٰذَا بِـَٔالِهَتِنَا يَـٰٓإِبْرَٰهِيمُ (٦٢)
They said, ‘Was it you who did this to our gods, O Abraham?’ (62)
قَالَ بَلْ فَعَلَهُۥ كَبِيرُهُمْ هَـٰذَا فَسْـَٔلُوهُمْ إِن كَانُوا۟ يَنطِقُونَ (٦٣)
He said, ‘No, it was this biggest one of them who did it! Ask them, if they can speak.’ (63)
فَرَجَعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ فَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّكُمْ أَنتُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٦٤)
Thereat they came to themselves and said [to one another], ‘Indeed it is you who are the wrongdoers!’ (64)
ثُمَّ نُكِسُوا۟ عَلَىٰ رُءُوسِهِمْ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ مَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ يَنطِقُونَ (٦٥)
Then they hung their heads. [However, they said,] ‘You certainly know that they cannot speak.’ (65)
قَالَ أَفَتَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَنفَعُكُمْ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَضُرُّكُمْ (٦٦)
He said, ‘Then, do you worship besides Allah that which cannot cause you any benefit or harm? (66)
أُفٍّ لَّكُمْ وَلِمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (٦٧)
Fie on you and what you worship besides Allah! Do you not exercise your reason?’ (67)

Commentary

Abraham’s Crushing Argument

Finally, that day of festival came to an end, and the idol-worshippers returned to the city in celebration. They went straight to the temple of idols, both to pay their respects to the idols and to eat from the food which, according to their belief, had become blessed by being placed beside them.

As soon as they entered the temple, they were faced with a scene that made them lose their wits. Instead of the flourishing temple, they were met with a mound of broken and scattered idols with shattered limbs! Their cries rose up; they called out: "Who has done this to our gods?" (قالُوا مَنْ فَعَلَ هذا بِآلِهَتِنا) [^1]. Surely, whoever it was, is among the wrongdoers and oppressors (إِنَّهُ لَمِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ‌).

He has wronged our gods, wronged our society and community, and wronged himself! For by this act, he has exposed himself to destruction.

However, a group who remembered Abraham’s threats against the idols and knew his insulting behavior toward these fabricated deities said: "We heard a youth speaking of the idols and mentioning them with ill-intent, whose name is Abraham" (قالُوا سَمِعْنا فَتًى يَذْكُرُهُمْ يُقالُ لَهُ إِبْراهِيمُ‌) [^2]. It is true that Abraham, according to some narrations, was quite young at that time—likely his age did not exceed 16 years—and it is true that all the characteristics of "chivalry," "courage," "bravery," "frankness," and "decisiveness" were gathered in his being. Yet, the idol-worshippers certainly intended nothing but humiliation by this expression. Instead of saying "Abraham did this," they said "a youth who they called Abraham used to say such things..." meaning someone completely obscure and, in their view, of no significance.

In principle, it is customary that when a crime occurs in a place, people look for hostile connections to find the person who committed the act. Certainly, in that environment, no one but Abraham was openly clashing with the idols.

[^1]: Some commentators have considered the word "man" (who) here as a relative pronoun, but considering the next verse which serves as an answer to the question, it appears that "man" here is interrogative.

[^2]: As we previously pointed out, the idol-worshippers were not even willing to say that this youth was criticizing the idols; they only said he "spoke of" them.

Consequently, all thoughts turned toward him. The crowd said: "Now that it is so, go and bring him before the eyes of the people so that those who know and have information may bear witness" (قالُوا فَأْتُوا بِهِ عَلى‌ أَعْيُنِ النَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَشْهَدُونَ‌).

Some commentators have also suggested the possibility that the intention was for people to witness the scene of Abraham’s punishment and retribution, not to testify to his guilt. However, considering the subsequent verses which have more of an interrogatory nature, this possibility is dismissed. Furthermore, the use of the word "la'alla" (perhaps) is not consistent with the second meaning, because if the people are present at the scene of punishment, they will naturally witness it; there is no "perhaps" about it.

Criers shouted throughout the city that anyone aware of the story of Abraham’s hostility and his speaking ill of the idols should come forward. Soon, both those aware of the matter and the rest of the people gathered to see what the end of this accused person would be.

A strange commotion and stir had fallen upon the people, for in their view, an unprecedented crime had been committed by an adventurous youth in the city, which had shaken the religious foundations of the local people.

Finally, the tribunal and court were formed, and the leaders of the people gathered there. Some say that Nimrod himself was also overseeing this event.

The first question they asked Abraham was: "They said: Is it you who has done this to our gods, O Abraham?" (قالُوا أَ أَنْتَ فَعَلْتَ هذا بِآلِهَتِنا يا إِبْراهِيمُ‌).

They were not even willing to say "you have broken our gods" or "cut them into pieces," but only said: "You did this to our gods?"

Abraham gave such an answer that it placed them in a severe predicament—a predicament from which they had no power to escape. "Abraham said: Rather, this big one of them has done it! So ask them, if they can speak!" (قالَ بَلْ فَعَلَهُ كَبِيرُهُمْ هذا فَسْئَلُوهُمْ إِنْ كانُوا يَنْطِقُونَ‌).

The principles of criminology state that the suspect is the one who carries the traces of the crime; here, the traces of the crime are in the hands of the big idol (according to a famous narration, Abraham placed the axe on the neck of the big idol).

Why did you come to me at all? Why do you not accuse your great god?

Do you not consider the possibility that he became angry with the small gods, or perhaps he considered them his future rivals and settled the account with all of them at once?! Since the literal meaning of this expression did not seem to align with reality to the commentators, and since Abraham is a Prophet and infallible (Ma'sum) and never tells a lie, they have offered various explanations for this sentence. What seems best of all is this:

Abraham عليه السلام definitely attributed this act to the big idol, but all the evidence testified that he had no serious intention with this statement. Rather, he wanted to throw the established beliefs of the idol-worshippers—which were superstitious and baseless—back in their faces. He wanted to make them understand that these lifeless stones and pieces of wood are so incompetent that they cannot even utter a single sentence or seek help from their worshippers, let alone solve their problems! Similar expressions are abundant in our daily speech where, to invalidate the opponent's argument, we present their certainties to them in the form of a command, a report, or a question so that they may be defeated, and this is by no means a lie. "A lie is that which is not accompanied by clarifying evidence."

In a narration cited in the book Al-Kafi from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"Abraham said 'Rather, this big one of them has done it' intending reform, and as a proof that they do not act. Then the Imam added: By Allah, the idols had not done such a thing, and Abraham did not lie."

A group of commentators has also suggested that Abraham عليه السلام stated this in the form of a conditional sentence, saying: "The idols, if they speak, have done such a thing." Certainly, this expression was not contrary to reality, because neither did the idols speak, nor had such an act originated from them. A hadith has also been narrated with the same meaning as this interpretation.

However, the first interpretation seems more correct because the conditional clause (إِنْ كانُوا يَنْطِقُونَ‌) is a qualification for the act of asking (فاسئلوهم), not for the sentence "Rather, this big one of them has done it" (Observe closely).

Another point to be noted here is that the phrasing implies that the broken idols should be asked who brought this calamity upon them, not the big idol, because the pronoun "hum" (them) and also the pronouns in "إِنْ كانُوا يَنْطِقُونَ‌" are all in the plural form, and this is consistent with the first interpretation [^1].

Abraham’s words shook the idol-worshippers and awakened their dormant consciences. Like a storm that blows away thick layers of ash from over the flames of a fire to reveal its glow, it revealed their monotheistic nature (Fitrah) from behind the veils of prejudice, ignorance, and pride.

[^1]: Furthermore, it appears that the pronoun for "kabiruhum" (their big one) is the same as the rest.

For a short and fleeting moment, they woke up from this deep and deadly sleep, as the Quran says: "They returned to their own selves and said: Indeed, it is you who are the wrongdoers" (فَرَجَعُوا إِلى‌ أَنْفُسِهِمْ فَقالُوا إِنَّكُمْ أَنْتُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ‌) [^1]. You have wronged yourselves, the society to which you belong, and the sacred presence of the Lord, the Bestower of blessings.

It is interesting that in the previous verses, we read that they accused Abraham of being a wrongdoer, but here they realized that the original and true wrongdoers were themselves.

In fact, this was Abraham’s entire purpose in breaking the idols; the goal was to break the thought of idol-worship and the spirit of idol-worship. Otherwise, breaking an idol is of no use; stubborn idol-worshippers would immediately build a larger and more numerous one and put it in its place, as there are many examples of this in the history of ignorant and prejudiced nations.

Up to this point, Abraham succeeded in implementing a very sensitive and delicate stage of his mission, which is awakening dormant consciences through the creation of a psychological storm.

But alas, the rust of ignorance, prejudice, and blind imitation was too thick to be completely removed by the polishing call of this hero of monotheism.

Alas, this spiritual and sacred awakening did not last long, and in their polluted and dark minds, a rebellion was staged by the forces of the devil and ignorance against this light of monotheism, and everything returned to its original state. What a subtle expression the Quran uses: "Then they were turned upside down on their heads" (ثُمَّ نُكِسُوا عَلى‌ رُؤُسِهِمْ‌).

And to bring an excuse on behalf of their mute and closed-mouthed gods, they said:

"You well know that these do not speak!" (لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ ما هؤُلاءِ يَنْطِقُونَ‌).

These are always silent and do not break the majesty of silence!! And with this hollow excuse, they wanted to conceal the weakness, helplessness, and humiliation of the idols.

It was here that a field for logical reasoning opened up for the hero Abraham to direct his most intense attacks against them and place their brains under a barrage of logical and awakening reproach: "He cried out: Do you then worship besides Allah that which neither profits you in the least nor harms you?" (قالَ أَ فَتَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ ما لا يَنْفَعُكُمْ شَيْئاً وَ لا يَضُرُّكُمْ‌).

These imaginary gods who have neither the power of speech, nor consciousness, nor understanding, who can neither defend themselves nor call their worshippers to their aid—what can they actually do and what use are they?! The worship of a deity is either because of His worthiness for servitude—which is meaningless regarding lifeless idols—or because of the expectation of a benefit that might come from them, or fear of their harm. But my act of breaking the idols showed that they have not the slightest power; despite this, is your action not foolish?!

Again, this teacher of monotheism took the speech even further and struck their painless souls with the whips of reproach, saying: "Fie upon you, and upon these deities you have chosen besides Allah!" (أُفٍّ لَكُمْ وَ لِما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ‌).

"Do you not then reflect, and have you no sense in your heads?" (أَ فَلا تَعْقِلُونَ‌).

Yet, in rebuking and reproaching them, he did not lose his gentleness, lest they become more stubborn [^1]. In truth, Abraham followed his program very calculatedly. The first time, when inviting them to monotheism, he called out: "What are these lifeless statues that you worship? If you say it is the tradition of your ancestors, then both you and they were in error."

In the second stage, he took action with a practical program to show that these idols do not have such power that they could destroy anyone who looks at them wrongly. Specifically, with a prior warning, he went to the idols and smashed them completely to show that the fantasies they had woven together were all in vain.

In the third stage, in that historic trial, he placed them in a severe deadlock. Sometimes he appealed to their nature (Fitrah), sometimes to their intellect; sometimes he advised them, and sometimes he reproached and rebuked them.

In short, this great divine teacher entered through every door and used whatever was in his power. However, certainly, the receptivity of the recipient is also a condition for impact, and unfortunately, this was largely lacking in that people.

But without a doubt, Abraham’s words and actions remained as a monotheistic foundation and at least in the form of question marks in their minds, and became a prelude for a broader awakening and awareness in the future.

It is understood from history that a group—though small in number but great in value—believed in him [^2], and a relative readiness was prepared for another group.

[^1]: Regarding the meaning of "Uff" (Fie), we have discussed it in detail in Volume 13 under verse 23 of Surah Al-Isra.

[^2]: Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh by Ibn al-Athir, Volume 1, page 100.

Ayahs

قَالُوا۟ حَرِّقُوهُ وَٱنصُرُوٓا۟ ءَالِهَتَكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ فَـٰعِلِينَ (٦٨)
They said, ‘Burn him, and help your gods, if you are to do anything!’ (68)
قُلْنَا يَـٰنَارُ كُونِى بَرْدًا وَسَلَـٰمًا عَلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ (٦٩)
We said, ‘O fire! Be cool and safe for Abraham!’ (69)
وَأَرَادُوا۟ بِهِۦ كَيْدًا فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْأَخْسَرِينَ (٧٠)
They plotted to harm him, but We made them the biggest losers. (70)

Commentary

Where Fire Becomes a Garden

Although all the idolaters were defeated by Ibrahim’s practical and logical arguments, and they internally confessed to this defeat, their stubbornness and intense prejudice prevented them from accepting the truth. For this reason, it is not surprising that they made a very severe and dangerous decision regarding Ibrahim, which was to kill him in the worst possible manner—by burning him to ashes!

Usually, there is an inverse relationship between "force" and "logic"; the more a person's force increases, the weaker their logic becomes, except in men of truth who become more humble and logical as they grow stronger.

When tyrants fail to achieve their goals through logic, they immediately rely on their force and power. In the case of Ibrahim, exactly this plan was utilized, as the Quran says: "They said: Burn him and help your gods, if you are to do [anything]" (قالُوا حَرِّقُوهُ وَ انْصُرُوا آلِهَتَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ فاعِلِينَ‌).

To incite the ignorant masses, oppressive rulers usually exploit their psychological weaknesses, for they are psychologists and masters of their craft! Just as they did in this incident, they shouted slogans intended to strike the "vein of zeal" (honor), saying: "These are your gods; your sanctities are in danger; the traditions of your ancestors are being trampled upon; where is your zeal and sense of honor? Why are you so weak and abject? Why do you not help your gods? Burn Ibrahim and help your gods if you are capable of doing anything and have strength in your bodies and power in your souls."

"See how all people defend their sanctities, while you, whose everything is at risk, [do nothing]."

In short, they spoke much of such nonsense and incited the people against Ibrahim to such an extent that instead of a few loads of firewood—which would have sufficed to burn several people—they piled up thousands of loads, creating a mountain of wood followed by a sea of fire. They did this to take better revenge and to restore the perceived awe and greatness of the idols, which had been severely damaged by Ibrahim’s actions. Historians have written many details here that do not seem at all unlikely:

Among them, it is said that for forty days, the people labored to collect firewood, gathering vast amounts of dry wood from every direction. It reached a point where even women whose work was spinning wool at home used their earnings to buy a bundle of wood to add to the pile. Terminally ill patients would bequeath a sum of money from their wealth to purchase firewood, and those with needs would make vows that if their desires were fulfilled, they would add a certain amount of wood to the pile.

Consequently, when they set fire to the wood from various sides, the flames were so immense that birds were unable to fly over that area.

Naturally, it was impossible to approach such a vast fire, let alone throw Ibrahim into it. Thus, they were forced to use a "catapult." They placed Ibrahim upon it and, with a swift movement, launched him into that sea of fire. [1] In narrations reported through both Shi'a and Sunni chains, we read: When they placed Ibrahim on the catapult and intended to throw him into the fire, the heavens, the earth, and the angels cried out, pleading before Allah to protect this hero of Monotheism and leader of free men.

It is also narrated that Jibra'il came to meet Ibrahim and asked him: "A laka hajah?" (Do you have any need so that I may help you?)

Ibrahim (peace be upon him) replied in a short phrase: "Amma ilayka fala" (As for you, no!) [I have need of Him who is independent of all and compassionate toward all].

At this moment, Jibra'il suggested to him: "Fas'al Rabbaka" (Then ask your need from your Lord).

And he replied: "Hasbi min su'ali 'ilmuhu bi-hali" (It is sufficient for me that He is aware of my state!) [2]

[1] Majma' al-Bayan, Tafsir al-Mizan, Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, and Tafsir al-Qurtubi under the verses in question; also Al-Kamil by Ibn al-Athir, Vol. 1, p. 98. [2] Rawdat al-Kafi as cited in Al-Mizan, Vol. 14, p. 336.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him), we read: At this moment, Ibrahim supplicated to Allah thus:

"O One, O One, O Self-Sufficient, O Self-Sufficient, O He who begets not nor is He begotten, and there is none like unto Him, I have put my trust in Allah." [1] This supplication has appeared with other wordings in other books as well.

In any case, Ibrahim (peace be upon him) was sent into the flames of the fire amidst the cheers, joy, and roars of the people. The people shouted with such joy as if the breaker of idols was destroyed and turned to ashes forever. But Allah, to whose command everything submits—He who taught the fire its burning property and taught mothers the secret of love—willed that this sincere, believing servant remain safe in this sea of fire, to add another document to his records of honor. As the Quran says here: "We said: O fire! Be coolness and safety for Ibrahim" (قُلْنا يا نارُ كُونِي بَرْداً وَ سَلاماً عَلى‌ إِبْراهِيمَ‌).

Undoubtedly, Allah's command here was a creative command (takwini), the same command He gives in the universe to the sun, the moon, the earth, the heavens, water, fire, plants, and birds.

It is well known that the fire became so cool and mild that Ibrahim’s teeth chattered from the intensity of the cold; and according to some commentators, had the expression "salama" (safety) not been used, the fire would have become so cold that Ibrahim’s life would have been endangered by the chill!

Furthermore, in a famous narration, we read that Nimrod’s fire turned into a beautiful garden. [2]

[1] Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi under the verse in question. [2] Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan under the verse in question.

Some have even said that the day Ibrahim was in the fire "was considered the calmest, best, and most comfortable day of his life." [1] In any case, there is much discussion among commentators as to how the fire did not burn Ibrahim, but the summary is that, according to the monotheistic worldview, no cause can perform any action without Allah's command. One day He says to the knife in Ibrahim’s hand: "Do not cut!" and another day He says to the fire: "Do not burn!" and another day He commands the water, which is the source of life: "Drown Pharaoh and his people!"

In the final verse under discussion, as a short and concise conclusion, He says: "And they intended a plot against him, but We made them the greatest losers" (وَ أَرادُوا بِهِ كَيْداً فَجَعَلْناهُمُ الْأَخْسَرِينَ‌).

It goes without saying that with Ibrahim remaining safe amidst the fire, the scene was completely transformed. The roars of joy subsided, mouths hung open in astonishment, and groups openly whispered to one another about this strange phenomenon. The greatness of Ibrahim and his God became the talk of the town, and the existence of Nimrod’s establishment was endangered. Yet, once again, arrogance and stubbornness prevented the full acceptance of the truth, although awakened hearts took their share from this event, and their faith in the God of Ibrahim increased, even if this group was in the minority.

Key Points

1- The Creation and Negation of Causes

Sometimes a person becomes so immersed in the world of causes that he imagines these effects and properties belong to the entities themselves, and he becomes heedless of that Great Originator who bestowed these various effects upon these beings. Here, to awaken His servants, Allah engages in "creating causes" and "negating causes."

Beings that apparently can do nothing become the source of great effects. He commands a spider to weave a few flimsy and weak threads over the entrance of the Cave of Thawr, and with these few threads, He despairs those who were searching everywhere for the Prophet of Islam and would have destroyed him had they found him. With this small means, He changed the course of world history.

Conversely, He sometimes disables causes that are proverbial in the material world (fire for burning and the knife for cutting), so it may be known that these too have nothing of their own; if the "Majestic Lord" forbids them, they cease to function, even if "Ibrahim the Friend" gives the order.

Attention to these realities—of which we have seen many examples more or less in life—revives and awakens the spirit of Monotheism (Tawhid) and Trust (Tawakkul) in the believing servant such that they do not think of [others] and do not seek help from other than Him. They seek the extinguishing of the "fire of problems" only from Him and request the destruction of the enemies' plots from His threshold; they see none but Him and desire nothing from other than Him.

2- The Heroic Youth

In some books of commentary, it is stated that Ibrahim was no more than sixteen years old when he was thrown into the fire, [1] while others mention his age at that time as twenty-six. [2]

[1] Majma' al-Bayan under the verses in question. [2] Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 6, pp. 43-44.

In any case, he was in the years of his youth, and although he apparently had no helper or supporter, he grappled with the great tyrant of his time who was the patron of other tyrants. He went alone to fight ignorance, superstition, and polytheism, making light of all the perceived sanctities of the environment, and felt not the slightest fear of the people's anger and revenge, for his heart was filled with the love of Allah and his trust and reliance were upon His Pure Essence.

Yes, such is faith; wherever it is found, it creates courage, and in whomever it exists, it is invincible!

In today's stormy world, the most important asset that Muslims must find to fight against the great diabolical powers is this very great asset.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), we read: "Indeed, the believer is firmer than pieces of iron; for pieces of iron, when they enter the fire, they change, but the believer, if he were killed, then resurrected, then killed again, his heart would not change." [1]

3- Ibrahim and Nimrod

It is recorded in histories that when they threw Ibrahim into the fire, Nimrod was certain that Ibrahim had turned into a handful of ashes. However, when he looked closely, he saw him alive. He said to those around him, "I see Ibrahim alive; perhaps I am mistaken!" He went to a high place and observed carefully, seeing that it was indeed so. Nimrod cried out, "O Ibrahim! Truly your God is great, and He has such power that He has created a barrier between you and the fire! ... Now that it is so, I wish to offer a sacrifice to Him for this power and greatness" (and he prepared four thousand sacrifices for this purpose). But Ibrahim pointed out to him that no sacrifice (or good deed) would be accepted from him unless he first believed.

However, Nimrod replied, "In that case, my kingdom and rule will be gone, and I cannot bear that!" In any case, these events caused a group of awakened and aware hearts to believe in the God of Ibrahim or increased their faith (and perhaps this very incident caused Nimrod not to show a severe reaction against Ibrahim, and to content himself only with exiling him from the land of Babylon). [1]

[1] Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 37 (under the root amn). [1] Al-Kamil by Ibn al-Athir, Vol. 1, p. 99.

Ayahs

وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ وَلُوطًا إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلَّتِى بَـٰرَكْنَا فِيهَا لِلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٧١)
We delivered him and Lot toward the land, which We have blessed for all nations. (71)
وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُۥٓ إِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ نَافِلَةً ۖ وَكُلًّا جَعَلْنَا صَـٰلِحِينَ (٧٢)
And We gave him Isaac, and Jacob as well for a grandson, and each of them We made righteous. (72)
وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنَا وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ وَإِقَامَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَإِيتَآءَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةِ ۖ وَكَانُوا۟ لَنَا عَـٰبِدِينَ (٧٣)
We made them imams, guiding by Our command, and We revealed to them [concerning] the performance of good deeds, the maintenance of prayers, and the giving of zakat, and they used to worship Us. (73)

Commentary

Abraham’s Migration from the Land of Idolaters

The story of Abraham’s ordeal by fire and his miraculous rescue from that perilous stage shook the foundations of Nimrod’s government. Nimrod completely lost his morale, as he could no longer portray Abraham as merely an adventurous, divisive youth. Abraham was now recognized as a divine leader and a courageous hero who could single-handedly challenge a tyrannical despot despite all his power and resources. Had he remained in that city and country in such a state, with his eloquent tongue, powerful logic, and unparalleled bravery, he would undoubtedly have been a focal point of danger for that tyrannical and autocratic regime. In any case, he had to leave that land.

On the other hand, Abraham had effectively fulfilled his mission in that land. He had dealt crushing blows one after another to the foundations of the government and had sown the seeds of faith and awareness in that territory. All that was needed was the factor of "time" for these seeds to gradually bear fruit and for the apparatus of idols and idol-worship to be dismantled.

He had to move from there to another land to implement his mission there as well. Therefore, he decided to migrate from that land toward Sham (Greater Syria) along with "Lot" (who was Abraham’s nephew), his wife "Sarah," and likely a small group of believers.
