As the Quran states in the verses under discussion: "And We delivered him and Lot to the land which We had blessed for the worlds" (وَ نَجَّيْناهُ وَ لُوطاً إِلَى الْأَرْضِ الَّتِي بارَكْنا فِيها لِلْعالَمِينَ).

Although the name of this land is not explicitly mentioned in this verse, considering the first verse of Surah Al-Isra (سُبْحانَ الَّذِي أَسْرى‌ بِعَبْدِهِ لَيْلًا مِنَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ إِلَى الْمَسْجِدِ الْأَقْصَى الَّذِي بارَكْنا حَوْلَهُ), it becomes clear that it is the land of Sham. This land is blessed both materially—being fertile and lush—and spiritually, as it has been a center for the nurturing of Prophets.

Regarding whether Abraham undertook this migration himself or Nimrod’s apparatus exiled him, or if both factors worked together, various discussions appear in commentaries and narrations. The synthesis of all these is that, on one hand, Nimrod and his associates saw Abraham as a great threat to themselves and forced him to leave the land. On the other hand, Abraham saw his mission in that land as nearly complete and desired another region to spread the call of monotheism, especially since staying in "Babylon" might have cost him his life and left his global mission unfinished.

Interestingly, we read in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: When Nimrod decided to exile Abraham عليه السلام from that land, he ordered that his sheep and property be confiscated and that he should leave alone.

Abraham said to them: "These are the product of many long years of my life. If you want to take my 'wealth,' then return to me the life I spent in this land!" It was agreed that one of the regime's judges should arbitrate. The judge ruled that they should take Abraham's property but return the life he had spent in that land to him! When Nimrod learned of this, he understood the true implication of the courageous judge's ruling and ordered that his property and sheep be returned to him so he could take them with him. He said: "I fear that if he stays here, he will ruin your religion and creed and harm your gods!" (انه ان بقى فى بلادكم افسد دينكم و اضر بالهتكم) [1]

[1] Rawdah al-Kafi (as cited in Al-Mizan under the verses discussed).

The next verse refers to one of Allah’s most important blessings to Abraham, which is having a righteous child and a noble, worthy progeny. It says: "And We bestowed upon him Isaac, and Jacob (Isaac’s son) as an additional gift" (وَ وَهَبْنا لَهُ إِسْحاقَ وَ يَعْقُوبَ نافِلَةً) [1] "and each of them We made righteous" (وَ كُلًّا جَعَلْنا صالِحِينَ).

Many years passed while Abraham lived in love and anticipation of a righteous child, and verse 100 of Surah As-Saffat expresses this inner desire: "My Lord, grant me [a child] from among the righteous" (رَبِّ هَبْ لِي مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ).

Finally, Allah answered his prayer, granting him first "Ishmael" and then "Isaac," both of whom were great and distinguished Prophets.

The use of the term "nafalah" (additional gift), which apparently describes only Jacob, may be because Abraham had only asked for a righteous child, but Allah added a righteous grandson as well; for "nafalah" originally means a gift or an extra deed.

The final verse under discussion refers to the station of Imamate and leadership of these great Prophets and mentions some of their important and valuable collective attributes and programs. In this verse, a total of "six parts" of these characteristics are enumerated, which, when adding the description of being righteous from the previous verse, form a total of seven characteristics. It is also possible that the set of six attributes mentioned in this verse serves as an elaboration of their being righteous mentioned in the previous verse.

First, it says: "And We made them Imams (leaders) of the people" (وَ جَعَلْناهُمْ أَئِمَّةً).

This means that in addition to the station of "Prophethood and Messengership," We also gave them the station of "Imamate." Imamate, as we have previously noted, is the final stage of human evolutionary progress, meaning comprehensive leadership—material and spiritual, outward and inward, physical and soulful—of the people.

The difference between Prophethood/Messengership and Imamate is that Prophets, in the station of Prophethood and Messengership, only receive the command of Truth, report it, and convey it to the people—a conveyance accompanied by glad tidings and warnings.

However, in the stage of "Imamate," they put these divine programs into practice, whether through the establishment of a government of justice or otherwise. at this stage, they are mentors, executors of laws and programs, nurturers of human beings, and creators of a pure, refined, and humane environment.

In reality, the station of Imamate is the station of actualizing all divine programs. In other words, it is "attaining the goal" (i-sal ila al-matlub) and both legislative and existential guidance.

In this respect, the Imam is exactly like the sun, which nurtures living beings with its rays. [1] In the next stage, it recounts the actuality and fruit of this station: "they guided by Our command" (يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا).

This is not just guidance in the sense of showing the way and providing direction—which exists in Prophethood and Messengership—but in the sense of taking one's hand and leading them to the final destination (of course, for those who possess the readiness and merit).

The third, fourth, and fifth blessings and characteristics were that: "And We revealed to them the doing of good deeds, and the establishment of prayer, and the giving of zakat" (وَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ الْخَيْراتِ وَ إِقامَ الصَّلاةِ وَ إِيتاءَ الزَّكاةِ).

This revelation could be legislative revelation, meaning We included various types of good deeds, the performance of prayer, and the giving of zakat in their religious programs. It could also be existential (takwini) revelation, meaning We granted them the success, power, and spiritual attraction to perform these matters.

Of course, none of these matters are compulsory or forced; rather, they are readinesses and foundations that would never reach a result without their own will and desire.

The mention of "establishing prayer and giving zakat" after "doing good deeds" is due to the importance of these two programs, which were first mentioned generally in the phrase "doing good deeds" and then specifically.

And in the final segment, it refers to their station of "servitude," saying: "and they were worshippers of Us alone" (وَ كانُوا لَنا عابِدِينَ). [1] Additionally, the use of "kanu" (they were), which indicates a continuous history in this program, perhaps suggests that even before reaching the station of Prophethood and Imamate, they were righteous, monotheistic, and worthy men, and in the light of these very programs, Allah granted them new blessings.

It is also necessary to mention that the phrase "they guided by Our command" is actually a means of identifying the Imams and leaders of Truth, as opposed to the leaders and pioneers of falsehood whose criterion for action is based on satanic whims.

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said: "The Imam in the Holy Quran is of two types. In one place Allah says: 'And We made them Imams who guide by Our command,' meaning by the command of Allah, not by the command of the people; they prioritize Allah's command over their own and place His judgment above their own. But in another place He says: 'And We made them Imams who invite to the Fire'—they prioritize their own command over the command of the Lord and place their own judgment before His, acting according to their whims and against the Book of Allah." [1] And this is the criterion and touchstone for identifying the Imam of Truth from the Imam of falsehood.

[1] For more explanation on this, please study Volume 1 under verse 124 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

[1] Placing the word "lana" (for Us) before "abidin" (worshippers) is evidence of restriction (hasr) and points to the station of pure monotheism of these great leaders; meaning they worshipped only Allah.

[1] The second verse, which is verse 41 of Surah Al-Qasas, refers to Pharaoh and his army. This narration is quoted in Tafsir al-Safi from the book Al-Kafi.

Ayahs

وَلُوطًا ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ ٱلَّتِى كَانَت تَّعْمَلُ ٱلْخَبَـٰٓئِثَ ۗ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمَ سَوْءٍ فَـٰسِقِينَ (٧٤)
We gave judgement and knowledge to Lot, and We delivered him from the town which used to commit vicious acts. Indeed, they were an evil and depraved lot. (74)
وَأَدْخَلْنَـٰهُ فِى رَحْمَتِنَآ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٧٥)
And We admitted him into Our mercy. Indeed, he was one of the righteous. (75)

Commentary

The Deliverance of Lot from the Land of the Defiled

Since Lot was a close relative of Abraham and among the first people to believe in him, following the story of Abraham, the Quran refers to a portion of his efforts and struggles in the path of conveying the Message and the blessings bestowed upon him by his Lord:

"And remember Lot, to whom We gave judgment and knowledge" (وَ لُوطاً آتَيْناهُ حُكْماً وَ عِلْماً) [^1]. The word "hukm" (judgment) has been used in some instances to mean the decree of prophethood and messengership, in other instances to mean the power of judgment (arbitration), and sometimes to mean intellect and wisdom. Among these meanings, the first seems more appropriate here, although there is no contradiction between them [^2].

[^1]: The accusative case (mansub) of "Lot" here is because it is the object of an omitted verb; this verb could be "We gave" (aatayna) or "Remember" (udhkur).

[^2]: We have also discussed the interpretation of the words "hukm" and "ilm" and the difference between them in Volume 9, page 360.

And the meaning of "ilm" (knowledge) is any form of knowledge that impacts human salvation and destiny.

Lot was one of the great prophets who was a contemporary of Abraham and migrated with him from the land of Babylon to Palestine. Later, he separated from Abraham and came to the city of "Sodom," as the people of that region were drowned in corruption and sin, especially sexual deviations and impurities. He strove and struggled immensely to guide this deviant people and endured great heartache in this path, but he had little effect on those blind-hearted individuals.

Ultimately, as we know, the severe wrath and punishment of Allah overtook them, and their settlements were completely overturned. Everyone was destroyed except for the family of Lot (with the exception of his wife). We have explained the full details of this event under verses 77 onwards of Surah Hud [^1]. Therefore, in the continuation of the verse under discussion, referring to the blessing He bestowed upon Lot, He says: "And We delivered him from the city that was practicing abominations" (وَ نَجَّيْناهُ مِنَ الْقَرْيَةِ الَّتِي كانَتْ تَعْمَلُ الْخَبائِثَ).

"For they were indeed a people of evil, rebellious" (إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا قَوْمَ سَوْءٍ فاسِقِينَ).

Attributing the foul and abominable acts to the "city" and the settlement instead of the people of the city is an indication that they were so drowned in corruption and sin that it was as if sin and foul deeds were raining down from the very doors and walls of their town.

The use of the term "khaba'ith" (abominations) in the plural form indicates that, in addition to the exceedingly heinous act of "sodomy," they also committed other foul and wicked deeds, which we have mentioned in Volume 9, page 197.

[^1]: Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 9, page 178 onwards.

The term "fasiqin" (rebellious/transgressors) after "people of evil" may indicate that they were rebellious people both in terms of divine laws and in terms of human standards; even regardless of religion and faith, they were base, foul, defiled, and deviant individuals.

Then, referring to the final divine blessing regarding Lot, He says: "And We admitted him into Our mercy" (وَ أَدْخَلْناهُ فِي رَحْمَتِنا). "For he was indeed of the righteous" (إِنَّهُ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ).

This special divine mercy is not given to anyone without reason; it was the merit and righteousness of Lot that made him worthy of such mercy.

Truly, what task is more difficult, and what reformative program is more grueling than for a person to remain for a long period in a city and land that has such corruption and defilement, constantly preaching to and guiding lost and deviant people, reaching a point where they even intend to harass his guests? Truly, such steadfastness is not possible except for the prophets of Allah and those who follow in their footsteps. Who among us can endure such agonizing spiritual torture?!

Ayahs

وَنُوحًا إِذْ نَادَىٰ مِن قَبْلُ فَٱسْتَجَبْنَا لَهُۥ فَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥ مِنَ ٱلْكَرْبِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٧٦)
And before that Noah, when he called out, We responded to him and delivered him and his family from the great agony. (76)
وَنَصَرْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمَ سَوْءٍ فَأَغْرَقْنَـٰهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٧٧)
And We helped him against the people who denied Our signs. They were indeed an evil lot; so We drowned them all. (77)

Commentary

Noah’s Deliverance from the Clutches of Stubborn Fanatics

After mentioning portions of the stories of Abraham and Lot, the text turns to a part of the life of another great prophet, namely "Noah," stating: "And remember Noah, when he called [upon His Lord] before them (before Abraham and Lot)" and requested deliverance from the clutches of the faithless deviants (وَ نُوحاً إِذْ نادى‌ مِنْ قَبْلُ‌).

This "call" of Noah apparently refers to the curse mentioned in Surah Noah in the Holy Quran, where he says: "My Lord! Leave not one of the disbelievers on the earth! For if You leave them, they will mislead Your servants, and they will beget none but wicked disbelievers!" (Noah, 26-27).

Alternatively, it may refer to the statement in verse 10 of Surah Al-Qamar: "So he called his Lord, 'Indeed, I am overcome, so help me!'" (فَدَعا رَبَّهُ أَنِّي مَغْلُوبٌ فَانْتَصِرْ).

The use of the term "nādā" (called out), which is usually used for calling with a loud voice, may indicate that they distressed this great prophet to such an extent that he finally cried out. Indeed, if we carefully examine the circumstances of Noah—parts of which are mentioned in Surah Noah and parts in Surah Hud—we see that he had every right to cry out. [^1] Then it adds: "So We responded to him and delivered him and his family from the great distress" (فَاسْتَجَبْنا لَهُ فَنَجَّيْناهُ وَ أَهْلَهُ مِنَ الْكَرْبِ الْعَظِيمِ‌).

In fact, the phrase "So We responded" (فَاسْتَجَبْنا) is a brief reference to the granting of his prayer, and the phrase "and We delivered him and his family from the great distress" serves as an elaboration and detail of that response.

Regarding who is meant by the word "ahl" (family/people) here, there is discussion among commentators. If it refers to his biological family, it would only include some of Noah's children, for we know that one of his sons "sat with the wicked and lost his prophetic lineage."

His wife was also not on his path. If "ahl" refers to his specific followers and faithful companions, it would be contrary to the common linguistic meaning of the word.

However, it can be said that "ahl" here has a broad meaning; it includes both his believing relatives and his close friends. This is because regarding his unworthy son, we read: "Indeed, he is not of your family (ahl); for his conduct was unrighteous" (Hud, 46).

Thus, those who had a shared ideological bond with Noah were, in reality, considered part of his family.

It is also necessary to mention that the word "karb" linguistically means intense grief, and it is originally derived from "karb" meaning to overturn the soil, because intense grief overturns a person's heart. Describing it as "great" (ʿaẓīm) conveys the ultimate intensity of Noah's sorrow.

What grief could be greater than this: according to the explicit verses of the Quran, he invited people to the true religion for 950 years, yet according to the famous view among commentators, during this entire long period, only eighty people believed in him! [^1] The work of the rest was nothing but mockery, ridicule, and harassment.

In the next verse, it adds: "And We helped him against the people who denied Our signs" (وَ نَصَرْناهُ مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا) ^2 "for they were indeed an evil people, so We drowned them all" (إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا قَوْمَ سَوْءٍ فَأَغْرَقْناهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ‌).

This sentence is once again an emphasis on the fact that divine punishments never have a vengeful aspect; rather, they are based on the "selection of the fittest." This means that the right to life and the use of life's blessings belong to the group that is on the path of perfection and the journey toward Allah—or if they fall into deviation one day, they later reconsider and return. However, a group that is corrupt and for whom there is no hope of reform in the future has no destiny other than death and destruction.

Key Point

It is also necessary to mention that in the stories of "Abraham" and "Lot" which we read previously, as well as "Job" and "Jonah" which will come in the following verses, the emphasis—just like with Noah—is on the issue of their salvation and deliverance from the clutches of hardships, sufferings, and tyrannical enemies.

It is as if the plan in this Surah (Al-Anbiya) is for Allah to recount His unwavering support for the prophets and their deliverance from the clutches of difficulties, so that it may be a source of comfort for the Prophet of Islam and hope for the believers. This issue becomes even more significant considering that this Surah is Meccan, and the Muslims at that time were in severe distress and suffering.

[^1]: Refer to Volume 9 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 68 to 125. [^1]: Majma' al-Bayan under verse 40 of Surah Hud, and Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 350.

Ayahs

وَدَاوُۥدَ وَسُلَيْمَـٰنَ إِذْ يَحْكُمَانِ فِى ٱلْحَرْثِ إِذْ نَفَشَتْ فِيهِ غَنَمُ ٱلْقَوْمِ وَكُنَّا لِحُكْمِهِمْ شَـٰهِدِينَ (٧٨)
And [remember] David and Solomon when they gave judgement concerning the tillage when the sheep of some people strayed into it by night, and We were witness to their judgement. (78)
فَفَهَّمْنَـٰهَا سُلَيْمَـٰنَ ۚ وَكُلًّا ءَاتَيْنَا حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا ۚ وَسَخَّرْنَا مَعَ دَاوُۥدَ ٱلْجِبَالَ يُسَبِّحْنَ وَٱلطَّيْرَ ۚ وَكُنَّا فَـٰعِلِينَ (٧٩)
We gave its understanding to Solomon, and to each We gave judgement and knowledge. We disposed the mountains and the birds to glorify [Us] with David, and We have been the doer [of these things]. (79)
وَعَلَّمْنَـٰهُ صَنْعَةَ لَبُوسٍ لَّكُمْ لِتُحْصِنَكُم مِّنۢ بَأْسِكُمْ ۖ فَهَلْ أَنتُمْ شَـٰكِرُونَ (٨٠)
We taught him the making of coats of mail for you, to protect you from your [own] violence. Will you then be grateful? (80)

Commentary

The Judgment of David and Solomon

Following the accounts related to Moses, Aaron, Abraham, Noah, and Lot, the verses under discussion refer to a portion of the lives of David and Solomon. At the outset, it makes a brief, coded reference to an event involving a judgment and arbitration carried out by David and Solomon:

It says: "And remember David and Solomon, when they gave judgment regarding the field into which the sheep of certain people had strayed by night" (وَ داوُدَ وَ سُلَيْمانَ إِذْ يَحْكُمانِ فِي الْحَرْثِ إِذْ نَفَشَتْ فِيهِ غَنَمُ الْقَوْمِ) [1] "and We were witnesses to their judgment" (وَ كُنَّا لِحُكْمِهِمْ شاهِدِينَ).

Although the Quran has expressed the details of this trial and judicial proceeding in a very concise manner, settling for a brief allusion and focusing only on its moral and educational outcome—which we will mention later—many discussions are found regarding it in Islamic narrations and the words of commentators.

A group has said: The story was such that a flock of sheep entered a vineyard at night, eating and destroying the leaves and clusters of grapes. The owner of the garden brought a complaint to David. David ruled that in exchange for this great loss, all the sheep must be given to the owner of the garden.

Solomon, who was a child at that time, said to his father: "O great Prophet of Allah! Change and moderate this ruling!" The father asked, "How?" In response, he said:

The sheep should be entrusted to the owner of the garden so that he may benefit from their products, milk, and wool, and the garden should be given to the owner of the sheep so that he may strive to repair it.

[1]: "Nafashat" is from the root "Nafsh" (rhyming with Kafsh), meaning to disperse at night. Since the dispersal of sheep at night in a field is naturally accompanied by grazing, some have defined it as grazing at night. "Nafash" (rhyming with Qafas) refers to sheep that disperse at night.

When the garden returns to its original state, it is handed back to its owner, and the sheep are also returned to their owner (and according to the next verse, Allah confirmed Solomon's judgment).

This content has been narrated in a tradition from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام [1]. It might be thought that this interpretation is inconsistent with the word "Harth," which means "tillage" or "crops," but apparently "Harth" has a broad meaning that includes both crops and gardens, as can be inferred from the story of the "Owners of the Garden" (أَصْحابَ الْجَنَّةِ) in Surah Al-Qalam, verses 17 to 32.

However, several important questions remain here: 1. What was the basis and criterion for these two rulings? 2. How did the rulings of David and Solomon differ? Did they judge based on Ijtihad (independent reasoning)? 3. Was this matter in the form of a consultation regarding the ruling, or did they issue two decisive and independent judgments?

In response to the first question, it can be said that the criterion was compensation for damages. David looked and saw that the damage caused to the vineyard was equivalent to the value of the sheep; therefore, he ruled that the sheep must be given to the garden owner to compensate for this loss, as the fault lay with the owner of the sheep. (Note that in some Islamic narrations, we read that during the night, it is the duty of the sheep owner to prevent his flock from trespassing on others' crops, while during the day, it is the duty of the crop owner) [1]. The standard for Solomon's ruling was that he saw the damage to the garden owner as equivalent to one year's benefits from the sheep.

Therefore, both judged with truth and justice, with the difference being that Solomon's judgment was implemented more precisely, as the loss was not paid all at once but gradually, so that it would not be heavy for the sheep owner. Furthermore, there was a proportion between the damage and the compensation; since the roots of the vines were not destroyed and only their temporary benefits were lost, it was more just that the principal of the sheep not pass into the ownership of the garden owner, but rather their benefits.

In response to the second question, we say: Undoubtedly, the judgment of Prophets is based on divine revelation. However, the meaning of this is not that a specific revelation descends for every single case of arbitration; rather, they judge according to general principles they have received from revelation.

Therefore, the issue of theoretical Ijtihad in its technical sense—meaning probabilistic reasoning—has no place in their rulings. However, there is no obstacle to there being two ways to implement a general principle, and two Prophets each suggesting one of these two ways, both of which are correct in reality. Coincidentally, in our subject of discussion, the matter was exactly like this, as previously explained. But as the Quran points out, the way Solomon suggested was closer (from an executive standpoint), and the phrase "وَ كُلًّا آتَيْنا حُكْماً وَ عِلْماً" (To each of them We gave judgment and knowledge), which appears in the next verse, is a testimony to the correctness of both judgments.

In response to the third question, we say: It is not unlikely that the matter was in the form of a consultation—a consultation that perhaps took place to test Solomon and his fitness for the matter of judgment. The expression "Hukmahuma" (the judgment of the two of them) is also evidence of the unity of the final ruling, even if two different suggestions existed at the beginning (reflect on this).

In a narration from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of this verse, we read: "They did not [both] issue a final ruling; rather, they were deliberating (and consulting)." [1] From another narration cited in Usul al-Kafi from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is also understood that this event took place as a means of determining the successor and deputy of David and testing them [2].

In any case, the next verse confirms Solomon's judgment in this story as follows: "We made Solomon understand the case" and with Our confirmation, he found the best solution for this dispute (فَفَهَّمْناها سُلَيْمانَ). However, the meaning of this is not that David's ruling was a mistake or incorrect, because He immediately adds: "and to each of them We gave judgment and knowledge" (وَ كُلًّا آتَيْنا حُكْماً وَ عِلْماً).

Then, He refers to another of the favors and honors Allah bestowed upon David, saying: "And We subjected the mountains to join David in sanctifying Us, and the birds as well" (وَ سَخَّرْنا مَعَ داوُدَ الْجِبالَ يُسَبِّحْنَ وَ الطَّيْرَ).

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan under the verses discussed. [1]: In Majma' al-Bayan under the verse discussed, we read: It is narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he ruled that the protection of livestock is upon their owners at night, and the protection of crops is upon their owners during the day. The same content is quoted in Tafsir al-Safi from the book al-Kafi. [1]: Man La Yahduruhu al-Faqih, as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 443. [2]: For further explanation, refer to Tafsir al-Safi under the verse discussed.

These are not significant matters before Our power: "We were well able to do this" (وَ كُنَّا فاعِلِينَ).

Key Points

There is discussion among commentators regarding how the mountains and birds joined in harmony with David:

  1. Sometimes it is suggested that it was David's resonant, captivating, and attractive voice that echoed in the mountains and drew the birds toward him.
  2. Sometimes it is said that this glorification (Tasbih) is a type of glorification accompanied by perception and consciousness that exists within the essence of the particles of the world of existence. According to this view, all beings in the universe possess a type of intellect and consciousness, and when they heard David's voice during his supplication and glorification, they harmonized with him, and the clamor of glorification blended together from them.
  3. Some have said: What is meant is "ontological glorification" (Tasbih Takwini) which takes place through the "language of state" (Zaban-e Hal) in all beings of the world. Since every being has a system—a very precise and calculated system—this precise system testifies to a God who is both Pure and Sanctified, and possesses attributes of perfection. Therefore, the wondrous system of the universe in every corner is "Glorification" and "Praise" (Glorification is sanctifying Him from defects, and Praise is extolling Him for attributes of perfection) [1]. And if it is said that this ontological glorification is neither exclusive to the mountains and birds nor exclusive to David, but rather the cry of this glorification rises always and everywhere from all beings.

[1]: For further explanation, refer to Volume 12 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 44 of Surah Al-Isra.

In response, they have said: It is true that this glorification is universal, but not everyone perceives it. It was the great soul of David that, in this state, became a confidant and harmonized with the inner reality of the universe, and he clearly felt that the mountains and birds were harmonizing and glorifying with him.

We have no decisive reason to favor any one of these interpretations; what we understand from the literal meaning of the verse is that the mountains and birds harmonized with David and glorified Allah. At the same time, there is no contradiction between these three interpretations, and combining them is possible.

In the final verse, He refers to another of the favors Allah gave to this great Prophet, saying: "And We taught him the making of coats of mail to protect you in your battles. Will you then be grateful for Allah's blessings?" (وَ عَلَّمْناهُ صَنْعَةَ لَبُوسٍ لَكُمْ لِتُحْصِنَكُمْ مِنْ بَأْسِكُمْ فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ شاكِرُونَ). "Labus," as the late Tabarsi says in Majma' al-Bayan, includes every kind of defensive and offensive weapon such as armor, swords, and spears [1]. However, the clues found in the Quranic verses show that "Labus" here means armor, which served a protective function in wars.

As for how Allah softened iron for David and taught him the industry of armor-making, the explanation of that will be given, Allah willing, under verses 10 and 11 of Surah Saba.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan under the verses discussed.

Ayahs

وَلِسُلَيْمَـٰنَ ٱلرِّيحَ عَاصِفَةً تَجْرِى بِأَمْرِهِۦٓ إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلَّتِى بَـٰرَكْنَا فِيهَا ۚ وَكُنَّا بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَـٰلِمِينَ (٨١)
And [We disposed] for Solomon the tempestuous wind which blew by his command toward the land which We have blessed, and We have knowledge of all things. (81)
وَمِنَ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينِ مَن يَغُوصُونَ لَهُۥ وَيَعْمَلُونَ عَمَلًا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ ۖ وَكُنَّا لَهُمْ حَـٰفِظِينَ (٨٢)
Among the devils were some who dived for him and performed tasks other than that, and We were watchful over them. (82)

Commentary

The Winds Under Solomon’s Command

In these verses, reference is made to a portion of the blessings that Allah bestowed upon another of the prophets, namely Solomon عليه السلام. First, It says: "And to Solomon [We subjected] the wind, blowing forcefully, proceeding by His command toward the land which We had blessed" (وَ لِسُلَيْمانَ الرِّيحَ عاصِفَةً تَجْرِي بِأَمْرِهِ إِلى‌ الْأَرْضِ الَّتِي بارَكْنا فِيها).

And this is not a strange matter, for "We are and have always been All-Knowing of everything" (وَ كُنَّا بِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ عالِمِينَ‌).

We are aware of the secrets of the universe and the laws and systems governing it; We know how they can be brought under command, and We are aware of the result and outcome of this action. In any case, everything is humble and submissive before Our knowledge and power.

The phrase "وَ لِسُلَيْمانَ ..." (And to Solomon...) is conjoined to the phrase "وَ سَخَّرْنا مَعَ داوُدَ الْجِبالَ‌" (And We subjected the mountains with David). This means Our power is such that sometimes We subject the mountains to one of Our servants so they may glorify Him along with him, and sometimes We place the winds under the command of one of Our servants to carry him wherever he wishes.

The word "عاصفة" (ʿāṣifah) means a gale or a storm, while it is understood from some other verses of the Quran that gentle winds were also at Solomon's command, as we read in Surah Sad, verse 36: فَسَخَّرْنا لَهُ الرِّيحَ تَجْرِي بِأَمْرِهِ رُخاءً حَيْثُ أَصابَ‌:

"So We subjected to him the wind blowing gently by his command wherever he directed."

Of course, the specific mention of "ʿāṣifah" (stormy wind) here might be to highlight the more significant instance; meaning that not only gentle winds but even fierce storms were under his command, as the latter is more wondrous and amazing.

Furthermore, these winds were not only subjected to him on the path to the blessed land (Syria/Levant) which was Solomon's headquarters; rather, according to verse 36 of Surah Sad, they moved wherever he intended. Therefore, the specific mention of the "blessed land" is primarily because it constituted the center of Solomon's government.

But how was the wind placed at his disposal?

At what speed did it move?

Upon what vehicle did Solomon and his companions sit and travel?

During movement, what factor protected them from falling, or from air pressure and other difficulties?

In short, what mysterious force was this that enabled him to have such rapid movement in that era and time? [^1] These are matters whose details are not clear to us. We only know this much: it was a divine blessing and a supernatural phenomenon granted to this great prophet, and we are unaware of its specifics. How many matters there are whose general existence we know of, but of whose explanation and details we are ignorant! Our knowledge compared to our ignorance is like a drop against a vast ocean, or like a speck of dust against a massive mountain.

In summary, from the perspective of a God-worshipping monotheist, nothing is difficult or impossible before the power of Allah; He is Capable of all things and All-Knowing of all things.

Of course, regarding this part of Solomon's life, like other parts of his wondrous life, many false or doubtful legends have been written which we never accept. We suffice only with what the Quran has stated here.

It is also necessary to mention that some recent writers believe the Quran does not explicitly mention the movement of Solomon and his carpet/throne by the wind; rather, it only speaks of the subjection of the wind for Solomon. They suggest this might refer to Solomon's use of wind power in matters related to agriculture, pollination of plants, winnowing of harvests, and the movement of ships—especially since Solomon's land (the Levant) was, on one hand, an agricultural land, and on the other, a significant part of it lay on the coast of the Mediterranean Sea and was utilized for navigation. [^2]

[^1]: From verse 12 of Surah Saba (وَ لِسُلَيْمانَ الرِّيحَ غُدُوُّها شَهْرٌ وَ رَواحُها شَهْرٌ), it is generally understood that they would travel a distance of one month's journey in the morning and one month's journey in the evening (by the standards of travel in that time).

[^2]: Qisas al-Quran, page 185; A'lam al-Quran, 386.

However, this interpretation is not very consistent with the verses of Surah "Saba" and "Sad" and some narrations that have been transmitted in this regard.

The next verse recounts another of the exclusive blessings given to Solomon عليه السلام: "And of the devils were those who dived for him" (and brought out jewels and precious materials) "and performed work other than that" (وَ مِنَ الشَّياطِينِ مَنْ يَغُوصُونَ لَهُ وَ يَعْمَلُونَ عَمَلًا دُونَ ذلِكَ‌).

"And We were observers over them" (to keep them from rebelling or defying his command) (وَ كُنَّا لَهُمْ حافِظِينَ‌).

What is mentioned in the above verse as "devils" (Shayatin) is presented in the verses of Surah Saba as "Jinn" (Saba, verses 12 and 13). It is clear that these two do not contradict each other, as we know that "devils" are also from the race of Jinn.

In any case, as we have previously mentioned: the Jinn are a type of creature possessing intellect, consciousness, aptitude, and religious obligation, who are hidden from the sight of us humans—and for this reason, they are called "Jinn" (the hidden ones). As understood from the verses of Surah al-Jinn, they, like humans, consist of two groups: righteous believers and rebellious disbelievers. We have no reason to deny the existence of such beings, and since the Truthful Informant (the Quran) has reported it, we accept it.

From the verses of Surah Sad and Saba, as well as the verse under discussion, it is well understood that this group of Jinn subjected to Solomon were intelligent, active, artistic, and industrial individuals with various skills.

The phrase "يَعْمَلُونَ عَمَلًا دُونَ ذلِكَ‌" (and performed work other than that) is a summary of what is detailed in Surah Saba: يَعْمَلُونَ لَهُ ما يَشاءُ مِنْ مَحارِيبَ وَ تَماثِيلَ وَ جِفانٍ كَالْجَوابِ وَ قُدُورٍ راسِياتٍ‌ which shows that "they made for him whatever he desired of sanctuaries, statues, basins like reservoirs, and fixed cooking pots." This indicates they built for him magnificent and beautiful temples and various living utilities, including very large pots and trays and the like.

From some other verses related to Solomon, it is understood that there was also a group of rebellious devils whom he had bound in chains: وَ آخَرِينَ مُقَرَّنِينَ فِي الْأَصْفادِ (Sad: 38). Perhaps the phrase "وَ كُنَّا لَهُمْ حافِظِينَ‌" (And We were their keepers) also refers to the fact that We prevented that group serving Solomon from rebelling and defying him. You will, Allah willing, read more details on this subject in the commentary of Surah Saba and Sad.

We remind you once again that regarding the life of Solomon and his army, there are many false or doubtful legends which must never be mixed with what is in the text of the Quran, lest they become a pretext for critics.

Ayahs

۞ وَأَيُّوبَ إِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبَّهُۥٓ أَنِّى مَسَّنِىَ ٱلضُّرُّ وَأَنتَ أَرْحَمُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ (٨٣)
And [remember] Job, when he called out to his Lord, ‘Indeed distress has befallen me, and You are the most merciful of the merciful.’ (83)
فَٱسْتَجَبْنَا لَهُۥ فَكَشَفْنَا مَا بِهِۦ مِن ضُرٍّ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ أَهْلَهُۥ وَمِثْلَهُم مَّعَهُمْ رَحْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِنَا وَذِكْرَىٰ لِلْعَـٰبِدِينَ (٨٤)
So We answered his prayer and removed his distress, and We gave him [back] his family along with others like them, as a mercy from Us and an admonition for the devout. (84)

Commentary

We Rescued Job from the Whirlpool of Afflictions

The two preceding verses speak of another of God’s great prophets and his instructive life story; he is "Job" (Ayyub), the tenth prophet whose life is briefly touched upon in Surah Al-Anbiya.

Job has a story that is both sorrowful and, at the same time, magnificent and awe-inspiring. His patience and fortitude, especially in the face of adverse events, were so extraordinary that "the patience of Job" has become an ancient proverb.

However, the verses under discussion specifically point to the stage of his rescue, his victory over problems, and his regaining of lost blessings. This serves as a lesson for all believers in every age and time who are submerged in the depths of difficulties—especially the believers in Mecca who, at the time of the revelation of these verses, were under a severe siege by the enemy.

He says: "And remember Job, when he called out to his Lord, presenting that distress, pain, illness, problems, and affliction have touched me, and You are the Most Merciful of the merciful" (وَ أَيُّوبَ إِذْ نادى‌ رَبَّهُ أَنِّي مَسَّنِيَ الضُّرُّ وَ أَنْتَ أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ‌).

"Durr" (rhyming with hurr) refers to any kind of evil or discomfort that reaches a person's soul, spirit, or body, as well as physical disability, loss of wealth, the death of loved ones, the trampling of reputation, and the like. As we shall mention later, Job was afflicted with many of these hardships.

Job, like other prophets, employed the utmost etiquette in the presence of Allah when praying for the removal of these grueling problems. He did not even use an expression that smelled of complaint; he merely said: "I have been touched by distress, and You are the Most Merciful of the merciful." He did not even say, "Remove my problem," for He knows that He is Great and knows the way of Greatness.

The next verse says: "Following this prayer of Job, We responded to him and removed the suffering and distress that was upon him" (فَاسْتَجَبْنا لَهُ فَكَشَفْنا ما بِهِ مِنْ ضُرٍّ). "And We restored his family to him, and the like of them with them" (وَ آتَيْناهُ أَهْلَهُ وَ مِثْلَهُمْ مَعَهُمْ‌).

"As a mercy from Our presence and a reminder for the worshippers of the Lord" (رَحْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِنا وَ ذِكْرى‌ لِلْعابِدِينَ‌).

This is so that Muslims may know that no matter how great the problems, how numerous the afflictions, and how tightly packed the enemies and their forces may be, all of these can be removed with a touch of the Lord's grace. Not only are the losses compensated, but sometimes Allah, as a reward for the steadfast and patient, adds to them the equivalent of what was lost. This is a lesson for all Muslims, especially those who, at the time of these verses' revelation, were caught in a harsh enemy siege and a multitude of problems.

Key Points

A Summary of the Story of Job

In a tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Someone asked, 'What was the reason for the affliction that befell Job?'"

Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام gave an answer, the summary of which is as follows: The affliction that befell Job was not because he had been ungrateful for a blessing; rather, on the contrary, it was because of his gratitude for blessings that Iblis (Satan) became envious of him. Iblis said to the presence of Allah: "If he performs all this gratitude for Your blessings, it is because You have given him a vast and comfortable life. If You take away the material gifts of the world from him, he will never give thanks to You. Grant me power over his world so it may become clear that this is the case."

In order for this event to be a testament for all wayfarers of the path of Truth, Allah gave Satan this permission. He came and took away Job's wealth and children one after another. However, these painful events not only did not decrease Job's gratitude, but his gratitude increased! Satan then asked Allah for power over his crops and sheep; this permission was granted to him, and he burned all the crops and destroyed the sheep. Again, Job's praise and gratitude increased.

Finally, Satan asked Allah for power over Job's body to cause him severe illness, and so it happened, to the extent that he was unable to move due to the severity of the illness and wounds, yet without the slightest defect occurring in his intellect or perception.

In short, blessings were taken from Job one after another, but parallel to that, his station of gratitude rose.

Eventually, a group of monks came to visit him and said: "Tell us, what great sin have you committed to be afflicted like this?" (And thus, the taunting of this person and that began, and this matter was very heavy upon Job). Job said: "By the glory of my Lord, I never ate a morsel of food unless an orphan or a weak person sat at the table with me, and no act of Divine obedience arose unless I chose its most difficult path."

It was at this moment, when Job had succeeded in all trials regarding patience and gratitude, that he opened his tongue in supplication and prayer, asking for the resolution of his problems with a very polite expression, free from any complaint to Allah (the expression mentioned in the verses above: رَبَّهُ أَنِّي مَسَّنِيَ الضُّرُّ وَ أَنْتَ أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ‌).

At this time, the doors of Divine mercy were opened, the problems were quickly removed, and Divine blessings returned to him even more abundantly than before. [^1] Indeed, the men of Truth do not change their thoughts and programs when blessings change. In ease and in affliction, in freedom and in prison, in health and in sickness, in power and in weakness—in short, in all states—they are attentive to the Lord. The fluctuations of life do not create a change in them; their souls are like the Great Ocean, whose tranquility is not disturbed by storms.

Furthermore, they never despair from a multitude of bitter events; they stand and persevere until the doors of Divine mercy are opened. They know that difficult events are Divine trials that He sometimes prepares for His special servants to make them more tempered and refined.

2- In the commentary of the phrase "وَ آتَيْناهُ أَهْلَهُ وَ مِثْلَهُمْ مَعَهُمْ‌" (And We gave him his family and the like of them with them), the well-known view among commentators is that Allah returned his children to their first life and, in addition, gave him other children as well. (In some narrations, we also read that Allah granted him both the children who had perished in this event and returned to him the children who had perished before that). [^1] Some have also suggested the possibility that Allah gave Job new children and grandchildren who filled the void of his lost children.

[Repulsive Illness is Impossible for Prophets]

3- In some unreliable narrations, we read that due to severe illness, Job's body had become so infected that people could not approach him. However, this meaning is explicitly denied in the narrations that have reached us through the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام. Logic also points to this same meaning, because if a Prophet possesses a repulsive state or quality, it is inconsistent with the program of his mission. He must be such that all people can easily contact him and hear the words of Truth; a Prophet always possesses attraction.

You will read a more detailed explanation regarding the story of Job in Surah Sad, verses 41 to 44.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qummi (as quoted in Tafsir al-Mizan). [^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 448.

Ayahs

وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِدْرِيسَ وَذَا ٱلْكِفْلِ ۖ كُلٌّ مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (٨٥)
And [remember] Ishmael, Idris, and Dhul-Kifl—each of them was among the patient. (85)
وَأَدْخَلْنَـٰهُمْ فِى رَحْمَتِنَآ ۖ إِنَّهُم مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٨٦)
We admitted them into Our mercy. Indeed, they were among the righteous. (86)

Commentary

Ishmael, Idris, and Dhul-Kifl

Following the instructive account of "Job" (Ayyub) and his patience and endurance against the storm of calamities, the verses under discussion refer to the station of patience of three other Divine prophets, stating: "And remember Ishmael, Idris, and Dhul-Kifl; all were of the patient" (وَ إِسْماعِيلَ وَ إِدْرِيسَ وَ ذَا الْكِفْلِ كُلٌّ مِنَ الصَّابِرِينَ).

Each of them, throughout their lives, exercised patience and resistance against enemies or the grueling hardships of life, never kneeling before these events; each served as a model of steadfastness and perseverance.

Then, He describes the greatest Divine blessing granted to them in exchange for this patience and steadfastness: "And We admitted them into Our mercy. Indeed, they were of the righteous" (وَ أَدْخَلْناهُمْ فِي رَحْمَتِنا إِنَّهُمْ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ). It is interesting that He does not say, "We granted Our mercy to them," but rather says:

"We admitted them into Our mercy," as if they were immersed with their entire body and soul in Divine mercy, just as they were previously immersed in a sea of hardships.

Idris and Dhul-Kifl

"Idris," the great Divine prophet—as we have mentioned previously—was, according to many commentators, the great-grandfather of Noah (Nuh). His name in the Torah is "Enoch" (Akhnukh), and in Arabic, it is Idris, which some believe is derived from the root "dars" (study/lesson), because he was the first person to write with a pen. In addition to the station of prophethood, he had mastery over the sciences of astronomy, mathematics, and cosmography, and it is said he was the first person to teach humans the art of sewing clothes.

As for "Dhul-Kifl," the famous view is that he was among the prophets [1], although some believe he was one of the righteous (Saliheen). The apparent context of the Quranic verses, which place him in the ranks of the great prophets, also suggests that he was a prophet, and it seems more likely that he was one of the prophets of the Children of Israel [2]. Regarding the reason for naming him this—considering that "Kifl" (rhyming with 'fikr') means both "portion/share" and "sponsorship/undertaking"—several possibilities have been suggested. Some have said that because Allah granted him an abundant portion (kifl) of reward and mercy in exchange for the many deeds and acts of worship he performed, he was named Dhul-Kifl (meaning "Possessor of the Ample Portion"). Others have said that because he had committed (ta'ahhud) to stand in worship at night, fast during the day, and never become angry while judging, and he fulfilled his promise to the end, he was named "Dhul-Kifl" (The One of the Undertaking).

[1]: Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr Razi, under the verse discussed.

[2]: Tafsir Fi Zilal, Vol. 5, p. 556.

Some also believe that Dhul-Kifl is a title for "Elijah" (Ilyas), just as "Israel" is the title for "Jacob" (Ya'qub), "Messiah" (Masih) is the title for "Jesus" ('Isa), and "Dhun-Nun" is the title for "Jonah" (Yunus) [1].

Ayahs

وَذَا ٱلنُّونِ إِذ ذَّهَبَ مُغَـٰضِبًا فَظَنَّ أَن لَّن نَّقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ فَنَادَىٰ فِى ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ أَن لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنتَ سُبْحَـٰنَكَ إِنِّى كُنتُ مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٨٧)
And [remember] the Man of the Fish, when he left in a rage, thinking that We would not put him to hardship. Then he cried out in the darkness, ‘There is no god except You! You are immaculate! I have indeed been among the wrongdoers!’ (87)
فَٱسْتَجَبْنَا لَهُۥ وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلْغَمِّ ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُـۨجِى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٨٨)
So We answered his prayer and delivered him from the agony; and thus do We deliver the faithful. (88)

Commentary

Jonah’s Deliverance from That Dreadful Prison

These two verses also recount a portion of the life of the great Prophet "Jonah" (Yunus). First, it says: "And remember Dhul-Nun, when he departed in anger" from his idolatrous and disobedient people (وَ ذَا النُّونِ إِذْ ذهَبَ مُغاضِباً).

"Nun" literally means a large fish, or in other words, a whale. Therefore, "Dhul-Nun" means "the companion of the fish." This title was chosen for Jonah because of the event that, God willing, we will refer to later.

In any case, "he imagined that We would not straiten [life] for him" (فَظَنَّ أَنْ لَنْ نَقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ) [^1]. He thought he had fulfilled his entire mission among his disobedient people and had not even committed a "leaving of the better" (Tark al-Awla) in this regard. He believed that now that he was leaving them to themselves and departing, nothing would be held against him. However, the "better" (Awla) was for him to remain among them longer, to exercise patience and steadfastness, and to endure with fortitude, so that perhaps they might awaken and turn toward Allah.

Ultimately, because of this "leaving of the better," We placed him under pressure; a giant whale swallowed him, "and he called out in the depths of darkness: 'There is no deity except You'" (فَنادى‌ فِي الظُّلُماتِ أَنْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنْتَ).

"Glory be to You, indeed I was among the wrongdoers!" (سُبْحانَكَ إِنِّي كُنْتُ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ).

I wronged both myself and my people; I should have accepted more hardships, difficulties, and rigors, and submitted to all manner of trials so that perhaps they would find the right path.

[^1]: "Tafsir Fakhr Razi" under the verse in question; also in "Tarikh al-Kamil" we read: Dhul-Kifl was one of the sons of Job (Ayyub) and his original name was "Bishr," and he lived in the land of Sham (Al-Kamil ibn al-Athir, Vol. 1, p. 136).

[^1]: "Naqdira" is from the root "Qadr," meaning strictness and restriction, because when a person is being strict, they consider everything in a limited measure (Qadr) rather than something expansive and uncounted.

"So We answered his prayer and delivered him from grief" (فَاسْتَجَبْنا لَهُ وَ نَجَّيْناهُ مِنَ الْغَمِّ).

Indeed, "thus do We deliver the believers" (وَ كَذلِكَ نُنْجِي الْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

This was not a program exclusive to Jonah; rather, any believer who offers an excuse to Allah for their shortcomings and seeks help and mercy from His Pure Essence, We shall answer their prayer and remove their sorrow.

Key Points

1- The Story of Jonah

God willing, the details of this will come in the commentary of Surah "As-Saffat," but the summary is as follows:

For years, he was engaged in invitation and preaching among his people (in the land of Nineveh in Iraq). However, no matter how hard he tried, his guidance did not affect their hearts. He became angry, left that place, and went toward the sea. There he boarded a ship. Mid-journey, the sea became turbulent, and the ship's inhabitants were nearly drowned.

The captain of the ship said: "I think there is a runaway slave among you who must be cast into the sea" (or he said: "The ship is too heavy and we must cast one person into the sea by drawing lots"). In any case, they drew lots several times, and each time it came out in Jonah's name! He realized there was a secret hidden in this matter and submitted to the events.

When they threw him into the sea, a giant whale swallowed him, and Allah miraculously kept him alive.

Finally, he realized he had committed a "leaving of the better," turned toward Allah, and confessed to his shortcoming. Allah also answered his prayer and delivered him from the straits [^1]. It might be imagined that this event is not scientifically possible, but undoubtedly this matter is a "miracle" (Kharq al-Adah), not a logical impossibility. It is like the resurrection of the dead, which is considered only a miracle and is not impossible; in other words, it is not possible through ordinary means, but with the help of the infinite power of the Lord, it presents no problem.

You will read more details about this event, God willing, in the commentary of Surah As-Saffat.

2- What does "Darkness" mean here?

This expression might refer to the darkness of the sea and the depths of the water, the darkness of the giant fish's belly, and the darkness of the night. A narration cited from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام confirms this [^2].

3- What "Leaving of the Better" did Jonah commit?

Undoubtedly, the expression "mughadiban" (in anger) refers to Jonah's anger and indignation toward his faithless people. Such anger and distress are completely natural in circumstances where a compassionate Prophet works for years to guide a lost people, yet they never give a positive response to his benevolent call.

On the other hand, since Jonah knew that divine punishment would soon overtake them, leaving that city and land was not a sin. However, for a great Prophet like Jonah, the "better" (Awla) was not to leave them until the very last moment—the moment after which divine punishment arrives. For this reason, Jonah was called to account by Allah for this haste as a "leaving of the better."

This is the same point we mentioned in the story of Adam: it is not an absolute sin but a relative sin, or in other words, an instance of "the good deeds of the righteous are the sins of those drawn near [to Allah]" (For more explanation, refer to Volume 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 122 onwards).

4- A Life-Changing Lesson

The profound sentence "Thus do We deliver the believers" shows that what happened to Jonah regarding his distress and deliverance was not a private ruling. Rather, while maintaining the hierarchy of status, it has a general and universal aspect.

Many tragic events and severe, calamitous hardships are the result of our sins; they are lashes to awaken sleeping souls, or a furnace to purify the metal of the human soul. Whenever a person, at such a time, pays attention to the same three points that Jonah attended to, deliverance and rescue will be certain:

1- Attention to the reality of Monotheism (Tawhid) and that there is no deity and no support except "Allah." 2- Considering Allah pure and sanctified from any defect, deficiency, injustice, or oppression, and any ill-thought regarding His Pure Essence. 3- Confessing to one's own sin and shortcoming.

Evidence for this is a Hadith narrated in "Durr al-Manthur" from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله who said: "One of the names of Allah, which if anyone calls Him by it, He answers, and whenever they ask for something by it, He grants it, is the prayer of Jonah."

Someone asked: "O Messenger of Allah, was this specific to Jonah or does it include the community of Muslims as well?" He replied: "It pertained to both Jonah and all believers when they call upon Allah by it. Have you not heard the statement of Allah in the Quran: 'And thus do We deliver the believers'? This is proof that whoever makes such a prayer, Allah has guaranteed its answer" [^1]. It goes without saying that the intention is not merely reciting the words, but the implementation of its reality within the human soul; that is, while reciting these words, one's entire being should harmonize with its meaning.

It is also necessary to remind that divine punishments are of two kinds: one is the punishment of "extirpation" (Istisal), meaning the final punishment that arrives for the destruction of unreformable individuals, where no prayer is useful at that time, because after the storm of calamity subsides, the same patterns would repeat.

The other kind is disciplinary punishment which has an educational aspect. In these cases, as soon as the punishment achieves its effect and the party becomes awakened and mindful, it immediately disappears.

From this, it becomes clear that one of the philosophies of afflictions, calamities, and unpleasant events is indeed awakening and education.

The story of Jonah also warns all leaders of the path of truth in various spheres never to consider their mission finished, and to regard every effort and endeavor in this path as small, for their responsibility is very difficult and heavy.

[^1]: Tafsir Fakhr Razi, Majma' al-Bayan, and Nur al-Thaqalayn under the verse in question. [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 336. [^1]: Durr al-Manthur as cited in Al-Mizan under the verses in question.

Ayahs

وَزَكَرِيَّآ إِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبَّهُۥ رَبِّ لَا تَذَرْنِى فَرْدًا وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلْوَٰرِثِينَ (٨٩)
And [remember] Zechariah, when he cried out to his Lord, ‘My Lord! Do not leave me without an heir, and You are the best of inheritors.’ (89)
فَٱسْتَجَبْنَا لَهُۥ وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُۥ يَحْيَىٰ وَأَصْلَحْنَا لَهُۥ زَوْجَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِى ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ وَيَدْعُونَنَا رَغَبًا وَرَهَبًا ۖ وَكَانُوا۟ لَنَا خَـٰشِعِينَ (٩٠)
So We answered his prayer, and gave him John, and cured for him his wife [of infertility]. Indeed, they were active in [performing] good works, and they would supplicate Us with eagerness and awe and were humble before Us. (90)

Commentary

Zakariya’s Deliverance from Loneliness

In these two verses, a portion of the life stories of two other great Divine Prophets, "Zakariya" and "Yahya," is presented.

First, it says: And remember Zakariya when he called upon his Lord and pleaded: "O my Lord! Leave me not alone, and You are the best of inheritors."

(وَ زَكَرِيَّا إِذْ نادى‌ رَبَّهُ رَبِّ لا تَذَرْنِي فَرْداً وَ أَنْتَ خَيْرُ الْوارِثِينَ‌).

Many years of Zakariya’s life had passed, and the snow of old age had settled upon his head, yet he still had no child; on the other hand, he had a wife who was barren and sterile.

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 13; p. 491

He longed for a child who could follow his Divine programs so that his missionary work would not remain unfinished, and so that opportunists after him would not seize control of the sanctuary of the Children of Israel and its wealth and gifts, which ought to be spent in the way of Allah.

At this moment, he turned to Allah’s threshold with all his heart, requesting a righteous and noble child. He called upon Allah with a prayer filled with the utmost etiquette; he began first with "Rabb" (Lord)—the same Sustainer whose grace encompasses man from the first moments of life. Then, using the expression "لا تَذَرْنِي" (leave me not), derived from the root "wadhr" (meaning to leave something due to its insignificance or out of neglect), he expressed the reality that if he remained alone, he would be forgotten—not just himself, but his programs too would be consigned to oblivion. Finally, with the phrase "وَ أَنْتَ خَيْرُ الْوارِثِينَ‌" (and You are the best of inheritors), he voiced the truth that: "I know this world is not an eternal abode, and I know You are the best of inheritors, but in the realm of causes, I seek a means that will lead me toward my goal."

Allah answered this sincere prayer, which was overflowing with love for the truth, and fulfilled his desire, as He says: "So We answered his prayer and bestowed upon him Yahya" (فَاسْتَجَبْنا لَهُ وَ وَهَبْنا لَهُ يَحْيى‌). And to achieve this purpose, "We cured his wife for him" (making her capable of bearing a child) (وَ أَصْلَحْنا لَهُ زَوْجَهُ‌).

Then, He points to three of the outstanding qualities of this family, stating:

"Indeed, they used to hasten in doing good deeds" (إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا يُسارِعُونَ فِي الْخَيْراتِ‌).

"And they would call upon Us in hope and fear" (out of love for obedience and dread of sin) (وَ يَدْعُونَنا رَغَباً وَ رَهَباً) [1]. "And they were always humble before Us" (a humility mixed with etiquette and respect, and a fear mixed with a sense of responsibility) (وَ كانُوا لَنا خاشِعِينَ‌).

The mention of these three qualities may indicate that when they attained blessings, they did not fall into the negligence and arrogance that afflict people of limited capacity and weak faith upon reaching a blessing. They never forgot the needy and were swift in charitable acts; in times of need and self-sufficiency, poverty and wealth, illness and health, they were always attentive to Allah; and finally, they did not become ensnared by pride and ego due to the arrival of blessings, but were always humble and submissive.

[1]: Raghaban means desire, inclination, and affection, while rahaban means fear, aversion, and dread. Regarding its grammatical position (I'rab), there are several possibilities: it could be hal (circumstantial), tamyiz (specification), maf'ul lahu (object of purpose), maf'ul mutlaq (absolute object), or it could imply adverbial usage meaning "in the state of hope and in the state of fear." Although the results of these five possibilities differ slightly, the difference lies in the nuances of the verse's meaning rather than its fundamental essence and conclusion.

Ayah

وَٱلَّتِىٓ أَحْصَنَتْ فَرْجَهَا فَنَفَخْنَا فِيهَا مِن رُّوحِنَا وَجَعَلْنَـٰهَا وَٱبْنَهَآ ءَايَةً لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٩١)
And [remember] her who guarded her chastity, so We breathed into her Our spirit, and made her and her son a sign for all the nations. (91)

Commentary

Mary, the Chaste Lady

This verse refers to the status of Mary and her greatness and honor, as well as that of her son, Prophet Messiah عليه السلام.

The mention of Mary alongside the discussions regarding the great Prophets is either because of her son, Prophet Jesus, or because her birth was similar in some respects to the birth of John (Yahya) from Zechariah, the details of which we provided under the verses of Surah Maryam [1]. Alternatively, it may be to clarify that greatness of status is not exclusive to great men; rather, there have been great women whose history is a sign of their greatness and who have served as role models and examples for the women of the world.

It says: "And remember Mary, who guarded her chastity" (وَ الَّتِي أَحْصَنَتْ فَرْجَها).

"Then We breathed into her of Our Spirit" (فَنَفَخْنا فِيها مِنْ رُوحِنا).

"And We made her and her son, the Messiah, a great sign for the worlds" (وَ جَعَلْناها وَ ابْنَها آيَةً لِلْعالَمِينَ).

Key Points

1- From a linguistic perspective, "Farj" (فرج) originally means a gap or an opening, and it has been used as a metaphor for the reproductive organ. However, since its metaphorical meaning is often overlooked in Persian [and English], the question sometimes arises as to how this term, which explicitly refers to that specific human organ, appears in the Quran. Paying attention to its metaphorical nature resolves this issue.

To put it more clearly, if we want to correctly express the metaphorical meaning, the equivalent of the phrase "أَحْصَنَتْ فَرْجَها" in Persian [and English] is that "she kept her hem clean" (guarded her chastity). Is this expression offensive?

In fact, according to some, in the Arabic language, there are no words that are explicitly for the reproductive organs or explicit for sexual intercourse; everything is metaphorical. For example, regarding sexual intercourse, terms such as "touching," "entering," "covering" (Ghashiyan) [1], or "going unto" one's spouse [2] are used in various verses of the Quran, all of which we see are metaphorical. However, sometimes translators are heedless of these metaphorical meanings and substitute explicit words instead of the equivalents of these metaphors, which becomes a cause for surprise and questioning.

In any case, in the interpretation of such terms that have appeared in the Quran, one must certainly pay attention to their original and root meanings so that their metaphorical aspect becomes clear and any ambiguity is removed.

It is also necessary to mention that the literal appearance of the above verse says that Mary protected herself from any pollution of indecency. However, some commentators have suggested the possibility that the verse means she refrained from any contact with a man (whether lawful or unlawful) [3], just as it says in verse 20 of Surah Maryam:

وَ لَمْ يَمْسَسْنِي بَشَرٌ وَ لَمْ أَكُ بَغِيًّا: "No man has ever touched me, nor have I been unchaste" [4]. In reality, this is a prelude to proving the miraculous birth of Jesus and his being a "Sign."

2- The meaning of "Ruhina" (Our Spirit)—as we have stated previously—refers to a magnificent and exalted spirit. This type of possessive construction is called an "Honorific Addition" (Idafah Tashrifiyyah), where we attribute something to Allah to express its greatness, such as "Bayt Allah" (the House of Allah) and "Shahr Allah" (the Month of Allah).

3- The above verse says: "We made her and her son a sign (Ayah) for the worlds." It does not say "two signs," because the existence of Mary and her son were so intertwined in this great divine sign that they were considered inseparable from one another. The birth of a child without a father is just as miraculous as a woman becoming pregnant without a husband. Furthermore, the miracles of Jesus عليه السلام in infancy and adulthood serve as a reminder of his mother.

These matters, each of which was extraordinary and contrary to natural causes, all testify to the reality that beyond the chain of causes, there is a Power that can change their course whenever He wills. In any case, the situation of the Messiah and his mother Mary was unique throughout human history; it was seen neither before nor after him. Perhaps the use of the indefinite form for "Ayah" (a sign), which indicates greatness, is an allusion to this very meaning.

[^1]: Refer to Volume 13 of Tafsir Nemouneh, the opening verses of Surah Maryam. [^2]: Surah Al-A'raf, verse 189. [^3]: Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 222. [^4]: Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr Razi and Tafsir Fi Zilal under the verse in question. [^5]: Maryam, 20.

Ayahs

إِنَّ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً وَأَنَا۠ رَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُونِ (٩٢)
Indeed this community of yours is one community, and I am your Lord. So worship Me. (92)
وَتَقَطَّعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَهُم بَيْنَهُمْ ۖ كُلٌّ إِلَيْنَا رَٰجِعُونَ (٩٣)
They have fragmented their religion among themselves, but everyone of them will return to Us. (93)
فَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَا كُفْرَانَ لِسَعْيِهِۦ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ كَـٰتِبُونَ (٩٤)
Whoever is faithful and does righteous deeds, his endeavour shall not go unappreciated, and We will indeed record it for him. (94)

Commentary

The Single Ummah

Since the previous verses mentioned a group of Divine prophets as well as Maryam, that exemplary lady, and parts of their life stories, the verses under discussion provide a general summary and conclusion, stating: "Indeed, these great prophets who were referred to were all one single Ummah" (إِنَّ هذِهِ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً).

Their program was one, and their goal and destination were also one. Although they possessed different characteristics, methods, and so-called "tactics" due to differences in time and environment, they all ultimately moved along the same line. They all stepped in the line of Monotheism (Tawhid), the struggle against polytheism, and inviting the people of the world toward oneness, truth, and justice.

This oneness and unity of programs and goals were because they all originated from a single source—from the Will of the One and Only Allah. Therefore, He immediately says:

"And I am your Lord, so worship Me alone" (وَ أَنَا رَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُونِ).

In fact, the ideological and practical Monotheism of the prophets stems from the Monotheism of the source of revelation. This statement is similar to what Ali عليه السلام said in his testament to his son, Imam al-Mujtaba عليه السلام:

“And know, O my son, that if your Lord had a partner, His messengers would have come to you, and you would have seen the signs of His kingdom and power, and you would have known His actions and attributes.” [^1]

The word "Ummah," as Raghib states in the book Al-Mufradat, refers to any group or population linked together by a common factor: sharing the same religion and creed, or the same time and era, or a specific place—whether this unity is voluntary or involuntary.

Some commentators have interpreted "Ummah Wahidah" (Single Ummah) here to mean "Single Religion," but as we mentioned, this interpretation is not consistent with the linguistic root of "Ummah."

Others have said that "Ummah" in this verse refers to all human beings across all centuries and ages; meaning, "O humans, you are all one Ummah, your Lord is One,

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Letter 31.

and the final destination of all of you is one."

While this interpretation is more suitable than the previous one, it does not seem appropriate given the connection of this verse to the preceding verses. The most fitting interpretation is that this sentence refers to the prophets and messengers whose biographies were mentioned in the previous verses.

In the next verse, He points to the deviation of a large group of people from this single principle of Monotheism, saying: "But they tore their affair [of unity] between them into pieces" (وَ تَقَطَّعُوا أَمْرَهُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ).

Their situation reached a point where they stood against one another; each group cursed the other and sought disassociation (bara'ah) from them. They did not stop there; they drew weapons against each other and shed much blood. This was the result of deviating from the fundamental principle of Monotheism and the single religion of truth.

The word "taqatta'u," which comes from the root "qat'" (to cut), means separating pieces from a cohesive subject. Given that it is in the "tafa''ul" form, which implies acceptance, the concept of the sentence becomes: they surrendered to the factors of division and hypocrisy, accepted separation and alienation from one another, ended their innate and monotheistic unity, and as a result, were afflicted with all those failures, frustrations, and miseries.

And at the end of the verse, He adds: "But all of them shall eventually return to Us" (كُلٌّ إِلَيْنا راجِعُونَ).

This discord, which is of a transient nature, will be folded away, and in the Resurrection, everyone will again move toward unity. In various verses of the Quran, much emphasis is placed on the fact that one of the characteristics of the Resurrection is the removal of differences and the drive toward unity.

In verse 48 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, we read: إِلَى اللَّهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعاً فَيُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ—"To Allah is your return all together, and He will inform you of that in which you used to differ."

This theme is seen in numerous verses of the Holy Quran, [^1] and in this way, the creation of humans begins from "unity" and returns to "unity."

In the last verse under discussion, He describes the result of harmony with the "Single Ummah" in the path of worshipping the Lord, or deviating from it and following the path of division, as follows:

"Whoever does any righteous deeds while he is a believer, his effort shall not be denied" (فَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِنَ الصَّالِحاتِ وَ هُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلا كُفْرانَ لِسَعْيِهِ).

And for further emphasis, He adds: "And We are surely recording [his righteous deeds] for him" (وَ إِنَّا لَهُ كاتِبُونَ).

It is noteworthy that in this verse, like many other verses of the Quran, faith and righteous action are mentioned as two fundamental pillars for the salvation of humans. However, by adding the word "min" (some of), which is for "partitioning" (tab'id), He adds the point that performing all righteous deeds is not a condition; even if believers perform a portion of righteous deeds, they are still among the people of salvation and felicity.

In any case, this verse, like many other verses of the Quran, considers faith to be the condition for the acceptance of

[^1]: Al-Imran: 55, Al-An'am: 164, An-Nahl: 92, Al-Hajj: 69, etc.

righteous deeds.

The mention of the phrase "فَلا كُفْرانَ لِسَعْيِهِ" (his effort shall not be denied) in the context of stating the reward for such individuals is an expression coupled with the utmost kindness, love, and nobility. For here, Allah acts in the position of thanking and appreciating His servants, and He thanks them for their striving and effort. This expression is similar to the one found in verse 19 of Surah Al-Isra: وَ مَنْ أَرادَ الْآخِرَةَ وَ سَعى‌ لَها سَعْيَها وَ هُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُولئِكَ كانَ سَعْيُهُمْ مَشْكُوراً—"And whoever desires the Hereafter and strives for it with the necessary effort while he is a believer—it is those whose effort is appreciated."

Ayahs

وَحَرَٰمٌ عَلَىٰ قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهَآ أَنَّهُمْ لَا يَرْجِعُونَ (٩٥)
It is forbidden for [the people of] any town that We have destroyed [to return to the world]: they shall not return, (95)
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا فُتِحَتْ يَأْجُوجُ وَمَأْجُوجُ وَهُم مِّن كُلِّ حَدَبٍ يَنسِلُونَ (٩٦)
until when Gog and Magog are let loose, and they race down from every slope, (96)
وَٱقْتَرَبَ ٱلْوَعْدُ ٱلْحَقُّ فَإِذَا هِىَ شَـٰخِصَةٌ أَبْصَـٰرُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يَـٰوَيْلَنَا قَدْ كُنَّا فِى غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ هَـٰذَا بَلْ كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ (٩٧)
and the true promise draws near [to its fulfillment], behold, the faithless will look on with a fixed gaze: ‘Woe to us! We have certainly been oblivious of this! Indeed, we have been wrongdoers!’ (97)

Commentary

Unbelievers on the Threshold of Resurrection

In the final verses of the previous discussion, the talk was about the "righteous believers and those who perform good deeds." In the first verse under discussion, it refers to individuals who stand in direct opposition to them—those who continue in deviation and corruption until their final breath.

It says: "And it is prohibited for [the people of] any city that We have destroyed to return [to this world]; they shall never return" (وَ حَرامٌ عَلى‌ قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْناها أَنَّهُمْ لا يَرْجِعُونَ‌) [^1]. In reality, these are people who, after witnessing divine punishment or after destruction and entering the world of Barzakh (the Isthmus), find the veils of pride and negligence removed from before their eyes. They wish that they could return to the world once more to compensate for all these errors, but the Quran explicitly states: their return is absolutely haram, meaning forbidden, and no path remains for compensation.

This is similar to what we read in verse 99 of Surah Al-Mu'minun: حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَهُمُ الْمَوْتُ قالَ رَبِّ ارْجِعُونِ لَعَلِّي أَعْمَلُ صالِحاً فِيما تَرَكْتُ كَلَّا ...:

"This state of theirs continues until death comes to one of them; he says, 'My Lord, send me back, so that I may do righteous deeds in that which I left behind.' But he hears nothing but a negative response."

Other explanations have also been mentioned in the commentary of this verse, some of which are noted at the bottom of the page [^2].

[^1]: According to this interpretation, "haram" (prohibited) is the predicate for a suppressed subject, and the phrase "أَنَّهُمْ لا يَرْجِعُونَ‌" (that they shall not return) serves as evidence for it. The implied structure is: "It is prohibited for the people of a city We destroyed to return to the world; indeed, they shall not return."

[^2]: Some have taken "haram" here to mean "obligatory" (wajib)! They say that in the Arabic language, it has occasionally appeared with this meaning, and the word "la" (not) is extra (redundant). Thus, the meaning of the verse would be that their return in the Hereafter is obligatory. Others have said "haram" retains its usual meaning of prohibited, but "la" is redundant, meaning their return to this world is prohibited. Others have interpreted the verse as a lack of return toward Allah and repentance (Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan and Fakhr Razi under the verse in question). Still others have said the verse is of the type of "negation of a negation," saying it is impossible that they do not return on the Day of Resurrection—meaning they will return (Tafsir Manhaj al-Sadiqin under the verse in question). However, what we have provided in the main text seems most appropriate of all.

In any case, these heedless ones are constantly in pride and negligence, and this misfortune continues until the end of the world.

As the Quran says:

"This continues until the way is opened for Gog and Magog, and they spread throughout the earth, and they descend rapidly from every elevation" (حَتَّى إِذا فُتِحَتْ يَأْجُوجُ وَ مَأْجُوجُ وَ هُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ حَدَبٍ يَنْسِلُونَ‌).

Regarding Gog and Magog (Yajuj and Majuj)—which tribe they were, where they lived, and what they will eventually do and become—we have discussed this under verses 94 and onwards of Surah Al-Kahf. We also discussed in detail the barrier that Dhul-Qarnayn built in a mountain pass to prevent their infiltration.

Does the "opening" of these two tribes refer to the breaking of their barrier and their infiltration into other regions of the world through that path? Or does it refer to their general infiltration into the globe from every side and every region? The above verse does not speak explicitly about this; it only mentions their spread and dispersion across the earth as a sign of the end of the world and a prelude to the Resurrection and the Day of Judgment.

And immediately it says: "At this time, the true promise of Allah draws near" (وَ اقْتَرَبَ الْوَعْدُ الْحَقُ‌). "And such a horror encompasses the entire being of the disbelievers that their eyes cease to move and they stare fixedly at that scene" (فَإِذا هِيَ شاخِصَةٌ أَبْصارُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا).

At this moment, the veils of pride and negligence are removed from before their eyes, and their cry rises: "Woe to us! We were in heedlessness of this scene" (يا وَيْلَنا قَدْ كُنَّا فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا).

And since they cannot cover their sin or exonerate themselves with this excuse, they say explicitly: "No, we were wrongdoers!" (بَلْ كُنَّا ظالِمِينَ‌).

Fundamentally, how is it possible to be in heedlessness despite all these divine prophets, heavenly books, and all these soul-shaking events, as well as the lessons of admonition that time gives them? What has proceeded from them is negligence and injustice against themselves and others.

Meaning of several words: "Hadab" (حَدَب) refers to elevations situated between lowlands, and sometimes the protrusion on a person's back (hunch) is also called hadab.

"Yansilun" (يَنْسِلُونَ) is from the root "nasul" (نَسُول), meaning to exit with speed. The fact that it says regarding Gog and Magog that they pass and exit rapidly from every elevation refers to their extraordinary infiltration across the globe.

"Shakhisah" (شاخِصَةٌ) is from the root "shukhus" (شُخُوص), which originally means leaving the house or leaving one city for another. Since, at the time of astonishment and staring, it is as if the eyes want to come out of the person's head, this state is also called shukhus. This is the state that will afflict the sinners in the Gathering; they will stare so fixedly that it is as if their eyes want to pop out of their sockets.

Ayahs

إِنَّكُمْ وَمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ أَنتُمْ لَهَا وَٰرِدُونَ (٩٨)
