Indeed you and what [idols] you worship besides Allah shall be fuel for hell, and you will enter it. (98)
لَوْ كَانَ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ءَالِهَةً مَّا وَرَدُوهَا ۖ وَكُلٌّ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٩٩)
Had they been gods, they would not have entered it, and they will all remain in it [forever]. (99)
لَهُمْ فِيهَا زَفِيرٌ وَهُمْ فِيهَا لَا يَسْمَعُونَ (١٠٠)
Their lot therein will be groaning, and they will not hear anything in it. (100)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ سَبَقَتْ لَهُم مِّنَّا ٱلْحُسْنَىٰٓ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَنْهَا مُبْعَدُونَ (١٠١)
Indeed those to whom there has gone beforehand [the promise of] the best reward from Us will be kept away from it. (101)
لَا يَسْمَعُونَ حَسِيسَهَا ۖ وَهُمْ فِى مَا ٱشْتَهَتْ أَنفُسُهُمْ خَـٰلِدُونَ (١٠٢)
They will not hear even its faint sound and they will remain [forever] in what their souls desire. (102)
لَا يَحْزُنُهُمُ ٱلْفَزَعُ ٱلْأَكْبَرُ وَتَتَلَقَّىٰهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ هَـٰذَا يَوْمُكُمُ ٱلَّذِى كُنتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ (١٠٣)
The Great Terror will not upset them, and the angels will receive them [saying]: ‘This is your day which you were promised.’ (103)

Commentary

Fuel of Hell!

Following the previous verses which discussed the fate of the oppressive polytheists, these verses address them directly, depicting the future of both them and their deities as follows:

"Indeed, you and what you worship other than Allah are the fuel of Hell!" (إِنَّكُمْ وَ ما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ حَصَبُ جهنم).

The word hasab (حصب) originally means "to throw" or "to cast," and it specifically refers to pieces of firewood that are thrown into an oven.

Some have stated that hatab (حطب), which means firewood, has different pronunciations in various Arabic dialects; some tribes call it hasab and others khadab. Since the Quran aimed to unite tribes, clans, and hearts, it occasionally employs their diverse dialects to foster this unity of hearts. Among these is the word hasab, which is a pronunciation used by the tribes of Yemen for the word hatab.[^1] In any case, the above verse tells the polytheists: the fuel of Hell and the wood that forms its flames are you yourselves and your fabricated gods, and like worthless pieces of firewood, you will be cast into Hell one after another! Then it adds: "You will [surely] come to it" (أَنْتُمْ لَها وارِدُونَ).

This phrase is either an emphasis on the preceding point, or it points to a new nuance: that first the idols are cast into the fire, and then you enter upon them—as if your gods are "welcoming" you with the fire emanating from their very existence.[^1] If it is asked what the philosophy behind throwing idols into Hell is, the answer is: this is itself a form of torment and punishment for the idol-worshippers, to see themselves burning in a fire that blazes from their own idols. Furthermore, it is a humiliation of their intellects, which sought refuge in such worthless entities.

Of course, this applies if ma ta'budun (what you worship) refers to inanimate deities and idols of stone and wood (as suggested by the use of the word ma, which is generally used for non-rational beings). However, if we take the meaning to be general, including the devils who were worshipped, the reason for these deities entering Hell is perfectly clear, as they are accomplices in the crime.

Then, as a general conclusion, He says: "Had these been [true] gods, they would not have entered it" (لَوْ كانَ هؤُلاءِ آلِهَةً ما وَرَدُوها). But know that not only will they enter Hell, but "all will abide therein eternally" (وَ كُلٌّ فِيها خالِدُونَ).

[^1]: Tafsir "Abu al-Futuh al-Razi," under the verses in question.

It is striking that these idol-worshippers will be eternally trapped with their own gods—the very gods they always worshipped, considered shields against calamities, and turned to for the resolution of their problems!

To further explain the painful state of these "misguided worshippers" in the presence of those "worthless deities," He says: "For them therein is groaning" (لَهُمْ فِيها زَفِيرٌ).

Zafir (زفير) originally refers to a loud cry accompanied by the exhaling of breath. Some have said the repulsive braying of a donkey is called zafir at its beginning and shahiq at its end. In any case, here it refers to the screams and moans arising from intense grief and sorrow.[^1] There is also the possibility that this zafir and mournful moaning is not limited to the worshippers, but that the devils who were their deities share in this as well.

The next sentence describes another of their painful punishments: "and they will not hear therein" (وَ هُمْ فِيها لا يَسْمَعُونَ).

This phrase may indicate that they will absolutely not hear any word that could bring them joy or happiness; they will only hear the soul-crushing moans of the dwellers of Hell and the shouts of the angels of punishment.

Some have said it means they are placed in coffins of fire such that they hear no sound from anyone at all, as if they alone are in torment. This itself is a cause for greater punishment, for if a person sees a group sharing their imprisonment, it provides a sense of consolation—as the saying goes, "A calamity that is general becomes bearable."

[^1]: For further explanation, refer to Volume 9 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 248 (under verse 106 of Surah Hud).

The next verse recounts the states of the true believers, both men and women, so that by comparison, the condition of both groups becomes clearer. First, He says: "Indeed, those for whom the best [reward] has preceded from Us—they are far removed from it" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ سَبَقَتْ لَهُمْ مِنَّا الْحُسْنى‌ أُولئِكَ عَنْها مُبْعَدُونَ).

This indicates that We will fulfill all the promises We made to the believers in this world, one of which is being distanced from the fire of Hell.

Although the apparent meaning of this sentence includes all true believers, some have suggested it refers to deities such as Prophet Jesus عليه السلام and Mary, who were worshipped by a group against their own will. Since the previous verses stated, "You and your gods will enter Hell," and this expression could potentially include the likes of Prophet Jesus عليه السلام, the Quran immediately presents this sentence as an exception, stating that this group will never enter Hell.

Some commentators have mentioned a "circumstance of revelation" (shan al-nuzul) for this verse, showing that some people asked the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله this very question, and the verse was revealed in response. Nevertheless, there is no contradiction in the verse being both an answer to that question and a general ruling for all true believers.

In the final verses under discussion, He enumerates four great Divine blessings bestowed upon this fortunate group:

First: "They will not hear its [Hell's] slightest sound" (لا يَسْمَعُونَ حَسِيسَها).

Hasīs (حسيس), as linguists have stated, refers to a perceptible sound, and it has also been defined as the movement itself or the sound arising from movement. The fire of Hell, which is constantly advancing through its fuel, has a specific sound. This sound is terrifying for two reasons: because it is the sound of fire, and because it is the sound of its advancement. True believers, being far from Hell, will never hear these terrifying sounds.

Second: "and they will abide eternally in whatever their souls desire" (وَ هُمْ فِي مَا اشْتَهَتْ أَنْفُسُهُمْ خالِدُونَ).

This means that there, the limitations of this world do not exist, where a person might long for many blessings but cannot access them. Every material and spiritual blessing they desire is available to them without exception—and not just for a day or two, but for an eternal life!

Third: "The Greatest Terror will not grieve them" (لا يَحْزُنُهُمُ الْفَزَعُ الْأَكْبَرُ).

Some have interpreted "The Greatest Terror" (al-faza' al-akbar) as the terrors of the Day of Resurrection, which are greater than any other terror. Others have interpreted it as the "Blowing of the Trumpet" (nafkh al-sur) and the cataclysms at the end of this world, and the extraordinary shaking of the foundations of this universe, as mentioned in verse 87 of Surah al-Nahl. However, since the terror of the Day of Resurrection is certainly even more significant, the first interpretation seems more accurate.

Finally, the last grace of Allah regarding those mentioned in these verses is that "the angels will meet them [saying], 'This is your Day which you were promised'" (وَ تَتَلَقَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ هذا يَوْمُكُمُ الَّذِي كُنْتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ).

In Nahj al-Balagha, we read that the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, said:

"Hasten with your deeds so that you may be with the neighbors of Allah in His abode, where He has made His messengers their companions, and sends His angels to visit them. Allah has so honored this group that even their ears shall never hear the sound of the fire of Hell."[^1]

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 183.

Ayah

يَوْمَ نَطْوِى ٱلسَّمَآءَ كَطَىِّ ٱلسِّجِلِّ لِلْكُتُبِ ۚ كَمَا بَدَأْنَآ أَوَّلَ خَلْقٍ نُّعِيدُهُۥ ۚ وَعْدًا عَلَيْنَآ ۚ إِنَّا كُنَّا فَـٰعِلِينَ (١٠٤)
The day We shall roll up the heaven, like rolling of the scrolls [meant] for writings. We will bring it back just as We began the first creation—a promise [binding] on Us. [That] indeed We will do. (104)

Commentary

The Day the Heavens are Rolled Up

In the final verse of the previous discussion, we read that the true believers will not be grieved by the "Great Terror" (al-faza‘ al-akbar). The verse under discussion provides a description of that day of great terror and, in fact, visualizes the cause of the magnitude of this horror, stating:

"This matter shall be realized on the day when We roll up the heaven like the rolling up of the scroll for writings!" (يَوْمَ نَطْوِي السَّماءَ كَطَيِّ السِّجِلِّ لِلْكُتُبِ). [^1] In ancient times, scroll-like sheets were used for writing letters and books. These scrolls were rolled up before writing, and the scribe would gradually open it from one side and write the intended content upon it.

[^1]: "Sijill" (rhyming with satl) means a large bucket full of water, and "Sijjill" (with kasra on the sin and jim and tashdid on the lam) refers to pieces of stone upon which things were written; subsequently, it was applied to all papers upon which matters are inscribed (Mufradat al-Raghib and al-Qamus). It should be noted that regarding the structure of the phrase "كَطَيِّ السِّجِلِّ لِلْكُتُبِ", several possibilities have been suggested, but the most appropriate is that "tayy," which is an infinitive, is annexed to "sijill," which is its object, and the lam in "lil-kutub" is either for annexation or to explain the cause (reflect on this).

After finishing the writing, they would roll it up and set it aside; therefore, both their letters and books were in the form of scrolls. This scroll, which was used for writing, was called a "Sijill."

In this verse, a subtle comparison is made regarding the folding of the scroll of the universe at the end of the world. Currently, this scroll is open, and all its inscriptions and lines are being read, each situated in its place. However, when the command for the Resurrection arrives, this massive scroll, with all its lines and inscriptions, will be rolled up.

Of course, the rolling up of the world does not mean its annihilation and non-existence, as some have imagined; rather, it means its being crushed and gathered together. In other words, the form of the world will be disrupted, but its matter will not be destroyed. This is a reality clearly observed from various expressions in the verses regarding the Hereafter (especially the return of man from decayed bones and from the graves).

Then He adds: "As We began the first creation, We shall repeat it," and this is not a difficult or arduous task for Our great power (كَما بَدَأْنا أَوَّلَ خَلْقٍ نُعِيدُهُ).

In fact, this expression is similar to the one found in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 29: "كَما بَدَأَكُمْ تَعُودُونَ" ("As He began you, so shall you return").

Or like: "وَ هُوَ الَّذِي يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ وَ هُوَ أَهْوَنُ عَلَيْهِ" ("And He it is who originates the creation, then He repeats it, and that is easier for Him") (Ar-Rum: 27). [^1]

[^1]: Of course, as we have pointed out in the past, regarding the infinite power of Allah, "difficult" and "easy" do not exist; rather, everything is entirely equal. Therefore, the expression used in the above verse is actually from the perspective of human beings (reflect on this).

However, the possibility suggested by some commentators that this "return" refers to a return to annihilation and non-existence, or to a state of being rolled up like the beginning of creation, seems very unlikely.

At the end of the verse, He says: "This is a promise We have undertaken; surely We are going to do it" (وَعْداً عَلَيْنا إِنَّا كُنَّا فاعِلِينَ). [^1] [^2] It is understood from some narrations that the meaning of the return of creation to its original state is that humans will once again return barefoot and naked—just as they were at the beginning of creation—but undoubtedly, the meaning of the verse is not restricted to this sense; rather, this is one of the aspects of the return of creation to its primary state. [^3]

[^1]: "Wa‘dan" is the object of the verb "wa‘adna," which is implied.

[^2]: This sentence actually contains several types of emphasis: the word "wa‘d" (promise), then the expression "‘alayna" (upon Us), then the emphasis with "inna" (surely We), and further the use of the past tense in "kunna" (We were/have been), as well as the word "fa‘ilin" (doers/performers).

[^3]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ كَتَبْنَا فِى ٱلزَّبُورِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ٱلذِّكْرِ أَنَّ ٱلْأَرْضَ يَرِثُهَا عِبَادِىَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحُونَ (١٠٥)
Certainly We wrote in the Psalms, after the Torah: ‘Indeed My righteous servants shall inherit the earth.’ (105)
إِنَّ فِى هَـٰذَا لَبَلَـٰغًا لِّقَوْمٍ عَـٰبِدِينَ (١٠٦)
There is indeed in this a proclamation for a devout people. (106)

Commentary

The Governance of the Earth Shall Belong to the Righteous

After the preceding verses pointed to a portion of the afterlife rewards for the righteous believers, the two verses under discussion refer to one of their clearest worldly rewards—"governance of the earth"—with an eloquent expression, stating: "And We have already written in the 'Zabur' after the 'Dhikr' that the earth shall be inherited by My righteous servants" (وَلَقَدْ كَتَبْنَا فِي الزَّبُورِ مِن بَعْدِ الذِّكْرِ أَنَّ الْأَرْضَ يَرِثُهَا عِبَادِيَ الصَّالِحُونَ).

"Ardh" (Earth) refers to the entire globe and encompasses the whole world unless a specific context suggests otherwise. Although some have considered the possibility that it refers to inheriting the entire earth in the Hereafter, the apparent meaning of the word "Ardh" when used absolutely is the earth of this world.

The term "Irth" (inheritance), as we have previously noted, refers to something that is transferred to someone without a transaction or trade. Sometimes in the Holy Quran, "Irth" is used to describe the dominance and victory of a righteous people over an unrighteous people and their taking control of their blessings and resources. As we read in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 137, regarding the Children of Israel and their victory over the Pharaohs: وَأَوْرَثْنَا الْقَوْمَ الَّذِينَ كَانُوا يُسْتَضْعَفُونَ مَشَارِقَ الْأَرْضِ وَمَغَارِبَهَا ("And We caused the people who had been oppressed to inherit the eastern regions of the land and the western ones").

Although "Zabur" originally means any kind of book or writing, and in the Holy Quran, in two out of the three instances where this word is used, it refers to the Psalms of David, it does not seem unlikely that the third instance—the verse under discussion—carries the same meaning.

The "Zabur of David," or as expressed in the books of the "Old Testament," the "Psalms of David," consists of a collection of supplications, prayers, and exhortations of the Prophet David (peace be upon him).

Some commentators have also suggested that "Zabur" here refers to all the books of the previous prophets [1], but it seems more likely—given the reason we mentioned—that the Zabur is indeed the Book of the Psalms of David, especially since in the existing Psalms, there are phrases that exactly correspond to the verse under discussion, which we will point to later, God willing.

"Dhikr" originally means a reminder or something that serves as a source of remembrance, and it is used in this sense in the verses of the Quran. Sometimes it is also applied to the Heavenly Book of Moses, namely the Torah (such as in Surah Al-Anbiya, verse 48: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ وَهَارُونَ الْفُرْقَانَ وَضِيَاءً وَذِكْرًا لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ).

And sometimes this title is used for the Quran, such as in Surah At-Takwir, verse 27: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَالَمِينَ.

Therefore, some have said that "Dhikr" in the verse under discussion refers to the Quran, and "Zabur" refers to all the books of the previous prophets, and the phrase "min ba'd" (after) would be roughly equivalent to "in addition to." In this way, the meaning of the verse would be: "In addition to the Quran, We wrote in all the books of the previous prophets that eventually the entire surface of the earth will be placed at the disposal of the righteous servants of Allah."

However, considering the expressions used in the verse, it appears that "Zabur" refers to the Book of David and "Dhikr" refers to the Torah. Given that the Zabur came after the Torah, the expression "min ba'd" (after) is also literal. Thus, the meaning of the verse becomes: "We wrote in the Zabur, after the Torah, that this earth shall be inherited by Our righteous servants."

Here, the question arises: why, among the Heavenly Books, are only these two mentioned by name?

This expression might be because David was one of the greatest prophets who established a government of truth and justice, and the Children of Israel were a clear example of an oppressed people who rose against the arrogant, dismantled their system, and inherited their governance and land.

Another remaining question is: who are the "righteous servants of Allah" (عِبَادِيَ الصَّالِحُونَ)?

By attributing the servants to Allah, the matter of their faith and monotheism becomes clear. And considering the word "Salihun" (righteous), which has a broad and vast meaning, all forms of merit come to mind: merit in terms of action and piety, merit in terms of knowledge and awareness, merit in terms of power and strength, and merit in terms of management, order, and social understanding.

When faithful servants provide these merits for themselves, Allah also assists and helps them to rub the noses of the arrogant in the dust, to cut off their tainted hands from the governance of the earth, and to become the inheritors of their legacies.

Therefore, merely "being oppressed" is not the reason for victory over enemies and governance of the earth; rather, faith is necessary on one hand, and the acquisition of merits on the other. The oppressed of the world will not reach the governance of the earth as long as they do not revive these two principles.

Hence, in the following verse, as a further emphasis, He says: "Indeed, in this is a clear message for a people who worship [Allah with sincerity]" (إِنَّ فِي هَٰذَا لَبَلَاغًا لِّقَوْمٍ عَابِدِينَ). Some commentators have considered "this" (hadha) to refer to all the promises and warnings mentioned in this Surah or in the entire Quran, which includes our topic of discussion within this general concept.

However, the apparent meaning of the verse is that "this" refers to the very promise given in the previous verse to His righteous servants regarding the governance of the earth.

Key Points

1- Narrations Concerning the Uprising of Al-Mahdi (عليه السلام)

In some narrations, this verse is explicitly interpreted as referring to the companions of Al-Mahdi (عليه السلام).

As we read in Majma' al-Bayan under this verse from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام): "They are the companions of Al-Mahdi in the end of times."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, it is also stated under this verse: "The meaning of the earth being inherited by the righteous servants of Allah is the Qaim (Al-Mahdi) (عليه السلام) and his companions."

It goes without saying that the concept of these narrations is not one of exclusivity, but rather the presentation of a supreme and manifest instance. We have repeatedly stated that these interpretations never limit the generality of the verse's concept.

Therefore, whenever and wherever the righteous servants of Allah rise, they will be victorious and successful, and will eventually become the inheritors of the earth and its governance.

In addition to the above narrations specifically regarding the interpretation of this verse, there are a vast number of narrations reaching the level of tawatur (successive transmission) from both Shi'a and Sunni sources regarding Al-Mahdi (عليه السلام) from the Noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and also from the "Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt" (عليهم السلام), all of which indicate that eventually the governance of the world will fall into the hands of the righteous, and a man from the family of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) will rise and fill the earth with justice and equity just as it had been filled with oppression and tyranny.

Among these is the famous Hadith narrated in most Islamic sources from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "If there remained of the world but one day, Allah would prolong that day until He sends a (righteous) man from my household who will fill the earth with justice and equity as it was filled with oppression and tyranny."

This Hadith has appeared with this wording or slight variations in many Shi'a and Sunni books [1]. Under verse 33 of Surah At-Tawbah, we mentioned that a group of great Islamic scholars, past and present, Sunni and Shi'a, have explicitly stated in their books that the Hadiths regarding the uprising of Al-Mahdi (عليه السلام) are at the level of tawatur and cannot be denied in any way. They have even written specific books on this matter, the details of which you can read in Volume 7 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 33 of At-Tawbah (page 374 onwards).

2- The Glad Tidings of the Governance of the Righteous in the Psalms of David

Interestingly, in the "Psalms of David," which is today part of the books of the Old Testament, the exact expression we read in the above verses or something similar is seen in several places, showing that despite all the distortions made to these books, this part has remained immune from tampering.

  1. In Psalm 37, verse 9, we read: "...For evildoers shall be cut off; but those who wait on the Lord, they shall inherit the earth. For yet a little while and the wicked shall be no more; indeed, you will look carefully for his place, but it shall be no more."

  2. And elsewhere in the same Psalm (verse 11), we read: "But the meek shall inherit the earth, and shall delight themselves in the abundance of peace."

  3. Also in the same Psalm 37, verse 22, this subject is seen in another expression: "For those blessed by Him shall inherit the earth, but those cursed by Him shall be cut off."

  4. In the same Psalm, verse 29, it says: "The righteous shall inherit the land, and dwell therein forever."

  5. And in verse 18 of the same Psalm, it says: "The Lord knows the days of the upright, and their inheritance shall be forever" [1].

Here we clearly observe that the same title "righteous" (Salihun) mentioned in the Quran appears with the same expression in the Psalms of David. Additionally, other expressions such as "the upright," "those who wait on the Lord," "the blessed," and "the meek," which are close in meaning, are also mentioned in other sentences.

These expressions are evidence of the universality of the governance of the righteous and perfectly align with the Hadiths of the uprising of Al-Mahdi (عليه السلام).

3- The Governance of the Righteous is a Law of Creation

Although it is difficult for those who have mostly witnessed the rule of cruel tyrants and powerful rebels to easily accept this truth—that all these governments are contrary to the norms of creation and the laws of the universe, and that what is harmonious with it is the governance of the faithful righteous—philosophical analyses lead to the conclusion that this is a reality. Therefore, the sentence "the earth shall be inherited by My righteous servants" is considered a cosmic law before it is a divine promise.

To explain: the universe, as far as we know, is a collection of systems. The existence of organized and general laws throughout this world is evidence of the unity and interconnectedness of this system.

The issue of order, law, and calculation in the expanse of creation is considered one of the most fundamental issues of this world. For example, if we see hundreds of powerful electronic brains working together to pave the way for space travel through precise calculations, and their calculations turn out correct and the lunar module lands exactly at the predicted spot on the moon—despite both the moon and the earth moving rapidly—we must realize that this process is indebted to the precise system of the solar system and its planets and moons. For if they deviated even one-hundredth of a second from their regular course, it would be unknown where the space travelers would be flung.

From the macro-world, we enter the micro-world and the sub-micro-world. Here, especially in the world of living beings, order takes on a more vivid meaning, and chaos has no place in it. For instance, the disruption of the regulation of a single human brain cell is enough to tragically dismantle the organization of his life.

It was reported in the newspapers that a young student, due to a severe brain jar in a car accident, forgot almost his entire past! While healthy in other respects, he does not recognize his brother and sister and is terrified when his mother embraces and kisses him, wondering what this strange woman wants with him. They take him to his birthplace, to the room where he grew up; he looks at his handicrafts and paintings, but says: "This is the first time I have seen such a room and painting!" Perhaps he thinks he has stepped onto this planet from another, where everything is new to him.

Perhaps among the several billion cells of his brain, only a few connective cells that linked the past with the present have stopped working. But what a terrifying effect this minor disruption of regulation brought about! Can human society, by choosing "disorder," chaos, oppression, injustice, instability, and abnormality, pull itself away from the path of the great river of creation in which everyone moves with an organized program? Does observing the general state of the world not make us think that humanity, too, must inevitably bow before the system of the universe, accept organized and just laws, return to the original path, and become harmonious with this system?

We look at the structure of the various and complex systems of every human body! From the heart and brain to the eye, ear, tongue, and even a hair bulb, we see they are all subject to laws, order, and calculation. In such a state, how can human society survive without following criteria, regulations, and a correct, just system?

We desire the survival of humanity and strive for it, but the level of our social awareness has not yet reached the point where we know that continuing the current path leads to our annihilation and destruction. However, we are gradually coming to our senses, and this understanding and intellectual growth are being achieved for us.

We desire our own interests, but we do not yet know that continuing the current situation blows our interests to the wind. Gradually, however, we place vivid and expressive figures and statistics before our eyes—for example, regarding the arms race—and we see how half of the most active intellectual and physical forces of world societies and half of the great wealth and capital are wasted in this way. Not only wasted, but used in the path of destroying the second half!

Simultaneously with the increase in our level of awareness, we clearly find that we must return to the general system of the universe and harmonize with it. And just as we are truly a part of this whole, we must also be so in practice so that we can reach our goals in all fields.

The conclusion is that the system of creation is clear evidence for the acceptance of a correct social system in the future of the human world, and this is the very thing derived from the verse under discussion and the Hadiths related to the uprising of the "Great Global Reformer" (Al-Mahdi, may our souls be sacrificed for him) [1].

We thank Allah for these coincidences!

Ayahs

وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٠٧)
We did not send you but as a mercy to all the nations. (107)
قُلْ إِنَّمَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ أَنَّمَآ إِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۖ فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ (١٠٨)
Say, ‘It has been revealed to me that your God is the One God. So will you submit?’ (108)
فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَقُلْ ءَاذَنتُكُمْ عَلَىٰ سَوَآءٍ ۖ وَإِنْ أَدْرِىٓ أَقَرِيبٌ أَم بَعِيدٌ مَّا تُوعَدُونَ (١٠٩)
But if they turn away, say, ‘I have proclaimed to you all alike, and I do not know whether what you have been promised is far or near. (109)
إِنَّهُۥ يَعْلَمُ ٱلْجَهْرَ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تَكْتُمُونَ (١١٠)
Indeed He knows whatever is spoken aloud, and He knows whatever you conceal. (110)
وَإِنْ أَدْرِى لَعَلَّهُۥ فِتْنَةٌ لَّكُمْ وَمَتَـٰعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ (١١١)
I do not know—maybe it is a trial for you and an enjoyment for a while.’ (111)
قَـٰلَ رَبِّ ٱحْكُم بِٱلْحَقِّ ۗ وَرَبُّنَا ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ ٱلْمُسْتَعَانُ عَلَىٰ مَا تَصِفُونَ (١١٢)
He said, ‘My Lord! Judge [between us and the polytheists] with justice.’ ‘Our Lord is the All-beneficent; [He is our] resort against what you allege.’ (112)

Commentary

The Prophet of Mercy for the Worlds

Since the preceding verses gave the glad tidings of the rule over the earth to the righteous servants, and such a government is a source of mercy for all the people of the world, the first verse under discussion refers to the universal mercy of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله existence, saying: "And We have not sent you except as a mercy to the worlds" (وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَاكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِلْعَالَمِينَ).

All the people of the world, whether believers or disbelievers, are indebted to your mercy, for you have undertaken the propagation of a religion that is the means of salvation for everyone. Now, if a group utilizes it and another does not, this pertains to themselves and does not affect the universality of the mercy.

This is exactly like establishing a well-equipped hospital for the treatment of all ailments, with skilled physicians and various medicines, and opening its doors to all people without distinction. Is this not a means of mercy for all members of that society? Now, if some stubborn patients refuse to accept this general grace, it will have no effect on its universal nature.

In other words, the existence of the Prophet being a mercy for all the worlds has the aspect of "potentiality" and the "action of the actor," and certainly, the "actuality" of the result also depends on the "receptivity of the receiver."

The expression "worlds" (عالمين) has such a broad meaning that it includes all human beings in all eras and centuries; therefore, this verse is considered an indication of the finality of the Prophet of Islam, for his existence is a mercy, a leader, a guide, and an exemplar for all future humans until the end of the world. In one respect, this mercy even encompasses the angels.

An interesting Hadith is narrated here that confirms this universality. The Hadith states that when this verse was revealed, the Prophet asked Jibril عليه السلام: "هل اصابك من هذه الرحمة شى‌ء" "Did any of this mercy reach you?"

Jibril replied: "نعم، انى كنت اخشى عاقبة الامر، فامنت بك، لما اثنى اللَّه على بقوله‌ عِنْدَ ذِي الْعَرْشِ مَكِينٍ" "Yes, I was fearful of the end of my affair, but I became secure because of the verse revealed to you in the Quran, where Allah praised me with this sentence: 'Possessor of power, with the Owner of the Throne, secure [in position]' (ذِي قُوَّةٍ عِنْدَ ذِي الْعَرْشِ مَكِينٍ)." [^1]

In any case, in today's world, where corruption, ruin, injustice, and tyranny pour from every wall, the fires of war are ablaze on every side, and the claws of the tyrannical powerful are squeezing the throats of the oppressed downtrodden—in a world where ignorance, moral corruption, betrayal, oppression, despotism, and discrimination have created a thousand types of disorder—yes, in such a world, the concept of the Prophet being a "mercy to the worlds" is more evident than ever. What mercy could be greater than bringing a program whose implementation is the end point for all these failures, miseries, and misfortunes? Yes, he and his instructions, his program, and his ethics are all mercy—a mercy for everyone—and the continuation of this mercy will ultimately result in the rule of the righteous believers over the entire inhabited earth.

And since the most important manifestation of mercy and its firmest foundation is the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and its manifestations, the next verse says: "Say: It is only revealed to me that your God is one God" (قُلْ إِنَّمَا يُوحَىٰ إِلَيَّ أَنَّمَا إِلَٰهُكُمْ إِلَٰهٌ وَاحِدٌ).

"Are you then willing to submit to this fundamental principle of Monotheism and set aside the idols?" (فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ).

In fact, three fundamental points are raised in this verse: First, the primary foundation of mercy is Monotheism, and truly, the more we reflect, the clearer the existence of this powerful relationship becomes: Monotheism in belief, in action, unity of word, unity of ranks, and unity in law and in everything.

Another point is that according to the word "innama" (إِنَّمَا), which indicates restriction, the entire call of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله is summarized in the principle of Monotheism. Careful studies also show that the principles and even the branches and rulings ultimately return to the principle of Monotheism. For this reason, as we have said before, Monotheism is not just one principle among the principles of religion, but rather like a strong thread that connects the beads of a rosary, or more accurately, it is the soul breathed into the body of religion.

The final point is that the main problem of all societies and nations is being tainted by polytheism (shirk) in various forms, because the phrase "Are you then Muslims?" (فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ) (Are you submissive to this principle?) implies that the main challenge is emerging from polytheism and its manifestations and rolling up one's sleeves to break the idols—not just idols of stone and wood, but idols of every kind, especially human tyrants (Taghuts)!

The next verse says that if, despite all this, they do not pay attention to Our call and message: "But if they turn away, then say: I have announced [the message] to you all alike" (فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَقُلْ آذَنْتُكُمْ عَلَىٰ سَوَاءٍ).

"Adhantukum" (آذَنْتُكُمْ) is from the root "idhan," meaning to announce accompanied by a threat, and sometimes it has come to mean a declaration of war. However, since this Surah was revealed in Mecca, where there was no ground for Jihad nor had the ruling of Jihad been revealed, it seems very unlikely that this sentence here means a "declaration of war." Rather, it is apparent that the Prophet, with these words, wants to declare his aversion and separation from them and show that he has completely washed his hands of them.

The expression "alike" (عَلَىٰ سَوَاءٍ) either refers to the fact that "I warn you all equally regarding the divine punishment," lest they imagine that the people of Mecca or the Quraysh are different from others and possess some merit or superiority before God; or it refers to the fact that "I have conveyed my call to the ears of all of you without exception."

Then he expresses this threat more clearly and says: "And I do not know whether that which you are promised is near or far" (وَإِنْ أَدْرِي أَقَرِيبٌ أَمْ بَعِيدٌ مَا تُوعَدُونَ).

Do not think this promise is far off; perhaps it is near, even very near.

This punishment and penalty with which they are threatened here might be the punishment of the Resurrection, or the punishment of this world, or both. In the first case, its knowledge is exclusive to Allah, and no one is accurately aware of the date of the occurrence of the Resurrection, not even the Prophets of God. In the second and third cases, it may refer to the details and its timing, of which "I am not aware of the specifics," because the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله knowledge regarding such events is not always "actual" but sometimes "intentional"—meaning he does not know until he wills to know. [^1]

Do not let the thought enter your minds that if there is a delay in your punishment, it is because God is unaware of your deeds and words; no, He knows everything: "Indeed, He knows what is declared of speech, and He knows what you conceal" (إِنَّهُ يَعْلَمُ الْجَهْرَ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تَكْتُمُونَ).

Fundamentally, "hidden" and "manifest" have meaning for you whose knowledge is limited, but for Him whose knowledge is infinite, the unseen and the witnessed are one, and the secret and the public are the same.

And also, if you see that divine punishment does not seize you immediately, it is not because He is unaware of your work: "And I do not know; perhaps it is a trial for you" (وَإِنْ أَدْرِي لَعَلَّهُ فِتْنَةٌ لَكُمْ). "And [He intends] to let you enjoy yourselves for a time" (وَمَتَاعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ). Then He will seize and punish you with the utmost severity.

In fact, two philosophies are stated here for the delay of divine punishment: First is the issue of trial and testing; God never hastens in punishment so that He may sufficiently test the creation and complete the proof (itmam al-hujjah).

Second is that there are individuals whose trial is finished and the decree of their punishment has become final, but in order for them to be severely penalized, He expands blessings upon them until they are completely drowned in bounty; and exactly at the moment when they are deeply immersed in pleasures, He strikes them with the whip of punishment so that it may be more painful, and they may well feel the suffering of the deprived and the oppressed.

The last verse under discussion, which is the final verse of Surah al-Anbiya, like the first verse of this Surah, speaks of the negligence of the heedless people. As a quotation from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, in a phrase resembling a prayer of grievance, it reflects his distress over all this pride and negligence and says: After observing all this turning away and avoidance, the Prophet "said: My Lord, judge [between us] in truth! And our Lord is the Most Merciful, whose help is sought against that which you describe" (قَالَ رَبِّ احْكُمْ بِالْحَقِّ ۗ وَرَبُّنَا الرَّحْمَٰنُ الْمُسْتَعَانُ عَلَىٰ مَا تَصِفُونَ). [^1]

In the second sentence, addressing the opponents, it says: "Our Lord is the Most Merciful, and we seek assistance from His holy presence against the false attributions you make to Him."

In truth, with the word "Our Lord" (ربنا), he draws their attention to the reality that we are all nurtured and created beings, and He is the Creator and Lord of us all.

The expression "The Most Merciful" (الرحمن), which refers to the general mercy of the Lord, reminds them that the divine mercy has encompassed your entire existence; why do you not reflect for a moment on the Creator of all these blessings and mercy?

And the expression "whose help is sought against that which you describe" (الْمُسْتَعَانُ عَلَىٰ مَا تَصِفُونَ) warns them: Do not imagine that we are alone against your vast numbers, and do not think that all these slanders, lies, and false attributions—whether regarding the pure Essence of God or against us—will remain without an answer. No, never; for He is the support of us all, and He is capable of defending His believing servants against every kind of lie and slander.

"End of Surah al-Anbiya"

O Lord! Just as You did not leave Your noble Prophet and his few companions alone against the multitude of enemies, do not leave us alone against the enemies in the East and the West who have resolved to destroy us!

O God! In this blessed Surah, You especially recounted Your special mercy toward the Prophets in hard and critical times and against the storms of life. O Lord! We too in this age and time are caught in those same storms and expect that same special mercy and relief. Amin, O Lord of the Worlds.

End of Volume 13 of Tafsir Nemouneh at Aliabad Farm, Qom. August 20, 1982 (Friday, 30 Shawwal 1402 AH).
